Crump weir modular to 15.6 cumecs (but bypassed at 14.2). Flat V also bypassed. Two small storage reservoirs with minor influence on low flows; receives Kettering effluent.
catchmentDescription: Underlain by clay (60%) and sandstone (25%) with 40% superficial deposits, mainly in higher areas on catchment margins. Mainly rural catchment of mixed farming, but includes Kettering (10% of catchment area) in lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire

name: Nene/Kislingbury at Upton Total
stationDescription: Main channel flow measured in 3.2m wide standing wave flume under mill. Flow in bypass channel measured at Crump profile weir (crest 6.12m) since 1969 and flows summed to produce total. Before 1969 flows through bypass controlled by broad-crested weir with no recorder, and total flows based on average relationship between levels in main channel and bypass. Very high flows bypass both channels. No major abstractions but sewage effluent can effect flow patterns.
catchmentDescription: Mostly clay (70%) with 30% superficial deposits, mainly Boulder Clay. predominantly rural catchment with land use of mixed farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Nene/Brampton at St Andrews Total
stationDescription: Main channel flow measured in 2.743m wide standing-wave flume in mill race. Flow in bypass channel measured at 9.11m wide broad-crested weir and flows summed to produce total. No recorder on bypass before 1969 - total flows were estimated using average relationship between levels in flume and bypass. Bypassing of both structures commences at about 17 cumecs. Abstraction from three water supply reservoirs reduce low flows - 30% of catchment controlled by reservoirs.
catchmentDescription: Mostly clay (76%), with 30% cover of Boulder Clay. Predominantly rural catchment of mixed farming. Urban development (Northampton) in lower catchment, u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire

name: Nene/Kislingbury at Dodford
stationDescription: Crump profile weir with 2.667m crest replaced broad-crested weir with low flow notch in 1967. Weir theoretically calibrated, but bypassing begins at 7 cumecs / 1.038m stage. Floodplain flow is considerable once the river is out of bank, so flows are significantly underestimed from c.1.1m stage. Weir has not been observed to drown at high flows. Reservoirs and abstractions affect regime, and low flows influenced by returns from STW.
catchmentDescription: Mostly clay (73%), nearly 50% superficial deposits (Boulder Clay, glacial sand and gravel in lower valley). Predominantly rural with mixed farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Nene at Wansford
stationDescription: Cross, multiple path Ultrasonic gauge measuring the full-range. Previously was a velocity-area station, for flows over 28.3 cumecs, with low and medium flows measured at 32001 Nene at Orton.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire

name: Wootton Brook at Lady Bridge
stationDescription: Simple Crump profile weir, crest 1.83m broad. Rated up to 0.25m (0.488 cumecs). Low flows only until mid 1990s. Largely natural regime; one small discharge in headwaters. Sporadic flow record.
catchmentDescription: Underlain by 80% Boulder Clay. Small rural catchment, with 20% woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Slade Brook at Kettering
stationDescription: Full-range Flat V weir. Replaced low flow simple Crump profile weir (crest 2.42m broad, rated up to 0.3m: 0.848 cumecs) in early 1988, following river improvements. Most high flows are truncated or missing in pre-1995 record; water balance more realistic over subsequent years.
catchmentDescription: Catchment is 50% clay and 50% s'st, with over 20% cover of Boulder Clay. Rural headwaters, with urban development (Kettering) in lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire

name: Wittering Brook at Wansford
stationDescription: Simple Crump profile weir, crest 2.44m broad. Rated up to 0.308m (0.896 cumecs). Low flows only. Truncation of high flows results in unrepresentative mean runoff.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire

name: Flore at Experimental Catchment
stationDescription: Trapezoidal critical depth flume rated to 1.92m (8.49 cumecs). Low flows only. Responsive regime but patchy record: closed in 1985.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Wootton Brook at Wootton Park
stationDescription: Triangular Flat V weir (crest length 8m); theoretical rating. Drowns at high flows due to limited capacity of culverts d/s. No abstractions or returns. Just d/s of Lady Bridge gauging station (32012), a low flow-only station running 1968 to 1985.
catchmentDescription: Mostly underlain by Boulder Clay with some exposed Upper Lias clays and small areas of plateau gravel and Oolitic L'st. Catchment predominantly rural, with expanding Northampton suburbs near gauging station.
operator: Environment Agency - Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Bedford Ouse at Brownshill Staunch
stationDescription: Lowest station on the Bedford Ouse. Station consisted of a navigation lock and three radial gates with a low flow notch. Complicated hydrometric arrangement, with no unique stage-discharge relationship. Low flows based on gate openings, Rated section for high flows, using calibration by float measurement. Site closed in 1962. Record likely to be of poor quality, but provides indication of runoff patterns from 1936-62.
catchmentDescription: Large, mixed catchment. Geology of Great Oolite and Oxford clays, with substantial Boulder Clay cover. Mixed rural land use, some urban development in lower valleys.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at Bedford
stationDescription: 3 broad-crested weirs, 30m, 20m and 12m wide + 3 vertical sluice gates (either fully open/closed). High flow rating confirmed by current meter measurements. The rating assumes set gate openings. Recommended to be used to bankfull only. Records before 1959 based on daily gauge-board levels and gate openings (limited accuracy, few hifs & many replicated dmfs). From 1972, more convincing flow series available from Roxton d/s (33039). The Apr 1998 peak flow is thought to be an overestimate. The period-of-record maximum is believed to have occurred in Mar 1947; no peak flow available but is unlikely to be significantly higher than the DMF (278 cumecs). Surface and groundwater abstractions in catchment for PWS, Milton Keynes' effluent now significant.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly clay. Land use: agricultural with some urban development in lower valley areas over last 25 years.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Cam at Bottisham
stationDescription: Triangular profile weir, 7.7m wide, plus two vertical lift gates and a lock. Prior to Jun 1982 broad-crested weir incorporating a sharp-crested rectangular central notch, 2.4m wide. The lock is opened at high flows. Weir drowns at approx. 0.3m head. Patchy peak-flow record available. Two substantial gw abstractions for industry; 21 for PWS. All abstractions returned within the catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk; overlain by Boulder Clay in the S. Land use: predominantly agricultural.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Lark at Isleham
stationDescription: Navigation lock with vertical lift gate u/s plus a 16m broad high level weir in bypass channel for flood flows only. Small notch inserted in 1980. 1986 weir was lowered, altered to triangular profile and flows diverted to the bypass channel. The lock is now used during high flows only. Since 1968 major peaks diverted through cutoff channel (10 km u/s) - to the Relief Channel at Denver. Two large gw abstractions (Bury St Edmunds).
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk. Land use: arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at Thornborough Mill
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir 10.2m wide and two sluice gates 3.6m broad. Before 1976 weir was broad-crested with centre V notch. A bypass channel exists, but operation of gates has ensured the highest flows have been recorded. Flows not calculated since Aug 1991. Inaccurate flow measurement complicated by automatic sluice movement which was not integrated into flow calculation methods.
catchmentDescription: The catchment is flat and lies mainly on the Great Oolite. One large tributary drains an area of Oxford Clay. There is a water supply reservoir and a number of ornamental lakes in the catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Wissey at Northwold Total
stationDescription: Flow record is summation of a rectangular critical depth flume (4.9m wide, local number 33106) and a weir on a separate side stream (from March 1981, local number 33206) which takes approx. 10% runoff. Streams rejoin just d/s of gauges. Drowning common - flows adjusted. Data from Jun - Dec 2007 were anomalously high and therefore adjusted by the Environment Agency; values appear plausible but should be treated with caution. Limited net impact of abstractions and discharges but spray irrigation increasing (substantial proportion from groundwater).
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk overlain by 70% Boulder Clay (permeable in parts). Low population density. Largely arable in (northern) upper catchment, extensive grassland and some woodland in lower (southern) half of catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Nar at Marham
stationDescription: Critical depth flume, 7.16m wide. Prior to Apr 1982, flume (7.47m wide) contained low flow notch. Weed growth can be a problem during summer if not cut regularly. Anomalous summer flows in June 2004 and August 2006, resulting from locals damming the weir; awaiting reprocessing (2007). Surface water abstraction for PWS immediately u/s of station. Other abstractions in catchment. Three small underground abstractions were abandoned in 1986.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk catchment overlain by Boulder Clay in upper reaches. Land use: agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at Harrold Mill
stationDescription: Compound structure comprising a compound broad-crested weir plus two side spilling broad-crested weirs u/s. Not constructed for flow measurement, essentially a level site. Rated by formulae. High flows estimated. Major abstractions in catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: L'st overlain by Boulder Clay. Land use: mainly agricultural with substantial urban development over last 15 years (Milton Keynes).
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Little Ouse at County Bridge Euston
stationDescription: Compound weir with triangular profile centre section, 3.4m broad; broad-crested flanks in trapezoidal channel - 9m. Gw abstractions for PWS and spray irrigation. Hifs from Nov 1960.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk with significant Boulder Clay cover. Land use: agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Kym at Meagre Farm
stationDescription: Compound weir with triangular profile centre section 3m wide and broad-crested flanks in a trapezoidal channel, 8.5m wide. Rating modified to correct for drowning by weed growth using tailwater levels. Predominantly natural regime, modest abstractions for agriculture. One of 3 stations used to calculate Offord flow to determine abstraction regime at Grafham Water.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly clay catchment - very flashy by Anglian standards. Land use: agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Sapiston at Rectory Bridge
stationDescription: Rectangular thin-plate weir, 8.8m broad, suppressed end contractions with theoretical rating. Minor gw abstractions for PWS and agriculture. Hifs on NRFA from May 1960.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominately Chalk with Boulder Clay cover. Land use: agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Lark at Temple
stationDescription: Compound broad-crested weir with rectangular cross-section, 5.8m broad, central notch 3m broad. Full range rating confirmed by c/m measurements. Flows affected by milling u/s of gauging station. Significant gw abstractions in catchment for PWS, industry and agriculture.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk - 70% overlain with Boulder Clay. Land use - agricultural.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Ouzel at Willen
stationDescription: 10m wide Flat V Crump profile weir replaced compound broad-crested weir, 10m broad, in 1977 when river realigned. Radial lifting gate immediately u/s of weir diverts very high flows to adjacent balancing reservoir which empties d/s of weir. This may result in high flows being appreciably less than those seen upstream at Bletchley (33058). Annual floods do not bypass.
catchmentDescription: Mixed bedrock geology, with Greensand and Oxford Clay and some Boulder Clay cover. Primarily a rural catchment of mixed agriculture, although Leighton Buzzard is in catchment, and southern suburbs of Milton Keynes are situated in lower valley just u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Cam at Jesus Lock
stationDescription: Broad-crested weir V-shaped in plan, 21.3m long in parallel with two sluice gates and a navigation lock. Flows based on upstream and downstream levels and gate openings, subject to inaccuracies from gate opening data. Some abstractions in catchment. Station closed in 1983.
catchmentDescription: Chalk catchment partly overlain by Boulder Clay. Gauging station is in Cambridge.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at St Ives
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Tove at Cappenham Bridge
stationDescription: Compound broad-crested trapezoidal weir, 7.6m broad; central notch, 2.7m broad. Theoretical rating since Aug 1970. Previously, rating derived using a hydraulic model. The weir is subject to drowning at high flows; further gaugings required to confirm extent of this.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk overlain with 50% Boulder Clay. Land use: agricultural.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Thet at Melford Bridge
stationDescription: Triangular profile weir, 6.2m broad. Theoretical rating modified in Apr 1968. Weir subject to drowning during summer due to weed growth d/s. Flows are affected by groundwater abstraction, and by groundwater augmentation during drought conditions which can significantly raise flows (e.g. during the autumn of 2003). Significant high flow event in September 1968 missing from record. 2007-2008 late summer peak flows under review, due to suspected stilling well blockage causing underestimation of flows.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Chalk catchment; approx 70% overlain by Boulder Clay. Land use: primarily arable, with some grassland and forestry.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Alconbury Brook at Brampton
stationDescription: Flat V weir in trapezoidal channel, plus ultrasonic for use when head above crest>1m, replaced (from 3/6/95) broad-crested CP weir with central low flow notch (closed in Sep 1993). Theoretical rating but hydraulic model calibration for flanks prior to Apr 1978. Pre 1993 rating modified by c/m measurement to correct for drowning. Drowns at approx. 1m stage; spills at 2m. Post-1995 high flows are suspect and under review pending a high flow rating due early 2009. High flows impeded by bridges u/s and d/s. The highest flow on record is estimated by the EA to be 70 cumecs on 10/04/98, although the gauge was subject to bypassing. Influenced by abstraction and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly impervious catchment. 50% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: mainly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Rhee at Burnt Mill
stationDescription: Trapezoidal cross-section weir with triangular profile crest, 6.1m broad. Weir drowns out at high flows; rating modified by c/m measurements to correct for drowning. Weir also subject to drowning during summer due to weed growth d/s. Substantial gw abstractions for PWS. Augmentation from gw sources to regulate river flow - scheme aims to maintain minimum 0.3 cumecs flow at g/s. Consistent records available of groundwater pump operation. Some effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Chalk catchment - approx 15% overlain with Boulder Clay, in NW. Land use is primarily arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Ivel at Blunham
stationDescription: Crump profile weir 7.31m wide. Drowning occurs at 0.91m (rating includes drowning correction). Significant bypassing when stage exceeds 1.1m; `truncation' effect evident in annual maxima series. Pre-1966 record of poorer quality. Hydrograph shows influence of u/s mill operation. Effluent from STW has substantial effect on low flows. Many abstractions for spray irrigation. Groundwater abstractions for PWS. One of 3 stations used to calculate Offord flows to determine abstraction regime at Grafham Water.
catchmentDescription: The Ivel rises near Hitchin and flows north across Greensand, Chalk and Gault Clays to meet the Great Ouse east of Bedford. 45% superficial deposits, mainly Boulder Clay with some glacial sands and gravels. Predominantly agricultural, with some urban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Lea Brook at Beck Bridge
stationDescription: Crump profile weir 4m wide, situated approx. 20m u/s of new road bridge. All but the very highest flows are contained. High flows may be affected by Beck Bridge and railway embankment in the floodplain, but no flood this large has been recorded so far. There is some doubt about the high flow calibration owing to two large concrete blocks which spoil the entry condition. The POR maximum flow is believed to have been in Sept 1968, estimated as 17.5 cumecs by the EA but likely to have been extrapolated. The low flow calibration has been confirmed by c/m. Some gw abstraction for PWS, also industrial/agricultural abstractions.
catchmentDescription: A Chalk catchment with 40% Boulder Clay cover in upper (S) part of catchment. Some river terrace deposits in N. Land use: mixed agriculture.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Cam at Dernford
stationDescription: Rectangular thin-plate weir, 5.8m broad. Bridge pier may affect approach velocity at high flows. Weir subject to drowning, tailwater levels measured to assist rating. Five groundwater abstractions for PWS. Abstractions for industry and agriculture. Regime affected by effluent returns derived from gw pumped within catchment.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly pervious catchment underlain by Chalk. 50% Boulder Clay cover, primarily in headwater areas. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Babingly at West Newton Mill
stationDescription: Sharp-crested weir inside converted mill building, with a bypass gate to a mill wheel. Owners manipulated bypass gate, with no record kept of such openings, which limits accuracy of flows (particularly high flows, so unsuitable for flood purposes). Spring-fed stream, with unrealistic runoff because contributing area is greater than topographical catchment. Station closed 1976.
catchmentDescription: Small rural catchment, Chalk with some Boulder Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at Offord
stationDescription: Complex of automatic radial tilting weir, 15.2m broad; triangular profile weir, 14.8m broad; compound broad-crested weir, 22.7m broad. Navigation lock opened at flows above 40 cumecs. Abstraction 2km u/s for Grafham Water Res. (approx. 2 cumecs). Pollution monitoring can lead to the cessation of the abstraction pumps resulting in flow increases and subsequent drops, e.g., 03/09/07-11/09/07 and 20/02/08-25/02/08. Substantial surface water abstractions for PWS, industry and agriculture. Significant gw abstractions.
catchmentDescription: Large catchment of mixed geology, with substantial superficial deposits (50% Boulder Clay). Land use: mixed agriculture, some substantial urban areas (Milton Keynes, Bedford) although overall urban fraction is small.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Rhee at Wimpole
stationDescription: Trapezoidal critical depth flume, 6.6m broad; horizontal crest 3.8m. Drowns at peak levels; correction incorporated into theoretical rating. Spills occasionally - high flows impeded by bridge abutments 20m d/s. Some surface water and groundwater abstractions in catchment.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Chalk catchment with approx. 25% Boulder Clay cover in N and W. Land use: agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Flit at Shefford
stationDescription: Trapezoidal critical depth flume, 9.8m broad; 2.1m broad at horizontal crest. Structurefull 0.76m stage. Subject to drowning at medium flows. According to the EA, the highest peak flow on record is 33 cumecs on 03/01/2003, although uncertainty attends this value as there was considerable ungauged floodplain flow. Flows affected by u/s mill operation. Surface water abstraction for spray irrigation. Abstraction for PWS closed 1985. Flows augmented by effluent from Luton.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Greensand (60%), 45% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: mixed agriculture with patches of woodland. Several small towns in catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Stringside at Whitebridge
stationDescription: A trapezoidal critical depth flume, calibrated by model and designed to operate in the non-modular range. High flow rating incorporates out-of-bank flows and should be reliable in periods when the channel has been maintained. Two groundwater abstractions for PWS, and abstractions for industry/agriculture. Baseflow-dominated regime but can be responsive.
catchmentDescription: Small catchment, mostly low-lying, developed mainly on Chalk, 20% Boulder Clay cover (mainly in W). Mixed land use - arable predominates, with significant woodland in E.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Clipstone Brook at Clipstone
stationDescription: Crump weir from 1966, replaced earlier broad-crested weir. No major abstractions. Station terminated in 1980.
catchmentDescription: Greensand catchment, tributary to the river Ouzel.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Broughton Brook at Broughton
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir 7.0m wide installed in 1977 when river realigned. Prior to 23/6/77 trapezoidal critical depth flume 7.4m wide, horizontal crest 1m wide. Flume was subject to drowning - flows corrected. One PWS abstraction accounts for virtually all abstraction in catchment. Station near STW, influenced by effluent return.
catchmentDescription: Clay and Greensand catchment, with 50% Boulder Clay cover. The catchment is largely rural and flat, the edge just impinging on the Chiltern escarpment. Land use: mixed agriculture with some forestry in S, and some urban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Heacham at Heacham
stationDescription: Two Crump profile weirs in parallel, 3m broad. Weirs never drown. Small bypass channel with weir upstream of station, rejoining downstream of weir, is factored in to the stage-discharge relationship. Gw abstraction for PWS and irrigation. Topographical catchment area substantially exceeds the true contributing area (by a factor of about two).
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk, overlain by 40% Boulder Clay. Land use: agricultural.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Hiz at Arlesey
stationDescription: Crump profile weir, 7m broad. Subject to drowning at peak flows (e.g. August 2010). Highest recorded flow was contained by weir banks. Augmentation by effluent affects diurnal flow pattern. Significant gw abstractions for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Chalk catchment, with significant superficial deposits (20% Boulder Clay, 20% sands and gravels). Land use: predominantly arable farming but with significant urban development (Hitchin).
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Little Ouse at Abbey Heath
stationDescription: Rectangular section Crump profile weir with crest tapping. Replaced 33008 (Thetford Staunch) in 1968. Weir subject to drowning and spills on rare occasions. Subject to weed growth, on crest and in channel. Instrument hut vandalised in August 2000, data missing until August 2002. Affected by gw abstraction and, since the late 1980s, low flows augmented with groundwater in drought conditions.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk with approx. 80% superficial deposits, mainly Boulder Clay. Land use: predominantly arable with significant areas of forest and heathland, particularly in centre and E. Urban development (Thetford) just u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Ely Ouse at Denver Complex
stationDescription: Three sluices: d/s sluice discharges flow to maintain level in Relief Channel; u/s sluice diverts surplus into Cut Off Channel; dividing sluice can raise level by 0.6m, reversing flow in the Cut Off Channel where it is abstracted at Blackdyke and diverted into the headwaters of Essex rivers (Ely Ouse/Essex Transfer Scheme). In high floods gates are raised permitting channel to be used for original flood protection purpose. Complexity of the structure and its management is reflected in the homogeneity of the flow series - which has extensive periods with no data or zero flows. Data should be used with great caution.
catchmentDescription: Low lying catchment. Geology mixed: Peat and alluvium on Fenland Plain; Chalk overlain with Boulder Clay in higher regions to E and S: W predominantly clay. High quality agricultural land.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at Newport Pagnell Total
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir (29.3m broad), with crest tapping and central notch (3m broad). Separate, complementary Crump weir (3.7m broad), with crest tapping (local number 33237) constructed in old mill throttle, 7m u/s of a double arch culvert; subject to drowning at high flows. Abstractions for PWS approx. 25km u/s, and abstractions within catchment for industry/agriculture.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology with significant component of permeable rocks, much of which is overlain by Boulder Clay. (60%). Land use: mix of arable and grassland, some forest and some urban development in lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Bedford Ouse at Roxton
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir (26m broad) with crest tapping, situated immediately u/s of confluence with R. Ivel. Drowns at very high flows (e.g. Easter Floods 1998) and can spill on rare occasions. Adjacent lock acts as an overspill in flood conditions. Use in preference to Bedford (33002) if possible. The POR maximum flow in April 1998 was extrapolated and subject to bypassing but thought to be a reasonable estimate in a contemporary report by WS Atkins. Significant surface water and gw abstractions for PWS, industry and agriculture. One of 3 stations used to calculate Offord flows to determine abstraction regime at Grafham Water.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology, including significant Clay and Greensand fractions, with 50% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: predominantly agricultural with substantial urban development (Milton Keynes and Bedford).
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Rhee at Ashwell
stationDescription: Trapezoidal standing wave flume in moulded glass reinforced plastic. Situated 0.5km d/s of source of R. Rhee. Flows influenced by large gw abstractions for PWS. Min. spring flow of 0.03 cumecs maintained by artificial gw recharge for conservation purposes. Gw contributing area exceeds topographic CA. Runoff data unrealistic.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk. Land use: agricultural.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Thet at Bridgham
stationDescription: Crump profile weir, 6m broad. Prior to Oct 1979, broad-crested weir (crest: 7.4m), situated under double-arch bridge. Current rating only reliable to bankfull, and limited gaugings at low flows. Theoretical rating for original weir confirmed by c/m measurements. Gw abstractions in catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk with approx. 75% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: arable with some forest and grassland, several small towns.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Wittle at Quidenham
stationDescription: Compound broad-crested weir (crest 3m), with central notch separated by splitter plates; situated under road bridge. Theoretical rating modified by c/m measurements. Weir drowned in 1968 floods. Hifs available from 1973. Flow regime altered by pumping of two boreholes into river u/s of Quidenham to support Ely Ouse to Essex Transfer Scheme (abstraction point at Hockwold on the Little Ouse). Other abstractions in catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk overlain with 60% Boulder Clay. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Thet at Redbridge
stationDescription: Crump profile weir 4m broad. Theoretical rating confirmed by current metering to structurefull, thereafter rating allows for drowning and spilling. Hiflows-UK lists period-of-record maximum as 17.5 in Sept 1968 - this was extrapolated and flow was likely to have been out of bank. Gw abstractions for PWS and industry; surface water abstractions for spray irrigation. Highest instantaneous flows available from 1973.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk overlain with 95% Boulder Clay. Land use: agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Larling Brook at Stonebridge
stationDescription: A concrete flume of triangular cross-section with 1:1.5 side slopes, depth 0.8m. Flume is permanently drowned: record very poor. Theoretical rating.
catchmentDescription: Geology comprises Chalk overlain by glacial sand and gravel. Land use - rural, largely non arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Stanford Water at Buckenham Tofts
stationDescription: Site used pre-existing broad-crested weir between two lakes. Station was designed to measure discharge from pilot groundwater scheme area, although is largely natural. Discontinued 1980.
catchmentDescription: Chalk overlain by Boulder Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Snail at Fordham
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir, 4m broad. Prior to 1985 subsidiary Crump profile weir (0.7m broad) measured bypass channel discharge. Flows combined into single series. Side weir removed 12/84 and main weir rating adjusted to compensate (flows increased by 2%). The rating is valid up to modular flow limit. Significant gw abstractions for PWS and surface water abstractions for spray irrigation.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk, with 20% Boulder Clay cover (in S) and some river terrace deposits. Land use: rural, with 50% grassland. Limited urban development (e.g. Newmarket).
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Cam at Chesterford
stationDescription: Compound broad-crested weir, 22.3m broad (in trapezoidal section) with central notch 3m broad, 0.23m deep. Significant gw abstractions for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk - approx. 60% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Swaffham Lode at Swaffham Bulbeck
stationDescription: Crump profile weir, 2.5m broad (wing wall height 1.6m), situated immediately u/s of road bridge. Prior to 1973 thin-plate weir, 1.45m broad. Theoretical rating for Crump weir fits well to gaugings. Significant gw abstractions for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk, with no appreciable superficial deposits. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Granta at Stapleford
stationDescription: Compound weir with Crump notch (1.5m broad) and broad-crested flanks (3m broad) superseded - in 1981 - original thin-plate weir; some flows estimated, only monthly means are considered valid. Suspect flows in Sept 2005 may be due to artificial damming upstream. High baseflow component; area of groundwater catchment is less than topographical catchment.
catchmentDescription: Chalk catchment with 45% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: dominated by arable agriculture.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Babingley at Castle Rising
stationDescription: Triangular profile Flat V Crump weir, 4.5m broad; level of wingwalls is 1.2m above crest. Subject to drowning. Significant gw abstraction for PWS, abstractions for industry/agriculture also. High baseflow catchment - groundwater catchment area exceeds topographic divide.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk catchment, with patchy cover of Boulder Clay (20%) and sands and gravels (10%). Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Granta at Babraham
stationDescription: Triangular profile Flat V weir, 8.3m broad; constructed on an old brick weir. Height of wing walls above crest - 0.6m. The station is drowned and bypassed at high flows. The POR maximum flow in Oct 2001 is listed as 20.41 cumecs by Hiflows-UK, but this is subject to considerable uncertainty - high proportion of overbank flows. Significant gw abstractions for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk catchment with 50% superficial deposits (in headwater areas). Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Quy Water at Lode
stationDescription: Compound weir, 4.8m broad, with Crump profile centre section, 1m broad, 0.3m deep. At flows greater than 0.32 cumecs flow occurs over broad-crested flanks between vertical side walls. Pre-1975 data imprecise. Peak flow data from 1979. In dry weather stream leaks through bed into the fen and can dry up. Three large abstractions in catchment for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk catchment, with patchy superficial deposits (20%) of Boulder Clay and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Ouzel at Leighton Buzzard
stationDescription: Crump profile weir (6m broad) in trapezoidal section (7.5m broad). Weir subject to drowning at flows of approx. 4 cumecs. Intake weir (1m broad) to gravel pit, immediately u/s of station - infiltrates into Lower Greensand aquifer.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly Chalk. Rural catchment draining from the Chiltern escarpment. Land in lower reaches is gently undulating.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Ouzel at Bletchley
stationDescription: Flat V weir, 10m broad. Constructed to measure flows just u/s of urban development (Milton Keynes). In a country park and subject to vandalism. Small gw abstractions. Flows augmented by effluent from Leighton Buzzard.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology - mainly Upper and Lower Greensand, with some Chalk and Oxford Clay. Land use: mainly rural with mixed farming, Leighton Buzzard within catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Shep at Fowlmere One
stationDescription: Crump weir 1.1m wide installed in Mar 1983. Replaced a rectangular thin-plate weir operational from 1978. Levels from 1964. Station opened as part of gw scheme. Weed growth d/s may affect flow. Bridge invert immediately d/s would cause drowning above 0.4cumecs. Mean annual runoff exceeds mean annual rainfall - this is under investigation (Jan 2008).
catchmentDescription: Very small, mainly gw fed, catchment draining from the Chalk, SSE of Cambridge. Rural - mainly arable with some grassland, marsh and woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Guilden Brook at Fowlmere Two
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume in trapezoidal, straight channel. Opened as part of gw scheme. Abstraction u/s for spray irrigation. High baseflow contribution - groundwater catchment area exceeds topographical area, resulting in unrealistic runoff.
catchmentDescription: Very small, mainly gw fed, catchment draining the Chalk, SSE of Cambridge. Rural - mainly arable, some grassland, orchard and deciduous woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Little Ouse at Knettishall
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir, 4.5m broad. Structure drowns above 3.35 cumecs. Minor abstractions and returns. 3 wells constructed in 1987 to augment low flows.
catchmentDescription: Chalk catchment with 75% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Whaddon Brook at Whaddon
stationDescription: Pre-cast fibreglass flume set in concrete; long-crested flume crest 0.1m broad. Flows affected by effluent from Royston STW u/s of station.
catchmentDescription: Small Chalk catchment. Land use: mixed farming but with significant built-up area (Royston).
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Hiz at Hitchin
stationDescription: Old concrete weir with crest reshaped by steel beam to form compound Crump profile, 6.2m wide; central notch 1m wide, 0.14m deep. Substantial abstractions for PWS. High baseflow - groundwater catchment area is less than the topographical area, resulting in notably low mean runoff.
catchmentDescription: Small spring fed stream flowing through Hitchin market place. Geology - predominantly Chalk catchment with small amounts of sand, gravel and clay. Land use: 90% arable; minor urban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Granta at Linton
stationDescription: Compound Crump Weir, 5.3m broad with 1.5m central crest. Structure drowns when u/s water level exceeds about 0.46m. Some gw abstraction. River is pump supported to maintain flow at approx. 0.03 cumecs; zero flows occur when pumps fail.
catchmentDescription: Small Chalk catchment with 70% Boulder Clay cover. Land use is agricultural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Cheney Water at Gatley End
stationDescription: Crump weir, 0.3m broad, only measures up to a level of 0.23m. It dams a broad pool immediately u/s which is spring fed. The station was opened to monitor this spring as part of a gw scheme. Flows are affected by gw abstraction. The spring did not flow at all throughout 1997. Flows are now normally supported by gw pumping, pumps failed in 1998.
catchmentDescription: Superficial deposits free Chalk catchment. Rural, mainly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Lark at Fornham St Martin
stationDescription: Site consists of three parts: Central crump profile low flow weir, 2.57 m wide, set between flanks which are treated as a broad-crested weir 0.796 m wide (rb width 0.66 m, lb width 0.136 m, length of crest 1.30 m), and a side-weir for flood relief purposes, which consists of a crump weir 5.17 m wide in a bypass channel.
operator: Environment Agency - Cambridgeshire and Bedfordshire

name: Yare at Colney
stationDescription: A compound weir 11.9m wide reconstructed in Jan 1964 from single level broad-crested weir. The present structure has a Crump section 8.9m wide separated by a pier from a broad-crested weir at a lower level. Frequent bypassing on the left bank. Topographical catchment exceeds contributing area (gw catchment). Mill sluices artificially regulate flow. Gw is abstracted for agricultural uses. Significant seasonal variations in abstractions due to the use of water for spray irrigation in the summer.
catchmentDescription: Chalk catchment with superficial deposits, mainly Boulder Clay (85%) and alluvium. Rural catchment, land use is predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Tas at Shotesham
stationDescription: Originally a flume set between high rough walls bypassed at 14 cumecs. Reconstructed in 1970 as a Flat V Crump and a bypass channel with movable gates added in 1980. Some high flows only partially gauged as water diverts around the station through the bypass channel. Magnitude of flood peak in Sept 1968 is uncertain owing to bypassing, but was undoubtedly an exceptional event generated by 80-100mm rainfall. Peaks 25-28/04/1981 and 02-04/05/1981 suspect due to instrument fault. Period of 5 May- 25 August 1986 has been deleted due to anomalously low flows - probable stage shift. Data for Jan-Apr 1989 are missing due to instrument fault. Small net augmentation of flows (sewage effluent).
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Chalk catchment overlain with drift, mainly (95%) Boulder Clay. Rural catchment, mainly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Bure at Ingworth
stationDescription: Two ogee profile weirs beneath bridge arches, bypassed (through low spots in the bank u/s) at 4.3 cumecs but maintains modularity. Occasionally drowns owing to weed and reed growth - some contention with coincident SSSI regime means that weed clearance is only done annually. Limited ground and surface water abstractions with some returns from public and agricultural uses.
catchmentDescription: Mixed Clay and Chalk geology, overlain with superficial deposits of Boulder Clay (55%) and glacial sands and gravels (45%). Land use: agriculture, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Wensum at Costessey Mill
stationDescription: 'Complex' structure comprising 30m Crump weir in main channel; tilting gate and 1.5m wide Crump weir in the mill channel. Gate fully automated in 1998; constantly regulating u/s levels to maintain level through the mill race. Also artificial regulation by sluice action at Taversham. Bypassed further upstream on the left bank at 20-25 cumecs. Many gaps in the record - in recent years particularly. Moderate surface and gw abstractions, influential in low flow years - very low flows in July 2006 believed to be due to over-abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Chalk catchment with 95% superficial deposits, mainly Boulder Clay but with sands and gravels in valley bottoms. Rural, predominantly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Tud at Costessey Park
stationDescription: Four trapezoidal standing-wave flumes under a road bridge have movable dam boards placed across the two outer arches to increase the sensitivity of low flow measurements. Dam boards are only used at very low flows, less than 1 cumec. The gw catchment is smaller than the topographical catchment with consequent losses to adjacent catchments and low annual gauged runoff. Affected by gw and surface water abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Chalk catchment with superficial deposits of Boulder Clay (80%), sands and gravels and alluvium. Rural catchment with mixed farming, mostly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Waveney at Needham Mill
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir 8.5m wide in main channel with single crested Crump in mill bypass. Sluice action at mill 2.4 km u/s is infrequent but evident in flow records; sluice rarely open after 1985. Suffers from weed growth problems. Surface water abstractions, and use of river gravels as an aquifer, influence flows but overall impact is minimal. Record affected by the Waveney Groundwater Scheme between 1975 and 1979.
catchmentDescription: Geology: clay and chalk with 90% Boulder Clay cover. Land Use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Dove at Oakley Park
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir with low flow notch and crest tapping; non-modular at 13 cumecs and bypassed at 18 cumecs. Obstructions on the weir have occasionally led to erroneously high summer flows. Gw abstractions and effluent returns have a minor net effect on flows, however, between 1975 and 1979 effects more significant due to the Waveney Groundwater Scheme.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk and clay catchment, overlain with >90% Boulder Clay. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Ant at Honing Lock
stationDescription: Crump type weir utilising the fall of an old navigation lock. Immediately u/s is a large marshy area with dense weed growth from which some flow bypasses the station. No chart back up: limited space within kiosk. Logger failure Jan. 98 so no dmfs. Suspect data in summer 1996. Gw abstractions moderately reduce the natural runoff.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly rural catchment of approximately 50% sand and gravel and 50% loam.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Waveney at Billingford Bridge
stationDescription: Two gauging stations located u/s of two bridge arches: i) compound Crump with low flow notch (insensitive, suffers occasional drowning due to d/s weedgrowth); ii) simple Crump with lifting gate to retain higher summer levels. Partial gate movements can affect summer flows (e.g. July 2008). Bypassing occurs at 6.4 cumecs, drowning can result from sluice action at Hoxne Mill (manual correction applied). Surface and gw abstracted, effluent returned. Affected by Waveney Groundwater Scheme between 1975 and 1979. Crest tapping blocked 25 November - 5 December 2008, estimation of hydrograph peak not possible. Flows above 1.66 cumecs are only available when the structure gate is open, otherwise they cannot be calculated.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk catchment overlain with 85% Boulder Clay. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Wensum at Fakenham
stationDescription: Compound Crump with low flow notch, immediately u/s of Fakenham Mill. A lifting gate for retaining summer levels acts as a sharp-crested weir. Gate is raised in winter to pass high flows. Unlikely to be bypassed apart from in exceptional events. Theoretical rating. Gaugings confirm rating when gate is closed but indicate flows are underestimated when gate is open. In need of rating review. Gw abstractions have a minimal impact on runoff.
catchmentDescription: Low-lying Chalk catchment overlain with 75% Boulder Clay cover and some sands and gravels. Land use: predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Burn at Burnham Overy
stationDescription: A Crump weir which bypasses at 2.3 cumecs. Can be affected by mill operations c 50m upstream - notable effects on low flows in summer 2005 and 2008. Annual hydrographs reflect high baseflow component from the Chalk aquifer. Gw abstractions have only a minimal impact on the natural runoff. May be a minor net import from outside the catchment due to effluent.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk with 60% Boulder Clay cover and some sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly (>80%) arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Wensum at Swanton Morley Total
stationDescription: Two structures 150m apart operate in parallel. Beneath the two-arch bridge are two Crump weirs which sometimes display non-modular flow as a result of summer weed growth d/s. Three Crump weirs are sited in arches beneath a second bridge. Gw abstractions cause a moderate reduction in the natural runoff.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk overlain by drift (70% Boulder Clay, 50% sands and gravels). Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Stiffkey at Warham
stationDescription: Flat V weir with crest tapping, drowns above 0.8 cumecs. Prior to 1978 (when dredging took place) d/s weed growth during summer months could cause complete drowning of gauging structure at lower flows. Large abstractions from gw for PWS causes a significant reduction in the natural runoff. Weir derived flows suspect from 7 June 2009 onwards due to construction of a riffle d/s.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk catchment with 75% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Bure at Horstead Mill
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir consisting of 5 Crumps: 4 at fixed levels, the narrowest of which incorporates a fish pass. A vertical lift gate converts the largest to a sharp edge weir, this gate is used, during summer months, to retain u/s water levels. Limited ground and surface water abstractions u/s. Hydrograph closely reflects mill gate operation. Data for 1997 - 2000 was reprocessed and loaded in 2003. Previous data was suspect owing to error in rating.
catchmentDescription: Low lying rural catchment of sand and gravel.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Gipping at Constantine Weir
stationDescription: A 152 ft long, broad-crested weir, within tidal range (thus strictly on the Orwell, not Gipping). Only measures flow at low tide. Station is primarily retained for estimation of high flows. Weir calibrated by model tests, no checks made. Situation is a problem - u/s and d/s bends are measured to correct for drowning. Major artificial influences (including Ipswich flood defence scheme), but net import is minor. Station closed in 1996.
catchmentDescription: A rural catchment, the only town being Ipswich. Boulder Clay overlying Chalk.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Deben at Naunton Hall
stationDescription: A compound Crump (with crest tapping) and low flow notch. Bypassing occurs at 12 cumecs and seasonal weed growth causes drowning. Some gw is transferred to beyond the catchment boundary and some is abstracted from within the catchment. The overall impact is to significantly reduce the natural runoff.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly clay and Chalk, overlain by Boulder Clay (80%) and sands and gravels (15%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Alde at Farnham
stationDescription: Broad-crested weir of ogee section with low flow notch and steel plate divide walls. Rating is non-modular at high flows (above 0.838m stage) and flows go out of bank; some high flows are missing as a result (e.g. Oct 93, May 2000, Oct 2000). Rating confirmed by gaugings. Significant gw abstractions; some water exported. The gw contours show only token relationship to the surface topography. Runoff reduced by abstraction, with some water exported from catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly clay and Chalk, overlain by Boulder Clay (85%) and sands and gravels (15%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Ore at Beversham Bridge
stationDescription: A compound Crump weir with low flow notch and crest tapping that occasionally drowns as a result of d/s weedgrowth and siltation. Gw catchment exceeds topographic catchment. Gw abstractions make a moderate reduction in the natural runoff.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly clay and Chalk, overlain by Boulder Clay (75%) and sands and gravels (25%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Gipping at Stowmarket
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir rebuilt in 1966 from a compound broad-crested weir. All flows contained. Minimal natural storage within the catchment and the Boulder Clay gives a flashy response. Abstractions from groundwater and effluent returns broadly balance. High flows have been significantly affected by flood relief scheme since the late 1980s.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly clay and Chalk, overlain by Boulder Clay (95%) and sands and gravels. Land use: mainly rural (arable farming) although there is some urban development just u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Gipping at Bramford
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir with 2m, 5m & 12m sections. Flood gate (automated during summer 2001) converts 12m section to a sharp-edge weir. Bypassing occurs at 12 cumecs and sluice operation on the weir is evident in the daily flow record. Gw abstractions have a significant impact on the natural runoff. Flows significantly affected by Ipswich flood relief scheme since the late 1980s.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly clay and Chalk, overlain by superficial deposits, mainly (>80%) Boulder Clay. Land use: mainly rural, (arable predominates), but with some urban development in the valley.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Belstead Brook at Belstead
stationDescription: Missing data during high flow periods exist when the current rating does not estimate high flows. The rating does not extrapolate beyond this level due to uncertainties in non-modular flows over the structure.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Blyth at Holton
stationDescription: An asymmetric compound Crump weir with low flow notch. Gw abstractions have a significant effect on the natural runoff. The river responds very rapidly to rainfall.
catchmentDescription: Surface geology dominated by Boulder Clay (80%), with sands, gravels and crag deposits. The land use is predominantly rural.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Mill River at Newbourne
stationDescription: Trapezoidal sharp-edged weir in an old mill stream.  Site known to suffer from bypassing.  Station closed in 1969.
catchmentDescription: Small catchment formed on Crag geology. Rural, with outer suburbs of Ipswich at top of catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Stour at Stratford St Mary
stationDescription: Three separate weirs and five sluice gates operated by Essex and Suffolk Water plc at Stratford St Mary WTW. Theoretically rated. Daily naturalised flows from 1932 to 1976. Records from 1928. Since Apr 1978 upper limit of reliable gauging 16 cumecs. Extreme floods bypass on rb. WTW (including PWS abstractions) and, to lesser extent Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme (since 1971), highly influence flow.
catchmentDescription: Rural. Chalk overlain by Boulder Clay in upper catchment and London Clay in lower part.
operator: Essex and Suffolk Water Plc.

name: Glem at Glemsford
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume with bypassing at high flows; modest modular limit; d/s water level recorder to allow for drowning. Occasional problems with weedgrowth. Highest floods unreliably gauged. Naturalised flows from 1960 to Sep 1976. Exceptional minimum in Oct 1997 follows cessation of augmentation (via Cranmore Green outfall) in Sept.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Upper Chalk (exposed in river valley sides) is overlain by glacial sand and gravel (10%) and semi-pervious Boulder Clay (85%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Box at Polstead
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume with high flow rated spillway. Throat tapping; rarely drowns. Non-modular flows recorded since 1997 using secondary instrument. Subject to reed/weed growth problems. Naturalised flows from 1961 to 1976. Minimal ground and surface water abstractions for agricultural purposes.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly London Clay; Chalk in N, all overlain by Boulder Clay (70%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Chad Brook at Long Melford
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with low flow side weir which drowns in summer due to weed growth; poorly maintained channel also influences modular limit. High flow spillway accurate to 1.1m. Theoretically rated. Full range with convincing water-balance but low flow data should be treated as indicative only. Magnitude of POR maximum flow in Sept 1968 is under review (Jan 2008) - flow is supported by EA, but may be an underestimate as flow known to have been extrapolated. Naturalised flows from 1965 to 1976. Runoff influenced by industrial/agricultural abstraction. Missing data in 2010 and 2011 is as a result of water level management practices which caused the structure to become non-modular.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk with complete cover of Boulder Clay. Land use: rural, predominantly (85%) arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Brett at Hadleigh
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with low flow side weir and high flow rated spillway. D/s water level recorder to allow for drowning. Naturalised flows from 1962 to 1976. Since 1976, adjustments for gw abstractions for PWS and industrial abstraction from surface water are no longer made to the gauged daily mean flows. Minor effluent returns may affect flow.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Upper Chalk overlain by semi-pervious Boulder Clay (>90%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Stour at Langham
stationDescription: Twin-trapezoidal flume, throat tapping. Spillway channel with thin-plate weir constructed in 12/85 takes some flow above 1.45m. Rating combines the elements. Excellent site. Bypassing also occurs over opposite bank above 1.85m. More bypassing possible from 0.5km u/s during extreme events. Naturalised flows to 9/76. Occasional peaks due to mill operation and augmentation by intermittent pumping from Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme. Low flows can be affected by PWS abstractions (e.g. June/July 2008). Occasional augmentation from SAGS (Stour Augmentation Groundwater Scheme) borehole pumping.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk outcrops in north, London Clay in south, overlain by superficial deposits, mainly semi-pervious Boulder Clay (75%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable, minor urban development in valley bottom.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Belchamp Brook at Bardfield Bridge
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume with throat tapping. Full range station in winter, occasionally drowns in summer due to weed growth. Naturalised flows from 1965 to 1976, only minimal adjustments needed to flows since then.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk in N, clays in S, all overlain by Boulder Clay (80%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly (90%) arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Stour at Westmill
stationDescription: Compound trapezoidal critical depth flume with d/s level recorder. Affected by weed growth but rarely drowns out. Above 1.15m some flow passes over a broad-crested weir 100m u/s into a spillway. Since March 1971, flow augmented by intermittent pumping from the Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme, archived flows adjusted for this until 1976. (Naturalised flows 1960 to 1976.)
catchmentDescription: Geology: Upper Chalk overlain by superficial deposits, mainly semi-pervious Boulder Clay (90%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Brett at Cockfield
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump weir). No spillway. Modular limit of 0.66m theoretically derived; secondary instrument to record non-modular flows installed in 2003. Lack of gaugings at high flows. Naturalised flows from 1969 to 1976, only minimal adjustments needed since.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Upper Chalk underlies the whole catchment, completely covered with Boulder Clay. Land use: rural, predominantly (85%) arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Bumpstead Brook at Broad Green
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping and high flow spillway. Modular limit approx. 6.0 cumecs. Approx. limit of gauging is 12.5 cumecs. Naturalised flows from 1968 to 1976, only minor adjustments needed to the gauged dmfs, virtually natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk catchment overlain by complete cover of Boulder Clay. Land use: rural, predominantly (>85%) arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Stour Brook at Sturmer
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping, insensitive. Modular limit approx. 5.0 cumecs. Immediately d/s of Haverhill - urban runoff and STW discharges cause short, sharp peaks. Adjustments were made for industrial and sewage effluent and gw abstractions from 1968 to 1976.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk with superficial deposits, mainly (95%) Boulder Clay. Land use: mainly rural (arable farming) upper catchment, considerable urban development (>15% of catchment area) in lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Stour at Kedington
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir, insensitive. No spillway. Crest tapping prone to siltation which made modular limit uncertain until 1970, when channel improved for Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme, making station full range. Ponding u/s above 11.5 cumecs. Structure built on peat, some percolation beneath. Naturalised flows 1968-1976. Post '76 adjustments made for Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme. Effect of Transfer Scheme results in unrealistic water balance.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk overlain with drift, predominantly (95%) Boulder Clay. Land use: rural, mainly arable with some grassland.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Brett at Higham
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping. Record incomplete, flows not calculated when drowning >70%. Very low modular limit due to backing up from Essex and Suffolk Water plc waterworks on Stour at Stratford St Mary (confluence approx. 450m d/s).
catchmentDescription: Upper Chalk covered with semi-pervious Boulder Clay N of Hadleigh, lower reaches mainly London Clay overlain by superficial deposits. Predominantly rural catchment (except for Hadleigh).
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Stour at Lamarsh
stationDescription: Flat V weir with low flow sharp-crested rectangular notch. Flood banks contain approx. 35 cumecs. No spillway. Breaching u/s may cause bypassing. Naturalised flows 1972-1976, since then adjustments needed for abstractions, discharges and PWS predominantly for Haverhill. Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme adjustments already made.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Upper Chalk with some London clay in S, overlain by superficial deposits (85% Boulder Clay). Land use: mainly rural (predominantly arable), some built-up areas (Sudbury is 5km u/s of station).
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Roding at Redbridge
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir superseded insensitive broad-crested weir in 1962. Calibration above 35 cumecs is based upon model tests. Flows augmented by moderate net import of water (naturalised flows 1951-75) but diversion of Luxborough STW effluent, completed in 1987, reduced dmfs. Pattern of low flows influenced by abstractions.
catchmentDescription: Low lying, mainly impervious (London Clay and superficial deposits) catchment. Land use: rural with significant urban development close to the gauging station.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Chelmer at Rushes Lock
stationDescription: Sharp-crested, shallow V profile weir (insensitive), replaced broad-crested timber weir in 1972. Complex hydrometric history. Weir provides head for lock (navigable river), discharge through lock not measured. Lock thought to influence flow pattern at low flows during busy periods, e.g. in summer of 2006. Weir repaired in 1982 because of serious leakage. No accurate measure of low or high flow, upper limit is 0.7m (19.99 cumecs). Bypassing begins at 0.57m. Flows naturalised from 1932 to 1976.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly London Clay with extensive superficial deposits (50% Boulder Clay). Land use: mainly rural (arable farming predominates), but some urban development; Chelmsford is u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Ter at Crabbs Bridge
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume with throat tapping, replaced less accurate station - Hatfield Peverel, 900m d/s, in 1964. Theoretically rated. Modular limit 0.95m, no level yet recorded above 1.6m (structurefull). Hatfield Peverel record held with this station - 1932 to 1964. Naturalised flows - 1964 to 1976. Minor surface water abstractions for spray irrigation, small discharges from STW but net export through PWS.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London clay, overlain with superficial deposits of Boulder Clay (80%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Blackwater at Langford
stationDescription: Complex of weirs and sluices near the intake works of what was the Southend Water Company. Measurement included the amounts abstracted from the river. Quality of data is unknown as there is limited information on the site. Station closed 1968.
catchmentDescription: Practically the whole catchment is covered with Boulder Clay, overlying glacial gravels. In the South East, London clay underlies the drift but is rarely exposed.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Colne at Lexden
stationDescription: Large trapezoidal flume with d/s level recorder. Occasional weedgrowth problems. Spillway flow commences at 17.0 cumecs (1.75m), flows above this are estimated (guided by highest gauging at 2.1m). Bypassed on the lb at very high flows. Naturalised flows for period 1959 to 1976. Exceptionally low summer 1965 flows under review. Provides flood warning for Colchester.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly London Clay and some Upper Chalk, with superficial deposits of semi-pervious Boulder Clay (70%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Can at Beach's Mill
stationDescription: Triple throated compound flume (built within mill race). Trapezoidal centre section for low flows. Limit of station - 35 cumecs. Spillway flow starts at 2.0m - very significant part of flow, allowance for this not made in record. Period-of-record maximum flow in Oct 2000 may have been higher due to spillway flows. High-end rating is currently under review (Jan 2008). Naturalised flows from 1962 to 1976. Adjustments needed for industrial and sewage effluent.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay overlain with Boulder Clay in N. Land use: rural, predominantly arable with some grassland; some built-up areas in the south of the catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Wid at Writtle
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir. Rated spillway starts at 1.25m. Full range, modular station. Weir began to subside in 1991. Flow during summer months consists predominantly of STW discharge, of which approximately 0.08 cumecs is derived from outside the catchment, adjustments needed for this. Flows naturalised from 1964 to 1976. Responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay with scattered areas of Boulder Clay cover. Land use: mainly rural, mixed arable and grassland, but with significant urban component.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Chelmer at Springfield
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir. Full range station, no drowning. Naturalised flows from 1965 to 1976. Surface water abstraction, mainly for spray irrigation, some industrial purposes. Gw abstractions from confined Chalk aquifer for PWS and industrial activities.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly London Clay, with 75% cover of Boulder Clay. Land use: mainly rural (arable farming predominates) but with urban development (N Chelmsford) just u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Brain at Guithavon Valley
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with throat tapping. Secondary instrument installed in 2003 to record non-modular flows. Drowning occurs at very low levels but with minimal effect. Station prone to vandalism. Some abstractions for agriculture; naturalised flows available from 1962 to 1976.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay with superficial deposits, mainly Boulder Clay (70%) and sands and gravels. Land use: Mainly rural, but some built-up areas; Braintree in mid-catchment and parts of Witham just u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Blackwater at Appleford Bridge
stationDescription: Double throated trapezoidal flume with throat tappings and a high flow rated spillway starting at 1.80m. Drowning starts at 1.2m (13.0 cumecs), degree of drowning variable. Oct 2001 was undoubtedly an exceptional event, but magnitude suspect as likely to have been affected by drowning; may be reduced in light of reviews undertaken by Hiflows-UK (Jan 2008). Naturalised flows from 1962-1976. Intermittently affected, since 1971, by Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme pumping, significantly affected in the late 1980s and 1990s. Abstractions from both Chalk and gravel aquifers for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay with Chalk in the headwaters, overlain by cover of Boulder Clay (80%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Chelmer at Churchend
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume (with central division wall which commences above the lowest contraction) with throat recorder and spillway for flows over 1.42m. Secondary instrument to record non-modular flows installed in 2003. Measures up to the 1 in 10 year flood (approx. 1.69m) above which bypassing occurs. More gaugings required at higher flows to confirm rating. Drowning minimal. Responsive regime. Naturalised flows from 1963 to 1976. Minimal adjustments needed.
catchmentDescription: Upland (for East Anglia) catchment at head of R. Chelmer. Upper quarter of catchment is Chalk, remainder is London Clay, both overlain by Boulder Clay (85%). Land use: rural, predominantly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Colne at Poolstreet
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume with throat tapping. V notch plate installed in summer to measure low flows. High flow spillway (above 1.34m). Above 1.6m flows are estimated as major bypassing occurs. Rarely non-modular. Naturalised flows from 1963-1976. Great Yeldham PWS borehole may influence flows, but unquantifiable. Period of Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme pumping in 1982.
catchmentDescription: Upland (for East Anglia) catchment. Geology: Upper Chalk, London Clay present in southern half, all overlain with Boulder Clay (90%) and sands and gravels. Land use: rural, predominantly (>80%) arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Sandon Brook at Sandon Bridge
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping. Insensitive. Modular limit approx 0.6m. Subject to weed growth and accretion. CA includes 13.7 sq.km draining to Hanningfield Reservoir 10km u/s. Naturalised flows (1963-1976) account for reservoir compensation water (0.011 cumecs) and storm overflows. Minor additions from industrial effluent.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay with sand and gravel over high ground in NE. Land use: rural, mainly arable with some grassland.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Roding at High Ongar
stationDescription: 'Essex' (modified Flat V) weir in an 11m wide section. Model-based calibration. Overfall operates (>1.7m) into a bypass channel; bypassing can also occur on both banks. Structure subject to drowning (during high flows backwater effects from two d/s bridges are influential); computed flows assume modularity - c/m rating under development; complex history of flood ratings. Evidence of weir settlement discovered in 1991, flows corrected 1987-91. Naturalised flows: 1964-76; minimal net import. Responsive flow regime. Spray irrigation can substantially deplete low flows.
catchmentDescription: Generally low lying, largely impermeable (London Clay/glacial deposits), agricultural catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Cripsey Brook at Chipping Ongar
stationDescription: Flat V weir (5.6m broad) installed in 1981 - superseded a compound thin-plate weir of limited capacity, most early flows truncated at around 2.5 cumecs. Modular calibration - therefore over-estimation of flows during periods of drowned flow (e.g. autumn 2000 - exceptionally high flow on the 29th Oct.). Flows missing (probably including POR minima) for much of 1976. Responsive flow regime. Sewage effluent is an important component of low flows. Impact of abstraction also evident on hydrograph.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly impermeable catchment (London Clay but with extensive areas of glacial deposits). A rural catchment, agriculture is the primary land use.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Pant at Copford Hall
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir (insensitive) with crest tapping. Measures up to 12.0 cumecs, flows above this are estimated because of the spillway. Naturalised flows 1965-1976. Intermittent pumping of Ely/Ouse Transfer Scheme has major effect on station, which is only 5km d/s of the Great Sampford Outfall.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Upper Chalk, overlain by Boulder Clay (85%). Glacial gravels exposed along the whole river valley. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Blackwater at Stisted
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping. No spillway. Modest modular limit affected by weed growth. Urban runoff from Braintree. Naturalised flows 1969-1976. Minor adjustments needed for ground and surface water abstractions, sewage and industrial effluent and Ely Ouse/Essex discharges. Net effect - minor export of water.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Upper two-thirds of catchment Chalk, remainder London Clay (exposed in valleys), all overlain with Boulder Clay (85%) and glacial gravel. Land use: mainly rural (arable predominates) but with Braintree in lower valley, just u/s of station.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Ingrebourne at Gaynes Park
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir in 9.5m wide section. All but exceptional floods contained. Model-based calibration assumes modularity; however, drowning occurs above about 7 cumecs (pressure tapping unreliable). Runoff increased by effluent returns but reduced by industrial/agricultural abstractions; minor net augmentation of runoff (transfer from R. Beam). Naturalised flow available for period 1970-75.
catchmentDescription: Largely impermeable catchment (London Clay/glacial deposits). Rural headwaters but substantial urban development around lower reaches.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Beam at Bretons Farm
stationDescription: 'Essex' (modified Flat V Crump) weir in 10.4m wide section. Model-based calibration assumes modularity; drowning is uncommon. Separate spillway accommodates flow > 16 cumecs. Naturalised flows: 1966-75; very small net diminution in runoff. Flood storage lagoons (on R. Rom) influence flows patterns (from late 1980s).
catchmentDescription: A predominantly impermeable catchment (London Clay overlain in places by glacial deposits). Mainly rural headwaters, substantial - and growing - urban development in lower reaches.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Chelmer at Felsted
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping measuring up to 1.21m - limit of reliable gauging, higher flows estimated. Flood plain storage starts at 1.1m, no spillway. Drowning commences at 0.6m, and varies in severity. Naturalised flows 1970-1976. Minimal abstractions, adjustments needed for STW and industrial effluent discharges.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Chalk in N, London Clay elsewhere, overlain with 75% Boulder Clay. Land use: rural, predominantly arable.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Roman River at Bounstead Bridge
stationDescription: Initially a temporary broad-crested weir 3/65-9/69 with low flow V notch (data suspect). 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir with crest tapping from 11/3/70. Low modular limit (0.4m); affected by weed growth and siltation (structure drowned from winter 1990/91 to May 92 and problems continue. Limited overspill starts at 0.35m - 3.0 cumecs. Flows naturalised 1970-1976. Data since 31/08/1991 not stored on NRFA due to continuing problems with drowning.
catchmentDescription: Rural catchment in conservation area. London Clay covered with glacial gravel and Boulder Clay in the upper third.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Holland Brook at Thorpe le Soken
stationDescription: 'Essex' profile (modified Flat V Crump) weir (very insensitive) with crest tapping. Tidal influence very important, gauging limits variable due to d/s tidal conditions, with drowning starting at very low levels. Very gentle river gradient makes siltation a major problem, accompanied by weed growth. Flows naturalised 1970-1976, abstractions significant at low flows. Station closed during 2012.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay with cover of Boulder Clay (35% of area) in NW. Land use: rural, predominantly arable with some grassland.
operator: Environment Agency - Essex Norfolk and Suffolk

name: Roding at Loughton
