operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Gade at Bury Mill
stationDescription: Rectangular flume with side contractions installed by Hemel Hempstead Development Corporation; the town has a significant effect on flood peaks (but moderated by balancing ponds u/s within built-up area). Leak in stilling well discovered in 1990. Vandalism and debris in channel can create problems. Rating has tendency to underestimate high flows. Baseflow-dominated regime with significant artificial disturbance.
catchmentDescription: Dip-slope stream draining the Chilterns; solid geology Chalk with some tertiary and extensive boulder clay cover. A mainly rural catchment draining to Hemel Hempstead (part of which is u/s of the station).
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Cole at Inglesham
stationDescription: Compound Crump Weir (high central crest). Channel divides immediately below weir; left-hand side of the structure is subject to non-modular conditions (this can cause significant data processing problems). Drowning due to weedgrowth also likely in the summer. More gaugings required to confirm rating. Pre-1992 flows may be too low; possible overestimation of contemporary flood flows also. Some baseflow from the Chalk but a responsive regime influenced by rapid recent urban development.
catchmentDescription: Chalk scarp headwaters but catchment is largely low-lying and impervious (clay). A mainly rural catchment, some urban growth below the headwaters (Swindon particularly).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Misbourne at Quarrendon Mill
stationDescription: Informal broad-crested control - secondary station, superseded in 1984 by the new Denham Lodge station d/s. Complex baseflow dominated regime (the Misbourne has a number of influent reaches). Substantial gw abstraction (especially for Aylesbury and Amersham) - historical decline in Misbourne flows.
catchmentDescription: The Misbourne is a dip-slope stream draining from the Chilterns - Chalk with a little Drift cover. Significant residential development in the valley but a largely rural (Green Belt) catchment given over to agriculture with scattered tracts of coniferous and mixed woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Dollis Brook at Hendon Lane Bridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir has replaced a compound broad-crested weir with rectangular thin-plate inset (lack of confirmatory gaugings, severe u/s siltation - occasionally moderated by flushing via sluice below weir - but can seriously affect hydrometric performance e.g. summer 2007). Modular throughout the flow range. Further gaugings required to confirm the Flat V rating. Some anomalous low flows (e.g. March 1997). Very responsive regime. Time series should be used with caution. Flows Jun 2004-Apr 2010 under review due to stilling pipe blockage.
catchmentDescription: Catchment in north-west suburbs of London: Barnet and Hendon but with significant rural tracts in western headwaters. Geology: entirely London Clay of Eocene age. Largely Drift free.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Brent at Monks Park
stationDescription: Critical depth flume (rectangular) in a concrete channel, d/s of Brent Reservoir. Discrepancy between rating and available gaugings. Responsive regime with very artificial low flow pattern (Welsh Harp reservoir is u/s).
catchmentDescription: A largely urban/suburban catchment in north-west London.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Crane at Marsh Farm
stationDescription: Rectangular critical depth flume (insensitive) in a straight concrete channel. Theoretical rating; modular limit to be determined. Capacity approx. 30 cumecs; yet to be exceeded. Substantial artificial influence on flow pattern - evident at low flows: sewage effluent, automatic weir u/s diverts flow into the Duke's River (may result in negligible flows), considerable area of gravel workings. Some natural runoff gain from the Colne catchment (but unlikely to compensate for the u/s diversion).
catchmentDescription: Very flat - drainage network difficult to delineate. Mainly urban; catchment contains Heathrow Airport and several pumped storage res. (abstracting from Thames).
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Quaggy at Manor House Gardens
stationDescription: A critical depth flume (width: 4m, wing wall height: 2m) in a concrete channel. The flume was constructed in 1961 and calibrated theoretically - the low flow rating has since been revised on the basis of c/m gauging results; more gauging required to confirm the high flow rating). Modular range uncertain. The structure was overwhelmed by the 1968 flood but sensibly full range otherwise. Very responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: The catchment is a mixture of urban and suburban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Wealdstone Brook at Wembley
stationDescription: Flat V profile weir in a culvert (below Olympic Way Wembley). Following modifications to the structure in 1978 it was refurbished but crest is sub-standard and a few gaugings suggest that the theoretical rating may not be applicable especially in high flow range. Data under review (as more confirmatory gaugings required). Responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: A largely impervious urban/suburban catchment in west London.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Thames at Buscot
stationDescription: Complex weir - radial gates and overfall weirs - embracing two channels. Two u/s and two d/s head recorders. Calibrated using c/m measurements. All but highest flows contained. Small net export of water (due to gw abstraction). Closed in 1998.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology; runoff from the Cotswolds (Oolitic L'st) provides a significant baseflow but the Oxford Clay valley is much more responsive. Land use: rural/agricultural with settlements concentrated in the valley where gravel extraction is significant.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Pinn at Uxbridge
stationDescription: Electromagnetic (overhead coil) station in formalised trapezoidal section. Full-range capability but limited number of higher flow gaugings suggest substantial overestimation in this range. Multiple 0.01 cumecs entries are an instrumental artifact. Very flashy regime. Very limited impact of artificial influences on the flow pattern, particularly given its suburban character. EM coil removed in 2004 - now a level-only flood warning site.
catchmentDescription: An impermeable (largely London Clay) surburban catchment west of London. Headwaters rise in the countryside but substantial urban development throughout the catchment - including Pinner, Ruislip and Ickenham.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Ampney Brook at Ampney St Peter
stationDescription: Flat V weir, 1:10 cross-slope. Theoretical calibration confirmed by gaugings. Drowning unlikely (but d/s stilling well installed). Full range. Realistic late-2000 flow pattern (rare amongst Cotswold stations). Primarily natural baseflow-dominated regime but gw abstraction lower down the Churn catchment is likely to have an impact. Contributing area > topographical catchment. Station is located just d/s of a fish farm (now closed).
catchmentDescription: The Ampney Brook is a dip-slope stream draining the pervious Great Oolite of the Cotswolds;. A rural catchment with small, scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Swill Brook at Oaksey
stationDescription: Flat V weir installed in 2002 (with US installed more recently to improve measurement when FV drowned). Superseded unreliable Electromagnetic gauge. Some bypassing at high flows over 2.2 cumecs. Essentially natural, responsive, flow regime but runoff may be influenced by gw pumping from the confined Oolite aquifer. Large range of velocities; zero flow during most summers, monitoring of the lowest flow rates is suspect. Early flow record is patchy. Not a primary gauging station.
catchmentDescription: Relatively flat, rural catchment given over to agriculture. Primarily impervious (Oxford Clay).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Aldbourne at Ramsbury
stationDescription: Two Flat V weirs - 1:10 cross-slopes (one is located on a bypass stream). Theoretical calibration. All flows contained. Sensibly natural baseflow-dominated flow regime. Flows 3/8/2000-28/10/2000 modelled by EA. Stilling well froze in Nov/Dec 2010; late-winter and spring flows under review. Some flows in Nov and Dec 2011 were estimated due to equipment failure.
catchmentDescription: The Aldbourne drains a Chalk downland catchment (very modest Drift cover). Land use: predominantly agricultural - Aldbourne is the only significant settlement.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Misbourne at Denham Lodge
stationDescription: Crump weir (crest: 3.5m wide) plus Flat V (width: 2.0m, 1:10 cross-slope) on small distributary. High flow range under review but drowning rare. Bypassed only in exceptional floods. Baseflow dominated Chalk stream, influent in some reaches (e.g. near the Chalfonts). Runoff substantially diminished by PWS abstractions; partially counteracted by alleviation of low flow scheme from 1992. Topographical CA confirmed as 94.8 sq.km (Gw catchment estimated at 81 sq.km.)
catchmentDescription: Elongated dip-slope catchment in the Chilterns (Chalk with appreciable Drift cover). Significant urban growth in valley but catchment is largely Green Belt - agriculture with scattered tracts of woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Kennet at Newbury
stationDescription: Ultrasonic gauging station - six pairs of transducers in cross-path configuration. Relatively high velocities. Full range. New instrument kiosk installed in 1996 (to improve reliability in hot weather). Sensibly natural baseflow dominated flow regime but possible small impact of West Berkshire Groundwater Scheme during drought conditions.
catchmentDescription: Principally a Chalk catchment but small tracts of low permeability above the station. Mainly rural (arable and mixed farming) with some urban growth concentrated along the valley.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Mole at Esher
stationDescription: New ultrasonic commissioned in 2001. Full range capability and improved reliability. Replaced earlier US just u/s (in a reach created as part of the Mole Flood alleviation scheme); debris and siltation (blocking transducers) and high temperature gradients caused substantial malfunction particularly at low flows. Limited stage range, d/s gates control levels. Responsive regime. Some sewage effluent derives from outside the catchment.
catchmentDescription: A largely impervious catchment divided by the W-E trending ridge of the North Downs (Chalk and Upper Greensand). Diverse land use - rural tracts and considerable suburban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Thame at Wheatley
stationDescription: Multi-path ultrasonic (cross- configuration). Skewed flow, weed-growth and summer temperature gradients can limit accuracy particularly at low flows (e.g. early autumn 2009) but continuous record post-1990. Full range, railway embankment ensures no bypassing; c80 cumecs gauged in 01/2003. Supersedes 39038 (u/s). Some spring flow contribution but responsive regime. Net import of water; sewage effluent is a sig. component of low flows.
catchmentDescription: The Thame drains a typical clay (principally Oxford Clay, some Greensand also) vale NW of the Chiltern escarpment. Land use: largely agricultural (considerable grassland); Aylesbury is the major town.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Hogsmill at Ewell
stationDescription: Rectangular flume; stage-discharge relation confirmed by c/m gaugings. Modular. Overfall u/s. Debris on u/s grill can influence levels. Grounding of float (stilling well floor corresponds to throat level of flume) can affect low flow accuracy. Frequently vandalised. Patchy flow record. Mean runoff suggests that topographical catchment greatly exceeds the contributing area. Station downgraded in 2010 to level only.
catchmentDescription: A largely suburban catchment developed mostly on Chalk (dip-slope of North Downs).
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Churn at Perrott's Brook
stationDescription: Flat V weir, 6 metres wide, 1:10 cross-slope. Full range - tailwater levels measured to facilitate flow computation in the non-modular range (but further gaugings required to check the reduction factor). Data missing for Nov/Dec 2000 (when previous maximum would have been exceeded). Extreme flow in July 2007. Baseflow dominant. Zero flow in severe drought conditions.
catchmentDescription: Permeable (Oolitic L'st) catchment on the dip-slope of the Cotswolds. Land use: mostly agricultural, some woodland and scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Coln at Fossebridge
stationDescription: Crump-profile crest, 7.8m wide (constructed on original overfall). Modular until high flows but submerged in floods as flow is restricted by a downstream culvert and construction under a downstream bridge; some bypassing also - intermittent flow in small bypass channel not measured. Baseflow-dominated regime. 39020 and 39110 are d/s.
catchmentDescription: A rural, dip-slope, catchment developed on the Oolitic L'st of the Cotswolds.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Coln at Fairford
stationDescription: Electromagnetic gauging station (buried coil). C/m calibration to be completed. Station performance generally good to bankfull (flows unreliable during exceptional floods (e.g. during extreme July 2007 event). Reasonable velocities are maintained at low flows (0.5 cumecs is a typical drought minimum) - but flows may be overestimated by around 10%. Baseflow-dominated regime. Levels affected by STW.
catchmentDescription: Linear catchment on the dip-slope of the Cotswolds (Oolitic Limestone). Rural with scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Thames at Staines
stationDescription: Ultrasonic gauging station - multi-path (6) in cross configuration; transducers located in piles set in the channel. Excellent agreement between multi-meter check gaugings and US calibration. Levels affected by gate and lock movements. In hot weather temperature gradients can deflect beams. Major PWS abstractions u/s.
catchmentDescription: Scarp and vale topography. Diverse geology. Predominantly rural headwaters contrast with substantial urban and suburban development in the lower reaches.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Letcombe Brook at Arabellas Lake
stationDescription: Flat V weir at outfall of the lake. Baseflow-dominated regime of the Letcombe Brook is heavily influenced by gw abstraction; flow augmentation scheme in operation. Zero flow during notable droughts.
catchmentDescription: Catchment area derived from the Digital Terrain Model. The Letcombe Brook is fed from springs issuing from the scarp slope of the Lambourn Downs (Chalk, Drift free); contributing area is very rural.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Manor Farm Brook at Letcombe Regis
stationDescription: Flat V weir. Flow augmented to counteract impact of gw abstractions (external to the topographical catchment). Spring outflow; interludes of zero flow in most years.
catchmentDescription: The Manor Farm Brook is fed by spring flow issuing from the scarp slope of the Lambourn Downs (Chalk). A very rural catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Pang at Frilsham
stationDescription: Flat V weir on ephemeral stretch of the Pang. Floods exceed bankfull and weedgrowth causes occasional drowning (flows adjusted but high submergence ratios limit accuracy). Monitors progress of ALF programme (headwater abstractions - currently much diminished - having substantially reduced runoff in the past). Baseflow dominated regime; effluent can form a significant component of low flows.
catchmentDescription: A largely permeable (Chalk with modest Drift cover) rural catchment; mostly arable and pasture with significant woodland on the Tertiary outcrops (in the south).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Pang at Bucklebury
stationDescription: Flat V weir. All flows contained but weed growth causes drowning - flows adjusted but high submergence ratios limit accuracy (e.g. May/June 1995 when flows unreliable). Monitors progress of ALF programme (headwater abstractions substantially reduced runoff but abstractions greatly reduced since 1992). Flows affected by STW effluent and, rarely, by West Berkshire Groundwater Scheme.
catchmentDescription: A very rural and largely permeable (Chalk with modest Drift cover)) catchment with significant areas of woodland on the Tertiary outcrops.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Sulham Brook at Sulham
stationDescription: Flat V weir. U/s and d/s levels monitored; modest d/s gradient and limited channel capacity/siltation causes structure to drown for substantial periods. Relatively responsive regime. Topographical catchment is substantially less than the true contributing area; runoff (which is reduced by gw abstraction) is therefore unrepresentative - in wet years runoff can greatly exceed rainfall. Levels/flows affected by STW until the mid-1990s (when effluent diverted).
catchmentDescription: A very rural catchment developed mostly on Chalk (with substantial Drift cover); some woodland on the Tertiary outcrops.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Wey at Alton
stationDescription: Flat V weir (1:10) cross-slope. U/s and d/s levels monitored. Weir lowered in 2002; drowning now occurs more frequently. Baseflow dominated regime - the upper Wey is ephemeral. Topographic catchment differs from groundwater contributing area. Significant abstractions in the catchment.
catchmentDescription: Mostly Chalk (with significant Drift cover); Upper Greensand outcrops in the east. Alton is the only large settlement; land use is largely pasture/arable.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Wey at Kings Pond (Alton)
stationDescription: Rectangular thin-plate weir at outfall from lake. Theoretical rating. Debris obstructing u/s grill can cause minor fluctuations in level. Flow data for Apr-Jul 1995 unreliable due to weed growth. Baseflow-dominated regime (zero flow during notable droughts) but some local urban runoff. Topographic catchment differs from groundwater contributing area.
catchmentDescription: Mostly Chalk (with significant Drift cover) and Upper Greensand. Rural (apart from Alton).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Caker Stream at Alton
stationDescription: Flat V weir. U/s and d/s levels monitored. Weedgrowth controlled by regular maintenance. Ephemeral stream but relatively responsive. Groundwater-dominated regime; contributing area substantially less than the topographical catchment: unrepresentative mean annual runoff.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology (largely Chalk with substantial Drift cover). A rural catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Thames at Walton
stationDescription: Ultrasonic gauging station, multi-path, cross configuration. Gaugings confirm calibration. In hot weather the gauge can malfunction (due to temperature gradients in the measuring section). No routine naturalisation - therefore d/s Kingston (39001) series is more hydrologically representative).
catchmentDescription: Scarp and vale topography developed on diverse geology. Predominately rural headwaters contrast with considerable suburban growth in the lower valley.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Cranleigh Waters at Bramley
stationDescription: Multi-path cross-configuration ultrasonic gauging station beneath road bridge; enlarged bridge opening creates large capacity but, correspondingly, low velocities; these can impact on low flow precision. Full range station. Notable Nov. 2000 peak under review. Flashy response with lengthy periods of very low flow. Low flows influenced by effluent returns. Station is used for flood monitoring purposes.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly impervious Weald Clay with some Lower Greensand in lower reaches. A largely rural catchment (south of Guildford) centred on Cranleigh.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Blackwater at Farnborough
stationDescription: Electromagnetic station installed as part of a R&D project into flow in two stage channels. Calibration to be completed. Gaugings indicate that EM flows are overestimated in the low and medium range. 9/97 to 9/98 flows particularly suspect but all flows should be treated with caution. EM decommissioned Feb 2009, side-look US installed.
catchmentDescription: Tertiary geology - mainly Bagshot Beds with London Clay in the headwaters and alluvium in the valley. Substantial and expanding urban development including Farnborough and Aldershot but large rural tracts remain; significant areas of heathland and woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Ver at Redbourn
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (4.0m wide). U/s and d/s level measurements are routinely made - drowns but d/s reach regularly maintained by dredging to improve modularity. Gauging station initially constructed to monitor low flows. Groundwater-fed ephemeral stream. Runoff reduced by gw abstraction - which has greatly diminished since May 1993 (the Ver is included in the EA's Alleviation of Low Flows Programme).
catchmentDescription: A predominantly rural catchment on the dip-slope of the Chilterns (Chalk with significant Drift cover); land-use mainly arable and pasture. Part of Redbourn is immediately above the station.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Red at Redbourn
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (4.0m wide) which frequently drowns. U/s and d/s measurements are routinely made. D/s reach regularly maintained by dredging which improves modularity. Gauging station initially constructed to monitor low flows. Flow data for Feb 1995 unreliable due to weed growth. Groundwater-fed ephemeral stream (but can be responsive).
catchmentDescription: A predominantly rural catchment on the dip-slope of the Chilterns (Chalk); land-use is mainly arable/pasture; some urban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Misbourne at Little Missenden
stationDescription: Rectangular thin-plate weir with flanking Crump crests. Theoretical rating supported by gaugings (more scheduled to confirm calibration). Screen u/s of weir requires regular maintenance. Very rare, and minor, bypassing during extreme flood conditions (e.g. Feb. 2001). Instrumentation difficulties over the 12/2006 - 5/2007 period, data under review. Heavy (but declining) gw pumping in the headwaters has reduced base flows (the Misbourne is included in the EA Low Flow Alleviation Programme). The Misbourne is a Chalk stream; ephemeral, and influent in some reaches.
catchmentDescription: A mostly rural catchment on the dip-slope of the Chilterns (Chalk); some development in the lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Bourne (South) at Addlestone
stationDescription: Velocity-area station - low level sharp-crested weir (rather insensitive with poor approach conditions. Control passes to road bridge at medium/high flows. Some confirmatory gaugings up to approx. 10 cumecs. Possible bypassing into R. Wey system (via canal) at very high flows. Patchy early record (when no back-up recorder). Rating change in Oct 1993 (earlier data may need reprocessing). Responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Geology: largely impermeable Bagshot Beds. Very mixed land use with woodland and heath plus significant urban development.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Thames at Farmoor
stationDescription: Multi-path cross-configuration ultrasonic gauging station d/s of intake for Farmoor Res. Drought flow accuracy uncertain (v. low velocities) and gaugings suggest that flows may be overestimated by around 5%. Naturalised flows not routinely calculated. Substantial left-bank by-passing (not via the Thames itself) under flood conditions. Levels affected by d/s gate movements, abstractions and lockages.
catchmentDescription: Catchment is predominantly rural u/s of the station. Geology: mixed - pervious headwaters (Oolitic l'st), Oxford Clay in the lower reaches.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Thames at Reading
stationDescription: Multi-path cross-configuration ultrasonic gauging station sited beneath Reading Bridge (incorporates adjustment for flows outside the transducer piers), very minor bypassing via Gosbrook stream. Good c/m confirmation of calibration throughout the range; doppler gauging on 4/1/2003 at 300-310 cumecs. River levels affected by d/s weir and lock.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology with Cotswold headwaters (Oolitic L'st) and Oxford Clay. Land use: predominantly rural but with important, and growing, urban development in the valley (i.e. Oxford and Reading).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Brent at Costons Lane Greenford
stationDescription: Flat V weir with vertical wing walls (bankfull: 2m). Lowest flow recording station on the Brent. Flow does not incorporate the d/s Greenford tributary. The Welsh Harp reservoir has significant impact on the flow regime.
catchmentDescription: A heavily urbanised (>50%)catchment in north west London, developed on London Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Ravensbourne East at Bromley South
stationDescription: Flat V weir (1:10 cross slope) with vertical wing walls. Full range station, which has been theoretically rated. Further gaugings needed to confirm rating. Superseded (in 1992) an earlier structure. Straight approach with no significant modification to the very responsive flow regime.
catchmentDescription: The Ravensbourne rises as Chalk springs (in Holwood Park; dry valleys extend into the North Downs). The lower catchment is mainly impervious Eocene deposits. Below the headwaters the catchment is heavily urbanised (south London).
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Quaggy River at Chinbrook Meadows
stationDescription: Flat V weir installed 2002 (large fall d/s implies full modular range); replaced (with marginal increase in catchment area) rectangular critical depth flume (flood walls added May 1982) 220m u/s. Original station subject to considerable gravel accretion. Sensibly continuous flow record. Responsive regime. No significant artificial influences.
catchmentDescription: Geology: London Clay, also Reading/Woolwich Beds. A linear urban/suburban catchment in south London.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Yeading West at Gutteridge Wood
stationDescription: Rectangular flume. Flow data in January 2003 under review - minimum appears unrealistically low.
catchmentDescription: Largely impervious suburban catchment in NW London.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London

name: Loddon at Twyford
stationDescription: Two side-looking ADCP devices installed since 2010 located within two rectangular road bridge culverts. Previously, data was supplied by a multi-path ultrasonic (cross configuration) located 200m downstream on gently curving reach spanning the total length of the lower stages of the river. Full range. No flows recorded 2006-2010 due to removal of ultrasonic equipment, installation of ADCPs and development of index velocity rating. Ultrasonics affected by power supply and instrumentation problems (e.g. siltation caused difficulties with the lowest of the six flight paths) causing some record gaps. MAF easily contained. U/s spillage (floodplain storage rather than bypassing) begins around 60 cumecs.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology and land use. Chalk headwaters, mainly London Clay below. Large rural tracts but substantial urban development, in the eastern catchment particularly.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Ray at Islip
stationDescription: Multi-path cross-configuration ultrasonic gauging station (in two-channel section). Good hydrometric performance but very low velocities in low flow conditions. Only minor disturbances to the natural (and responsive) flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Relatively flat impermeable (Oxford Clay) catchment given over largely to agriculture (but includes Bicester); station is just below Otmoor.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Wey at Guildford
stationDescription: Multi-path ultrasonic gauging station (cross configuration); upgraded in 2003. Installed below a road bridge in the centre of Guilford where the river and navigation channels are coincident. All but extremely rare flows contained. Responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology and land use.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames

name: Windrush at Bourton on the Water
stationDescription: Crump Weir. U/s and d/s levels monitored; structure rarely drowns but submerged during exceptional floods. Baseflow-dominated regime; significant groundwater abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Station sited close to the edge of the Oolitic L'st outcrop. A largely permeable catchment, very rural in character.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Dikler at Bourton on the Water
stationDescription: Crump Weir. All flows contained, no bypassing but weir submerged during floods. Substantial baseflow but responsive to rainfall.  Significant groundwater abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology: Oolites and Lias. Rural catchment with very limited urban development (but station is just below Bourton).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Sor at Bodicote
stationDescription: Crump Weir (u/s and d/s levels monitored); replaces Adderbury (39051) which closed in April 1988. Significant abstraction u/s - but only operates when flows > 0.16 cumecs. Peak of April 1998 flood estimated at around 17 cumecs. Appreciable baseflow but also very responsive to rainfall.
catchmentDescription: Primarily an impermeable (Middle Lias) catchment - rural in character.
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Yeading Brook East at Western Avenue A40
stationDescription: Flat V weir from 1974, removed in 1981. Replaced in Oct 1985. EA hold records from water year 1988. Rebuilt in 1993, NRFA holds records from 1995. Very responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: A heavily urbanised catchment in west London developed in London Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Hertfordshire and North London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Mill Brook at Blewbury
stationDescription: Flat V weir. Thames Water abstraction u/s. Flow constraint 40 Megalitres/day. Groundwater catchment substantially exceeds the topographical catchment; runoff totals are unrealistic. Stable regime - the Mill Brook is spring-fed.
catchmentDescription: Rural catchment developed on the Chalk (but Blewbury village is immediately u/s).
operator: Environment Agency - West Thames
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Wendover Springs at Wendover
stationDescription: Thin-plate weir (0.91m wide) monitoring outflow from the Wendover Springs (into the Wendover Arm of the Grand Union Canal). Algal/weed growth on and u/s of the crest can affect levels - overestimating runoff. Station commissioned in the 1962, processed flows from 1989 (microfilmed level charts for pre-1989 period). Complemented by historical monthly series 1841-1897; method of flow measurement uncertain but runoff patterns consistent with rainfall. Earliest extant gauging station in the UK - provides unique insight into spring flow variability in the 19th century.
catchmentDescription: The Wendover Springs issue from the scarp slope of the Chilterns - Chalk (with a limited amount of Drift cover). Land use is mostly woodland and pasture but significant urban growth near the gauging station.
operator: Canal & River Trust
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Medway at Weir Wood Reservoir
stationDescription: Trapezoidal critical depth flume with low flow notch - measures compensation and overflow from Weir Wood Reservoir.
operator: Southern Water
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Darwell at Darwell Reservoir
stationDescription: Compound thin-plate weir for low flows. High flows monitored 70m downstream at rectangular critical depth flume. Measures outflows from Darwell Reservoir - storage augmented by pumping from River Rother.
operator: Southern Water
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Medway at Teston / East Farleigh
stationDescription: Crump weir plus sharp-crested weir (on flood gate); insensitive, but less so than previous broad-crested weir. Flows > ~25 cumecs measured 2km downstream at East Farleigh (226 cumecs gauged in Oct 2000) - but some hysteresis problems; reprocessing anticipated. Water levels maintained for navigation purposes. Patchy record 1966-73. Combination of flows from Teston and East Farleigh may not be complete on National River Flow Archive. Springs provide some baseflow but a responsive regime. Bypassed by navigation lock; possibly no account of significance of bypassing during busy summer periods. Levels fluctuate quickly, influenced by pumping, and locks create pulsing effect, though undetectable in gauged daily flows. Complex water utilisation; low flow augmentation from Bewl Water (via River Teise); Weir Wood and Bough Beech Reservoir also in the headwaters; >20 years of naturalised flows available.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology; impervious formations constitute up to 50% of the catchment. Diverse land use with significant areas of woodland and orchard; several substantial towns also (including East Grinstead and Tunbridge Wells).
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Rother at Udiam
stationDescription: Flat V weir with - since Dec 2000 - multi-path ultrasonic to extend the range (calibration is ongoing); since 2007, ultrasonic no longer working. Flat V superseded original Flat V (1992-2000) that was insensitive at low flows; this replaced shallow broad crested weir (1961-1992). Part of weir crest missing (since 2009); plans to repair crest. Cableway (65 cumecs gauged in Nov 2000). 1992-2000 flows truncated at ~5.9 cumecs (downstream tidal sluice limited modularity of the Flat V); significant underestimation of runoff - flows under review. Truncation of flows due to lack of extended rating. Prior to 1992, broad-crested weir (crest deformation suspected, low flows may be overestimated). Period of record minimum should be considered indicative only; similar minimum in 1990. Flow confined (at Udiam) except in extreme floods when river goes out of bank upstream and downstream of weir. Responsive regime with evident artificial influences. Offtake for Darwell Reservoir (9.6 km2) upstream; other reservoirs and sewage effluent (some imported) also influence flow patterns (small net export of water). Robertsbridge flood alleviation scheme commissioned in 2003.
catchmentDescription: Catchment developed mainly on clays of Wadhurst series (very limited permeability), substantial tracts of Ashdown Sands also. Rural with sig. woodland and scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Beult at Stilebridge
stationDescription: Flat V (with crest & downstream tappings), commissioned in 2003, with full-range, single-path ultrasonics for high flows (calibration to be completed) in long and reasonably straight reach. Structure limit ~6 cumecs. Replaced compound structure - central flume separated, by short divide piers, from broad-crested flanking sections. Calibration: model tests and cableway gaugings (79 cumecs gauged during Oct 2000 flood), though cableway no longer in place. Right bank flood banking constructed to confine most flows, but water now seeps through into adjacent field due to rabbit burrowing. Out-of-bank flows can be significant but accounted for in rating. Possible backing-up from Medway during exceptional floods. High flows required to flush out weed growth. Stepping boards in place on upstream bridge in summer employed to maintain levels; their insertion and removal managed separately so time of adjustment of levels unknown. Minor baseflow (from the Lower Greensand) but very responsive regime. Small overall impact of artificial influences - very variable agricultural abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Weald Clay (but includes some pervious sandstones). Rural (arable and grassland with significant woodland) with scattered settlements throughout the catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Bourne at Hadlow
stationDescription: Electromagnetic gauge (installed Jul 2002) located in straight reach. Exceeds capacity (8.5 cumecs) of previous trapezoidal critical depth flume; theoretical rating with some confirmatory gaugings but drowns at high flows - flow record often incomplete (Foot & Mouth restrictions in spring 2001). 1968 flood estimated at 57 cumecs on basis of wrack marks. Very substantial baseflow contribution but capable of flashy response. Some artificial regulation from mill upstream. Significant spray irrigation.
catchmentDescription: A largely rural catchment (with a substantial acreage devoted to orchards) developed on very mixed geology - pervious formations cover a little over half the catchment; rapid runoff from the Weald Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Medway at Chafford / Colliersland Bridge
stationDescription: Humped trapezoidal flume (capacity 8.5 cumecs) plus rated section 0.8km downstream at Colliers Land Bridge; gaugings above bankfull included in the calibration. Channel subject to erosion. Bypassing occurs in major flood events e.g. 1960, 2000; stilling well extended after later event (to prevent 'flat-top' hydrographs). Quite a responsive regime despite significant baseflow. Catchment includes Weir Wood Res. (which provides compensation flows). Small net export. Sluices upstream can influence levels.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mixed but mainly Ashdown Sands and Wadhurst Clay. The Medway drains from Ashdown Forest and the catchment is predominantly rural in character but with a number of towns close to the headwaters.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Great Stour at Wye
stationDescription: Crump weir (width 7.61m) - drowns at <3 cumecs (no flow adjustment) - Velocity-Area (VA) station (just downstream) for high flows. Data reprocessed in 2010; summer 2007 high flows significantly modified, 2000 flows added. 1976 drought minimum (lowest flow on record) estimated. Weedgrowth can cause overestimation of flows. Ashford effluent is a significant component of low flows; small net import of water. Flood retention reservoirs above Ashford (constructed 1990-92). Hydrographs show evidence of upstream mill sluice operation (declining).
catchmentDescription: The east and west branches of the Stour flow over impermeable (mainly) Weald Clay; below Ashford (the only major settlement) Chalk predominates. A rural catchment with mixed land use.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Teise at Stonebridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir (commissioned Sep 1995), 7m wide, superseded broad-crested weir (crest width: 5.95m; weir capacity: ~3 cumecs) in trapezoidal section; record gap in 1994-95 relates to construction of Flat V weir. Weed growth can cause problems and no high flow gaugings pre-1995. Crest tapping no longer used. Cableway at site - calibration now extends throughout the flow range (but less reliable above bankfull); gauging of 54 cumecs after Oct 2000 peak. Significant baseflow but also responsive. Bewl Water Reservoir offtake is ~1km upstream. Augmentation (from Bewl Water) dominates post-1975 low flow patterns; gauged daily flow minimum (0.033 cumecs on 16/02/2005) resulted from significant upstream abstraction. Isolated and anomalous low flows (e.g. 2004) reflect influence of abstractions for Bewl Water. Station used as control point for Bewl, data inspected regularly by Southern Water. Also some regulation from Hope Mill Sluice ~3.2km upstream.
catchmentDescription: A largely rural catchment with scattered settlements developed on the sands and clay of the Wealden Series.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Eden at Penshurst / Vexour Bridge
stationDescription: Combined series: Crump profile weir (crest width: 4.877m) at Penshurst measures flows up to ~4 cumecs, combined with flows above this threshold measured at Vexour Bridge (3km upstream, NGR: TQ510455). Also, ultrasonics installed in 2003 to quantify hysteresis effects due to water impoundment at the Leigh Barrier (13 km downstream). Post-1995 low and high flows combined (with adjustment for catchment area) but combination of earlier gauged daily flows is incomplete. Rating well defined and all but exceptional flows contained. Offtake for Bough Beech Reservoir is upstream - small net export of water. Also some slight regulation afforded by Hever Lake.
catchmentDescription: A rural catchment with scattered settlements developed on sands and clays of the Wealden Series - impervious types predominate but many spring sources in the permeable headwaters.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Great Stour at Horton
stationDescription: Broad-crested weir (width: 10.55m, insensitive) in trapezoidal section plus a Velocity-Area section for flows >20 cumecs. Theoretical rating endorsed by gaugings. (Electromagnetic gauge installed 1992 - some technical difficulties, now rarely used). No wing walls but all flows contained by sloping side bunds. Substantial baseflow but relatively flashy storm response. Very modest net impact of artificial influences on runoff. Minor public water supply and irrigation abstractions in lower valley - some counterbalancing due to small amounts of effluent (from outside catchment). Flood storage reservoirs above Ashford (constructed 1990-92). Upstream mill regulation evident in hydrographs.
catchmentDescription: The east and west branches of the Stour flow over Weald Clay; below the confluence (at Ashford - the only significant urban area). Geology: Chalk dominates - but with appreciable Drift cover. A rural catchment with mixed land use.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Darent at Hawley
stationDescription: Crump weir (7.62m broad). Modular to bankfull. Crest width may be restricted during periods of low flow to increase sensitivity. Large structure capacity (30 cumecs) but bypassed (right bank) during extreme flood of Sep 1968 (flow estimated at ~50 cumecs). Influent upstream; flows commonly lower than at Lullingstone (40018). Sluices and abstractions to and returns from gravel workings upstream affect flow - evident at low flow (e.g. Oct 1989). Baseflows were greatly reduced by increasing groundwater abstractions, but the Darent Augmentation Scheme (part of Augmentation of Low Flows (ALF) programme) now operates, can involve water imported from outside catchment. Augmentation pumps affected flows 08/08/2007 to 04/12/2007.
catchmentDescription: A mainly pervious (Chalk and Upper Greensand) catchment with moderate Drift cover and some sand/clay; predominantly rural with some expanding urban centres.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Darent at Otford
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir (crests: 3.04m and 2 x 2.286m broad) with crest tapping in a straight reach. Structure width exceeds natural channel width, in the past this has caused upstream accretion. Superseded original Velocity-Area station in 1969. Modular rating only (gaugings indicate overestimation at high flows; structure drowns completely before bankfull). Station bypassed during floods and upstream accretion / bank encroachment is significant. Baseflow-dominated regime but responsive to storms. September 1968 floods estimated at 23 cumecs. Upstream groundwater abstractions, which showed a substantial historical increase, have been reduced - helping increase flows during dry periods. Upstream gravel pits can moderate flood flows.
catchmentDescription: A mainly pervious (Chalk) catchment with considerable areas of Gault and Lower Greensand upstream of station. Predominantly rural with some expanding urban centres including Sevenoaks and Westerham.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Wingham at Durlock
stationDescription: 120 degree V-notch weir; capacity of notch ~0.09 cumecs. Flows should be treated with caution: all but a few early peaks truncated. Some recent high flows set to missing. Theoretical rating. Drowns for extended periods - poorly maintained downstream channel subject to blockage after high flows. Site suffers from weed and silt drowning out the weir during the summer months (e.g. 2008). Sewage effluent is a very minor flow component. Topographical catchment substantially exceeds the contributing area.
catchmentDescription: Mainly Chalk - overlain in parts by Drift - plus Tertiary deposits; baseflows derive from the Thanet Sands. Predominantly rural embracing the village of Ash.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: White Drain at Fairbrook Farm
stationDescription: Trapezoidal critical depth flume designed for modular operation. Principally a low flow station. Occasional overtopping onto wide alluvial floodplain. Flume tends to silt up, also blocking of stilling intake (e.g. in autumn 2000) - impacting on computed flows. Calibration limit ~2.7 cumecs. High flow event in Oct 2000 under review. Runoff diminished due to groundwater abstraction from the Chalk. Sewage effluent - discharging 1km upstream - formed a major component of low flows until 1991 (treatment now undertaken outside catchment).
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology: Chalk and Lower London Tertiaries provide baseflow, surface runoff from the London Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Cray at Crayford
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound Crump profile weir (crests: 3.052m and 7.612m broad). Modular limit ~6 cumecs. Significant bypassing rare. Weed growth can be a problem. Significant structural instability is an issue; low flows since 2007 and high flows since 2005 subject to considerable uncertainty. Major baseflow component but responsive to storms. Flows are substantially affected by artificial influences; surface and groundwater abstractions, stormwater overflows and extensive local gravel workings. Considerable net export of water.
catchmentDescription: A mainly pervious (Chalk) catchment; the Cray flows in an alluvial tract which has been widely exploited for gravel/sand. Urban land use is extensive and increasing particularly in the lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Dudwell at Burwash
stationDescription: Crump profile weir (crest: 4.88m) in straight reach, rating review performed in 2003; few gaugings at high flows. Trash may gather in energy dissipator. Steep banks contain all but exceptional flows. Wide and flat floodplain. Bypassing at stages >1m; maximum recorded stage significantly above bankfull. Some truncation of peaks around modular limit (~6 cumecs). Responsive and essentially natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: A relatively steep catchment draining from the High Weald. Geology: Ashdown Sands (about 80% - variable permeability) and Purbeck Beds (about 20%). A largely rural catchment with significant woodland and scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Darent at Lullingstone
stationDescription: Three-bay, non-standard broad-crested weir (total crest width: 11m) at outfall of an ornamental lake (hence stage range is limited); stop boards fitted, in the past, to increase low flow sensitivity. Large downstream fall ensures modularity. Calibration based on gaugings. Wind fetch across lake may possibly affect levels. 1968 flood greatly exceeded subsequent maxima. Significant period of missing data from Feb - Nov 2009 attributed to re-design and re-build of gauging station. Baseflow-dominated regime. Runoff diminished by major groundwater abstractions, but Augmentation Scheme now operates (borehole upstream); Darent is influent downstream. Some water meadows upstream (e.g. at Shoreham); sewage outflows from Sevenoaks now piped direct to Dartford.
catchmentDescription: Catchment is predominantly pervious (Chalk). Mixed land use: agricultural with woodland plus expanding urban centres in the headwaters (including Sevenoaks).
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Eridge Stream at Hendal Bridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir (width: 5.6m) superseded velocity-area station (data: 1973-83). Kink at base of weir. Significant u/s accretion. Theoretically rated, high flow rating not defined. Modular limit ~1.9m. Many peaks truncated at around 17 cumecs. Patchy record prior to 1986. Chart recorder only. Responsive regime; goes out-of-bank fairly easily, particularly over right bank. Runoff increased by effluent returns. Station is one of control points for Medway barrage.
catchmentDescription: Catchment drains Wealden area of Sussex/Kent, encompassing Hastings Beds, Tunbridge Wells Sand, Wadhurst Clay and Ashdown Beds. Rural, mainly agricultural land use.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Hexden Channel at Hopemill Bridge Sandhurst
stationDescription: Trapezoidal critical depth flume. Structure capacity 2.25 cumecs, channel capacity is considerably greater but bypassing on left bank. Few check gaugings available. Some truncation of peaks at ~5-7 cumecs. December 2003 peak flow under review.
catchmentDescription: A relatively narrow catchment trending SE from headwaters in the High Weald (source is in Bedgebury Forest). Rural land use - about 20% woodland - developed on Wadhurst Clay (about 50%) and the overlying Tunbridge Wells Sands.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Great Stour at Chart Leacon
stationDescription: Flat V weir (7.96m broad; shallow approach depth - modular limit ~0.22m) superseded - in 1979 - a velocity-area station (level records from 1967). Theoretical rating, Velocity-Area calibration for high flows - but wide uncertainty band; site is not appropriate for high flow measurement (backwater effects from downstream road bridge). Some upstream regulation (mill sluice operation, Hothfield flood retention reservoir) otherwise very little artificial disturbance to the responsive flow regime.
catchmentDescription: A largely impervious catchment (but some baseflow from the North Downs) trending southeast towards Ashford. Rural with scattered settlements - urban development near to station.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: East Stour at South Willesborough
stationDescription: Flat V weir, 1:10 cross-slope, superseded a Velocity-Area station (affected by weedgrowth) in 1976. Theoretical rating - to 5.3 cumecs (under review). Improved downstream conditions have increased modular range. Almost all flows contained but flood alleviation scheme truncates peaks. Mill sluice operation and, from 1990, flood retention reservoirs provide a degree of regulation. Runoff increased by sewage effluent.
catchmentDescription: A rural catchment, developed mostly on clay. Significant land use change from the late 1980s (industrial / commercial development, by-pass etc) above Ashford.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Bartley Mill St at Bartley Mill
stationDescription: Broad-crested weir with low flow notch. All flows contained. Station discontinued in 1981 but recommissioned in late 1990s. Sensibly natural flow regime - responsive but with significant natural baseflow support.
catchmentDescription: A rural catchment in the Weald developed principally on Tunbridge Wells Sands and - in the headwaters - Wadhurst Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Brede at Brede Pumping Station
stationDescription: Flume. Calibrated to 3 cumecs; low confidence is flows extrapolated above this threshold due to relatively few check gaugings. Responsive regime. Powermill reservoir and abstraction borehole impact on the water balance.
catchmentDescription: Small and largely impermeable catchment draining west to east. Mixed land use with significant woodland and scattered settlements; Battle is in the western headwaters.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London

name: Sarre Penn at Calcott
stationDescription: Ultrasonic station since 2006; superseded Flat-V (glass-fibre) weir, 1:10 cross-slope. Owned by Mid Kent Water. Downstream channel control at high flows. Theoretical rating - confirmed by gaugings in low and medium ranges, zero flows possible. Chart recorder only. Decommissioned in 1994, re-opened in 2006. All but exceptional flows contained, peak flows truncated at ~1.7 cumecs (maximum processed stage). Patchy flow record prior to 1980. Natural regime.
catchmentDescription: A mostly rural catchment developed on impervious Tertiary formations (London Clay predominates).
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Len at Lenside
stationDescription: Flat V weir, theoretically rated. Chart recorder only. Most flows contained but most peaks truncated at ~3.8 cumecs. Dec 2002 maximum generated by notable storm but flow is indicative only. Reliable springs sustain flows through summer months. Effluent returns from Leeds sewage treatment works have major impact on flow pattern.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Lower Greensand, Folkestone and Hythe Beds. Predominantly rural land use, although urbanised in lower catchment near confluence with the Medway.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Dour at Crabble Mill
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (1:20 cross-slope, capacity: 1 cumec) within concrete berms in brick-lined section, almost all flows contained. Modular. Theoretical rating - extends to 1.59 cumecs; exceeded in Feb/Mar 1995 and in early 2001, when outstanding flows (confirmed by current metering 03/02/2001) reflect record groundwater levels and substantial urban runoff following >40mm storm. Velocity-Area station prior to 1984. Initially chart recorder only (now telemetry) also ornamental lakes upstream. Reprocessing of high flow data carried out in May 2007 following rating review by Peter Brett Associates. Data estimated for Jun - Aug 1995. Spot gauging of 0.06 cumecs on 05/10/1976 represents good approximation for 1976 drought minimum. Runoff reduced by substantial groundwater abstraction. Baseflow-dominated regime but urban fraction of catchment very responsive to notable storms.
catchmentDescription: The Dour is a spring-fed Chalk stream (two main branches) draining to Dover. Rural headwaters but significant urban development in the lower valley above Crabble Mill.
operator: Environment Agency - Kent and South London
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Nunningham Stream at Tilley Bridge
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir. From May 1981 to November 2005, thin plate weir (insensitive) used in summer to overcome drowning of flume and to increase depth for 'wet fencing' upstream. Thin plate removed in winter and weir used exclusively. Changeover day between winter and summer ratings varied by year; thin plate no longer used. Early flow records unreliable. Frequency of drowning reduced following downstream channel improvements - under non-modular conditions flows estimated using 41002. Since November 2005, thin plate weir not been installed during summer months for environmental reasons and because check gaugings suggest drowning no longer occurs. Currently, weir and corresponding rating in place all year round; accuracy of weir verified in summer. Many peaks truncated at ~9 cumces; lack of high flow rating at this site. Structure and channel choked by vegetation, extending downstream when flow not sufficient to flush vegetation through. Essentially natural responsive regime, although sewage treatment works upstream. Groundwater augmentation during droughts (e.g. 1989/90).
catchmentDescription: Varied topography developed on Hastings Beds - some permeable strata (Ashdown Sands). Land use is mainly arable with considerable woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: R: Regulation from surface water and/or ground water.

name: Ash Bourne at Hammer Wood Bridge
stationDescription: Compound thin-plate weir with compound critical depth flume (with minor tilting). Plate weir used in summer (maintains depth for 'wet fencing' upstream); insensitive and flow pattern can be erratic due to raising / lowering of plate. Frequency of drowning reduced following downstream channel improvements in 1953. Highest flows exceed the structure calibration; few check gaugings available. Limited storage in Ashburnham Lake. Minor net effect of abstractions and discharges but significant groundwater augmentation (from Ashdown Sands) in most summers - evidently artificial low flow pattern.
catchmentDescription: A mainly impervious catchment (Wadhurst Clay) of rural character, with considerable woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Cuckmere at Sherman Bridge
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (width 10m, cross-slope 1:10), high flows from Velocity-Area station upstream at Arlington (high flows formerly measured at now-abandoned open channel site ~100m downstream of weir). All but exceptional flows contained, but peaks truncated at ~27 cumecs - common in 2000. Modular limit ~6 cumecs, flows >10 cumecs unreliable; river goes out of bank across adjacent fields at high flows. New upstream cableway provides capability to upgrade high-flow rating; very few check gaugings currently available. Prior to 1994: compound broad-crested weir (no flows 29/09/1992 - 03/03/1994) - drowned regularly (tidal influence); flows then assessed using fall-discharge method (adjustment discontinued 1981 - peaks truncated at ~5 cumecs until ~1990). Responsive flow pattern. Limited net impact of variations but Arlington pumped storage reservoir upstream, for which there is an abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Relatively narrow catchment developed on mixed geology (mainly Hastings Beds and Gault Clay). Primarily a rural catchment with significant areas of woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Ouse at Barcombe Mills
stationDescription: In 1994, a four-path ultrasonic gauge superseded (except for minimum flows) complex structure (weirs and sluices) - subject to drowning - sluice operation further complicates the derivation of discharges. Water utilisation in the catchment is complex; major abstraction located immediately upstream and Ardingly reservoir (2km upstream) provides regulation. Ultrasonics moved upstream of abstraction intake in 1999. High flow measurement problems remain; goes out of bank at high flows.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mixed - Hastings Beds (mainly permeable) predominate. A largely rural catchment with substantial woodland and scattered urban centres.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Ouse at Gold Bridge
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (crest: 10m wide, cross slope 1:10) commissioned July 1992, superseded a compound short-crested semi-circular weir (width 10.7m). Cableway for higher flows. Modular limit 8-10 cumecs. All but exceptional floods contained - no overtopping of flood banks in Oct 2000; 85 cumecs current-meter gauging following peak, and site has many check gaugings available. But complex combination of drowning and circulatory effects (associated with inflows from a ditch carrying flows culverted under the A272) may occur at highest flows. Full flow record reprocessed in 2003. Data estimated for Jun - Jul 1983 and for Sep 1993. Releases from Ardingly reservoir (from 1978) have a substantial impact on low flows. Sewage treatment works and flood retention structures also have a limited influence on the responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology with substantial permeable outcrops - particularly the Tunbridge Wells Sands. Diverse land use - chiefly rural with significant woodland but some urban centres.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Uck at Isfield
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (width 7.62m; 1:10 cross-slope) with cableway, crest tapping (not currently instrumented) and some non-standard elements (e.g. experimental fish-ladder,) constructed (in 1999) between the abutments of a pre-existing Crump weir (with crest tapping and capacity of ~50 cumecs). Both original and existing weirs exhibit mid-range drowning (~10-30 cumecs). Cableway also installed in 1999 - improved high flow capability; lots of check gaugings available. Station is well sited downstream of railway embankment with large capacity bridge opening. All but very exceptional floods contained (by-passing commences at head of ~2.4m). Flow record reprocessed in 2003. No substantial abstractions but discharge from sewage treatment works (and operation of Uckfield Mill flood gates) can produce abrupt flow changes. Very responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Catchment geology is very mixed; Hastings Beds predominate. Above Isfield the catchment is rural with significant areas of woodland, but the Uckfield urban area is expanding.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Rother at Hardham
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir and flood gate (installed in 1982) superseded a broad-crested weir. Lower Crump section acts as fish pass and flood gate is rarely used. Water can back-up from downstream confluence of Rother and Arun, causing station to drown at 16 cumecs or less (dependent on tide level); flows often truncated around this modular limit (e.g. 2000s). River also goes out of bank over adjacent fields at such flows. High flows extrapolated from Velocity-Area station at Fittleworth ~3km upstream. Critical site for low flows. Full combination of Crump weir and VA flows awaited. No data on National River Flow Archive 1977-98. Abstractions for for public water supply (offtake is immediately upstream); minor spray irrigation impact.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Adur W Branch at Hatterell Bridge
stationDescription: Three-bay rectangular critical-depth structure; flanking sections (each 2.16m wide) can be closed to concentrate flow in the central bay (0.864m). Stop-board removal can produce odd flow patterns. Structure non-modular at ~5 cumecs; few high flow gaugings. Prior to 2003, two different ratings (summer and winter) were applied; all data since 2003 should be considered highly suspect, with flows under review. Flood flows bypass the structure across adjacent fields due to shallow river bank. Almost all flood flows truncated at ~11 cumecs (very common in 2000-02). Sensibly natural - and very responsive - flow regime.
catchmentDescription: A rural catchment developed principally on impervious formations - Weald Clay dominates.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Rother at Iping Mill
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir (crests: 3.05m and 2 x 5.03m broad). Bankfull: 1.9m above crest. Non-modular during high flows (conveyance affected by growth of trees obstructing a downstream bridge; downstream siltation may also be a factor). New ultrasonics downstream have high flow measurement capability. Bypassing (via mill channel) during exceptional floods. Station overtopped in 1968 flood, peak flow estimated at 100-150 cumecs; 1968 is largest high flow event on record, significantly greater than late 2000 maxima when all flows were contained with no overtopping or bypassing. Many springs provide a large baseflow component but the Rother is very responsive to rainfall. Abstractions for spray irrigation and sewage treatment works discharges affect the flow regime, particularly low flows - but limited impact overall. The Rother is influent above Iping Mill.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology; 60% pervious - large tracts of Lower Greensand. A mainly rural catchment with some urban development.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Adur E Branch at Sakeham
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir (crests: 1.219m and 2 x 2.438m, right-hand crest is 0.01m higher than left-hand crest). Crest tapping (seldom used) removed in 1993-94 when weir crest and wing-walls replaced. Flows rarely corrected for frequent drowning due to downstream channel conditions (including sluices); high submergence ratios. Flows on 13/08/2004 and for Aug-Sep 2007 recession under review. Responsive regime. No substantial abstractions, small net import of water; Burgess Hill sewage effluent has substantial impact on low flows.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology - permeable headwaters but predominantly Weald Clay in lower reaches. Largely rural lower catchment but significant urban growth along the eastern catchment boundary (including Cuckfield, Burgess Hill and Haywards Heath).
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Huggletts Stream at Henley Bridge
stationDescription: Compound thin-plate weir with compound critical depth flume for higher flows. Downstream dredging in 1952 facilitated modular operation (earlier data suspect) but intermittent drowning still factor. Responsive, essentially natural, flow regime but some groundwater augmentation (from Ashdown Sands) during droughts (e.g. 1989/90). Few check gaugings available. Stilling well cleared on 19/09/2007; flows under review.
catchmentDescription: Huggletts Stream flows south from the main High Weald drainage divide. A rural catchment developed mostly on impervious formations - extensive tracts of Wadhurst Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Arun at Pallingham
stationDescription: Flat-V weir (installed in 1994) with (from 2002) ultrasonics for high flows (calibration ongoing). Superseded an insensitive broad-crested weir (15m wide), with 0.03m fall along crest due to settlement. Limited gaugings to confirm rating. Despite cableway installation, velocity-area measurement is restricted by effects of tidal lag at high flows. Tides also cause drowning. All but exceptional floods contained (overtopped by 1974 flood) but most post-1979 peak flows truncated at ~55 cumecs. From 2009, flows truncated at 5.56 cumecs due to poor performance above this level; more gaugings required before rating can be extended. Flows comparable to the 1973 minimum occurred in several years. The Arun has a relatively natural, and very responsive, regime.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly impervious (largely Weald Clay and Lower Greensand) catchment. Land use: mixed - basically rural, with substantial woodland. Growing urban fraction especially around Horsham.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Ems at Westbourne
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound Crump profile weir; crests: 0.61m (showing effects of erosion) and 4.12m broad. Theoretical rating. Modular throughout flow range. Differential drawdown can affect river level measurement. All flows contained but flows exceeded structure limit for significant periods in late 2000. Baseflow-dominated regime. Significant net export of water from the catchment (groundwater abstractions) but low flows augmented by compensation water (from borehole).
catchmentDescription: The Ems - which is ephemeral over much of its length - is a Chalk stream draining the South Downs; Drift cover is minimal. A rural catchment with significant woodland and scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Cuckmere at Cowbeech
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound Crump profile weir (crests: 2.13m and 2.97m broad) with crest tapping - not currently used. Theoretical rating - supported by gaugings up to 0.6m stage, although only very few check gaugings available. Very limited head during droughts. Weir drowns at ~6 cumecs. Structure capacity exceeded in large floods, but good quality high flow data. Early data (1939-67) is of poorer quality and relates to low flows only; some data have been infilled. Responsive to rainfall on impervious fraction of catchment. Sewage treatment works discharges upstream, but earlier surface and groundwater abstractions have ceased.
catchmentDescription: A rural catchment (with a significant urban fraction in the headwaters) developed on mixed geology (Hastings Beds predominate).
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Combe Haven at Crowhurst
stationDescription: Station level-only from 2004, due to bypassing and access issues. Compound Crump profile weir (crests: 2.44m and 2 x 2.13m broad) subject to frequent drowning. Full range station. Poor differentiation between low flows over lengthy periods (repeated sequences of 0.02 cumecs being common). Responsive regime. Earliest data less reliable due to subsidence of the weir.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology and land use. Mainly impervious formations (Wadhurst Clay) but with significant areas of Tunbridge Wells and Ashdown Sands. A predominantly rural catchment with some urban centres.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Kird at Tanyards
stationDescription: Station decommissioned in 2001. Crump profile weir (8.7m broad - fall of 0.012m along crest due to settlement) with crest tapping - not currently used. Structure is insensitive and subject to drowning at low flows. Very minor impact of artificial influences on the exceptionally flashy flow regime.
catchmentDescription: An impervious (Weald Clay) catchment given over to agriculture; some extensive woodland tracts.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Arun at Alfoldean
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound Crump profile weir (crests: 4.0m and 6.0m broad). Crest tapping not currently used; over-estimation of high flows (structure drowns frequently); but 1996-2002 flows truncated at ~15 cumecs. 1983 minima under review (those of Aug 1976 look more realistic). Stilling well leakage can influence water levels. Limited impact of artificial influences on responsive flow regime - small net augmentation due to sewage effluent, which can significantly affect low flow patterns.
catchmentDescription: Principally an impervious (Weald Clay) catchment - mainly rural but includes Horsham.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Bevern Stream at Clappers Bridge
stationDescription: Original crump profile weir (crest: 6.0m broad, rather insensitive) in straight reach of realigned channel; altered by insertion of low flow crump weir (0.5m wide) in centre of structure in Oct 2005. Modular limit 0.6-0.9m stage. Crest tapping on original structure not used since 1981; original crump thought to be first weir on this site, although evidence for two previous structures in locality. Low flow crump weir 0.521m below level of main crump, generating substantial fall below station; two sets of baffles (with notches in sides for fish passage) reduce scour on downstream side of weir. Since Jan 2007, two cross-path ultrasonics in place. Rating for new structure being developed (as of Nov 2009); 2005-12 flows derived from combination of rated structure and ultrasonic, with flows between Aug 2005 and Dec 2007 (inclusive) under review. Since 2012, all flow data from this site derived using solely ultrasonic. Bypassing occurs readily at stage >1.15m as river on higher ground than surrounding land; water flows through ditch d/s of Clappers Bridge (100m upstream), overtops banks upstream of weir, and overtops abutments at gauging station. However, since redevelopment in Oct 2005, no bypassing observed during 12 instances with stage >1.15m. Stream channel narrows downstream of structure, exacerbated in summer by weed growth. Negligible impact of artificial influences on responsive flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Primarily an impervious (Weald Clay) catchment but north-flowing tributaries from South Downs provide a significant baseflow. A rural catchment with considerable woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Clayhill Stream at Old Ship
stationDescription: Crump profile weir (crest: 3.0m broad) with crest tapping - structure drowns at around 2 cumecs. Insensitive at low flows (algal growth on crest can influence stage). Theoretical rating. Possible bypassing at notably high flows. Some (sporadic) early flow data available (at Southern Water) from 1955. Ephemeral river; zero flows common in dry years.
catchmentDescription: The Clayhill stream is ephemeral and drains an impervious (Weald Clay) catchment. Land use: almost exclusively rural with considerable woodland and scattered settlements.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Lod at Halfway Bridge
stationDescription: Crump profile weir (crest: 7.05m broad) with crest tapping (easily blocked, not currently used but when in operation reduction factors of 0.8 assessed for high flows). All but highest flows modular. Rating confirmed to 10 cumecs by gaugings but footbridge can control flows above ~40 cumecs. Some bypassing in exceptional floods. Data estimated for Nov 2000 - Jan 2001. Minor flow regulation associated with upstream mill. Very responsive regime. Flows are sensibly natural - small net export of water due to groundwater abstraction.
catchmentDescription: Primarily an impervious catchment with Weald Clay more extensive than pervious Lower Greensand. Steep topography in upper catchment. Rural with considerable woodland.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Lavant at Graylingwell
stationDescription: Flat-V weir; crest breadth 5m. Cross-slope 1:10, maximum head 1m. Weir capacity is 6 cumecs, bypassing during extreme events (e.g. Jan 1994 and Nov 2000 when peaks were ~8 cumecs - based on current-meter gaugings). Severe weed growth can cause structure to drown. Extended periods with zero flow. Baseflow-dominated regime but spring outflows can increase dramatically following exceptional rainfall. Runoff is substantially reduced by groundwater abstraction.
catchmentDescription: The Lavant is an ephemeral stream draining the dip-slope of the South Downs (Chalk). A permeable catchment - sparsely populated in the headwaters. Land use: agricultural with significant woodland; some urban development close to Graylingwell.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Shell Brook at Shell Brook
stationDescription: Flat-V weir, 1:10 cross slope, from summer 1995, modified from the existing Crump profile weir (crest: 4.0m broad), station level remains unchanged. Runoff pattern changed fundamentally following the construction of Ardingly Reservoir (1978) immediately upstream. Presence of reservoir poses challenges for quality controlling data and for gauging.
catchmentDescription: Catchment is mainly permeable Hastings Beds with Wadhurst Clay in the valley. A rural, heavily wooded basin.
operator: Environment Agency - Solent and South Downs

name: Loxwood Stream at Drungewick
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound Crump profile weir (crests: 2.0m and 4.0m broad) with crest tapping. Full range - all but extreme flows contained; bunds rise from the wing walls to give station capacity of ~57cumecs. Rated up to 149 cumecs at 3.62mALD although structure drowns at ~0.7m, invalidating rating on occasion (may result from construction of an aqueduct downstream; high levels in the Arun can also be a factor). Loxwood Stream susceptible to siltation, e.g. in 2005, when May-Oct flows are suspect. Annual minimum (and 2004-06 drought minimum) in Oct 2005 accurate owing to flushing of stilling well.
