operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Severn at Haw Bridge
stationDescription: Velocity-area station at B4213 road bridge, downstream of Warwickshire Avon inflow. Tidally affected - flows do not account for this, but cross path US gauge u/s at Deerhurst (54110, installed Dec 1995) measures tidal flow to bankfull. Hysteresis observed during high flow events. July 2007 peak exceeded limits of rating curve by a large margin, all flows for event estimated using National Flow Forecasting System hydrodynamic model. Heavily regulated/modified flows; compensation flows from Llyn Clywedog and the Shropshire Groundwater Scheme, with PWS exports (to Liverpool, West Midlands and Gloucestershire) and effluent returns (chiefly to Avon, Stour (Worcs.) and Severn at Worcester).
catchmentDescription: Very large, diverse catchment, lowest on the Severn. Paleozoic slates in Welsh headwaters, Permo-Triassic sediments in middle reaches and Jurassic and Liassic clays in the Avon basin.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Stoke Park Brook at Stoke Park
stationDescription: Flat V weir, closed in 1978. Part of Shropshire Groundwater Scheme network.
catchmentDescription: Agricultural, low relief catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Allford Brook at Allford
stationDescription: Flat V weir, closed in 1979. Part of Shropshire Groundwater Scheme network.
catchmentDescription: Agricultural, low relief catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Potford Brook at Sandyford Bridge
stationDescription: Prefabricated Flat V Crump profile weir, initially installed for the Shropshire Groundwater investigation. Site closed 1979-86 and repositioned 1987 to avoid backing up. Low flows may be significantly affected when the Severn augmentation is in operation. Baseflow dominated but can be responsive.
catchmentDescription: Flat catchment on predominantly Sherwood S'st overlain by intermittent Boulder Clay and glacial sands and gravel.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: G: Runoff influenced by groundwater abstraction and/or recharge.

name: Hodnet Brook at Hodnet
stationDescription: Flat V weir, closed in 1977. Part of Shropshire Groundwater Scheme network.
catchmentDescription: Agricultural, low relief catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Stoke Brook at Stoke on Tern
stationDescription: Prefabricated Flat V Crump profile weir, 1.6m wide, installed for the Shropshire Groundwater Investigation. Base flow affected by gw pumping.
catchmentDescription: Agricultural catchment with argillaceous rock of the Lower and Middle Lias in the headwaters and mixed Permian and Triassic Sandstones within the lower catchment. Overlain by intermittent Boulder Clay and glacial sands and gravels.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Stour (Worcs) at Prestwood Hospital
stationDescription: Original secondary velocity-area station replaced in 1995 with a Flat V Crump profile weir, 11m wide, 1:20 cross slopes, wing walls at 0.9m in a good reach. Contained on rb, possibly bypassed on lb behind the hut. Gw for PWS significantly augments low flows as effluent. Pre-2000 minima were significantly higher - causation under review. Magnitude of July 2007 flood event subject to considerable overestimation - flows under review.
catchmentDescription: A largely urban catchment (approx. 40%), draining the western suburbs of Birmingham. Underlying geology predominantly argillaceous and sandstone rocks of the Carboniferous with Triassic Sandstones within the lower third of the catchment
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Roden at Stanton
stationDescription: Velocity-area station, closed in 1979. Part of Shropshire Groundwater Scheme network.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Triassic Mudstones and Lower Lias. Blanketed extensively by Boulder Clay and morainic sand and gravel.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Platt Brook at Platt
stationDescription: Prefabricated Flat V Crump profile weir, 2m wide, installed for Shropshire Groundwater Investigation. Very limited artificial disturbances to the natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Flat catchment on predominantly Permian Sandstones overlain by Boulder Clay and glacial sands and gravel.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Smestow Brook at Swindon
stationDescription: Velocity-area station, closed in 1978.
catchmentDescription: Low relief. Significant urban area (Wolverhampton). Predominantly Triassic Sandstones.
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Springs Brook at Lower Hordley
stationDescription: Flat V weir, closed in 1978. Part of the Shropshire Groundwater Scheme network.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: War Brook at Walford
stationDescription: Prefabricated Flat V Crump profile weir, 2m wide, installed for Shropshire Groundwater Investigation. Very limited impact of artificial influences on the flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Very flat catchment with meres puddled on peat and Boulder Clay. Some glacial sands and gravels.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Severn at Dolwen
stationDescription: Informal station (NRFA data: 1977-83, 86-2000) replaced by compound triangular profile (1:2, 1:8) weir in 1999-2000, crests 7.1m and 12.0m (total), wing walls at 1.6m. Designed to permit fish movements and counting (non-standard profile due to fish sensors on downstream face of weir). Cableway approx 60m for floodplain flows. 10km d/s of Clywedog reservoir so control patterns dominant at low flow.
catchmentDescription: A high relief, wet and responsive catchment on Silurian shales, mudstones and slates with Boulder Clay on valley sides. Land use is pasture, forest and moorland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Clywedog at Bryntail
stationDescription: Flat V weir, 16m wide; 1:20 cross-slope, immediately below Clywedog reservoir. Installed for the purpose of measuring compensation and regulation releases. Reservoir drawn down over winter months to moderate flood peaks.
catchmentDescription: Steep, wet catchment draining Ordovician and Silurian shales and slates. Substantially Drift free. Most tributaries afforested on valley sides.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Crow Brook at Horton
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir, 8m wide; cross-slope 1:10, installed for Shropshire Groundwater Investigation. June 1982 peak under review.
catchmentDescription: Catchment of moderate relief whose headwaters are in Telford and drain glacial sand and gravel overlying Permian Sandstones. Lower reaches clad in Boulder Clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Cannop Brook at Parkend
stationDescription: Crump profile weir, 3m wide, 1m wingwalls, set in a wider channel. Closed between 3/86 and 2/92. Substantially natural flow regime (but sewage effluent from Parkend). Similar minimum flows in 1982 and 1983.
catchmentDescription: Moderately steep catchment entirely within the Forest of Dean; drains the Upper Coal Measures - principally s'st with thin shale and coal horizons with little Drift cover.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Cannop Brook at Cannop Cross
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir, 2m wide; cross-slope 1:10. Natural, responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Small, moderately steep, afforested catchment in the Forest of Dean; drains Upper Carboniferous s'sts.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Allford Brook at Childs Ercall
stationDescription: 90 degree Vee notch with side contractions set in an access chamber to a culverted section. Standard and tipping bucket raingauges adjacent. Close to a Shropshire GW scheme abstraction point. Station closed 26/01/12 due to problems calibrating the rating caused by H&S issues gauging at site.
catchmentDescription: Agricultural catchment on Bunter Sandstone overlain by Boulder Clay in the lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Little Avon at Berkeley Kennels
stationDescription: Velocity-area station replaced, in 2000, earlier gauging station which suffered from afflux from ornamental bridge upstream. New site is 10m upstream, of the bridge, in rectangular concrete channel; gauged from road bridge. Rating is stable for low to medium flows. High flows are influenced by flood gates downstream, which are in place to cope with the large tidal range of the River Severn and extreme events. Moderate influence from public water supply abstractions and spray irrigation. Daily mean flow data missing from June 2002 to January 2004.
catchmentDescription: Responsive catchment with steep headwaters which drain complex sequence of limestones, sandstones and clays of Lower and Middle Jurassic; flat Vale of Berkeley is underlain by Cambrian inlier, Keuper Marl and Lias clays. Land-use: rural.
operator: Environment Agency - Wessex

name: Avon at Bredon
stationDescription: Ultrasonic single path instrument installed in 1979, unsuccessful experiment. Replaced in 1988 by multipath cross configuration US in broad reach. Flows are valid to bankfull (but US has under-measured, data being reassessed). River inundates hams on lb extensively during floods. For very high flows use 54002. Extensive flow modification by abstractions and returns. Responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Large catchment of low relief, draining argillaceous rocks almost exclusively. Contains some large towns but chief land use is agriculture.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Tanllwyth at Tanllwyth Flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed to gauge streams which have supercritical flow regimes. U/s sediment trap for sediment yield studies assists in maintaining performance. IH (now CEH) research catchment nested within 54022. Responsive natural flow regime. Primary 15 minute dataset available.
catchmentDescription: Steep and very wet (~2500mm). Mature coniferous plantations cover catchment. Geology: Silurian shales and grits. Rainfall, evaporation and soil moisture estimates also available from dense monitoring network.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Severn at Hafren Flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed to gauge streams which have supercritical flow regimes. Drowning may occur following big floods owing to sediment deposited d/s.  IH (now CEH) research catchment nested within 54022. Primary 15 minute dataset available. Responsive, natural regime.
catchmentDescription: Upper third has rounded, peat moorland headwaters; lower two thirds has steep, incised valleys with mature conifer plantations. Geology: Palaeozoic shales, grits and mudstones. Very wet (>2400mm). Rainfall, evaporation and soil moisture estimates also available from dense monitoring network.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Hore at Hore Flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed to gauge streams which have supercritical flow regimes. Drowning may occur following big floods owing to sediment deposited d/s. Natural, IH (now CEH) research catchment nested within 54022. Primary 15 minute dataset available.
catchmentDescription: Very wet (c2500mm) with steep gradients. Highest and lateral fringes of catchment are peat moorland. The remaining 78% had mature coniferous forest until 1985, when the lower two thirds was clear felled. Replanting has taken place. Geology: Palaeozoic grits, shales and mudstones. Very steep gradients, ~2500mm rain. Rainfall, evaporation and soil moisture estimates also available from dense monitoring network.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Strine at Crudgington
stationDescription: Electromagnetic gauge, using a bubbler device for level measurement, in trapezoidal channel. Very low velocities experienced. Replaced poor weed-affected open channel site in 1981. Early record not available. Substantial modification to flow regime from WRW discharges.
catchmentDescription: Very flat catchment draining Weald Moors. Headwaters dominated by argillaceous Carboniferous rocks. Lower reaches mostly Permian Sandstone overlain by mixed surperficial deposits. Urban areas include Newport and the northern part of Telford.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Severn at Buildwas
stationDescription: Multiple US cross-configuration gauging station. Flows fully contained by Buildwas Bridge and floodbanks. Following refurbishment of the ultrasonic gauge in 2005, only lower racks working untill March 2008, possible over-estimation of high flows under review. Substantial modifications of lowest flows by Clywedog and Vyrnwy Reservoirs and Shropshire Groundwater Scheme; otherwise artificial effects modest.
catchmentDescription: Large diverse catchment. Geology largely mixed Palaeozoic formations with moorland and forestry land use in the wet headwaters. Permian Sandstones dominate in the lower reaches with extensive Drift cover of Boulder Clay and sands and gravels and mixed farming landuse.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Hadley Brook at Wards Bridge
stationDescription: Crump profile Flat V (1:10) weir set in low 0.5m vertical wingwalls. Straight approach. Very responsive; out of structure flows could be gauged from an u/s bridge. Significant reduction in peak flows across period of record as a result of rating review in 2010. Monitors compensation flows from a compensation borehole as substantial gw abstraction in the catchment.
catchmentDescription: Low to moderate relief catchment principally on Mercia Mudstone; Headwaters have Sherwood S'st outcrops. Wholly rural with mixed farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Hore at Upper Hore flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed to gauge streams which have supercritical flow regimes. Drowning may occur following big floods owing to sediment deposited d/s. Natural catchment nested within 54022. Researchers should note the availability of the primary 15 minute dataset. Missing data Feb-March 2001 due to access restrictions because of Foot & Mouth.
catchmentDescription: The highest and southern lateral fringes of catchment are peat moorland. Remaining 70% has mature coniferous forest planted in 1958 and 1960. Geology: Palaeozoic grits and shales. Very steep gradients, ~2500mm rain. Rainfall, evaporation and soil moisture estimates also available from dense monitoring network.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Cam at Cambridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir, 6m wide with 2.295m wing walls and integral access bridge. Subject to accretion u/s. Owned and built by British Waterways but now operated by the EA. No dmfs 17/06 - 08/07/98 - gauge house rebuilding. June and July 2007 high flows extrapolated beyond upper limit of rating curve. Cam acts as important feeder for Gloucester and Sharpness Canal. Relatively unresponsive flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Drains Cotswold escarpment. Geology predominantly argillaceous Lias formations. Overlain by limited drift deposits. Contains the town of Cam.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Coley Brook at Coley Mill
stationDescription: Rectangular thin-plate weir 1.9m wide with concrete side contractions, set in existing brick lined channel. Non standard approach (sharp bend u/s). Weir drowns at high flows. Flows affected by u/s PWS abstractions and spray irrigation. High baseflow component.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Triassic Sandstones overlain by Boulder Clay and sands and gravels within the lower reaches. Land use is mainly arable farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Lonco Brook at Whitleyford Bridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir, opened in 2002.
catchmentDescription: Agricultural, low relief catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Avon at Lilbourne
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Stour (Warks) at Shipston
stationDescription: Station flooded during July 2007, high flows beyond upper limit of rating curve.
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Severn at Deerhurst
stationDescription: Cross path ultrasonic gauge designed to measure low to medium flows where tides affect the flow regime. Upgrade of ultrasonic gauge started June 2007. High flow and gauging site at Haw Bridge (54057) but prone to overestimate flows during tidal phases - leading to significant differences in flow series during tidal phases and dropouts in flow. Deerhurst more accurate at low flows. Unable to infill gaps during 2011 due to ultrasonic gauge fault. Substantial modification to flow owing to PWS exports and effluent returns (chiefly to the Stour, Worcester, Avon and Thames).
catchmentDescription: Very large, diverse catchment, Paleozoic slates in Welsh headwaters, Permo-Triassic sediments in middle reaches and Jurassic and Liassic clays from the Avon catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Avon at Rugby
stationDescription: Velocity-area station opened in 1973. Early record subject to negative flows and poor rating. Datum reset in 1981. Flood alleviation scheme built in 1980s led to gauge house being relocated and river channel being lowered by approx 0.5 m - causing significant shift in rating curve. Bankfull as 6mASD but river rises from main channel at 1.6mASD. Flows only calculated since Jan 1990 and subject to high uncertainty due to weed growth problems, insufficient gaugings for rating, and vandalism. Due to site issues the NRFA does not hold data from this site.
catchmentDescription: Large artificial open channel site with steep reinforced banks as part of flood defence works. Located in a mainly agricultural catchment on Lower Lias. Recent and progressive constriction of industrial estates may have possible impact on flows in recent years. PWS at two points above the site and canal abstraction in the Upper Avon and River Swift.
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Avon at Warwick
stationDescription: Responsive regime with evident artificial influences.
catchmentDescription: Low relief catchment. Mostly agricultural, but containing Rugby, Coventry, Leamington Spa.
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Stour (Worcs) at Kidderminster Gilt Edge
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Arrow at Broom
operator: Environment Agency - Staffordshire Warwickshire and West Midlands

name: Wye at Cadora
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Wye at Belmont
stationDescription: Channel control velocity-area station, width at bankfull approx. 49m; cableway span 62m. Embankment built on the left extends flood containment. Severe weed growth problems. Extrapolated flows probably under-estimated as rating does not take account of floodplain flow. Originally, stages taken from 1908 at Hereford, 1.2km d/s; flows were measured at current site. Prior to 1932, data unreliable. Highest flood peak believed to have been in Oct 1998 (estimated at c.700 cumecs in modelling work by WS Atkins) but this is subject to ongoing investigation. Moderate flow modification, high levels of agricultural abstraction u/s. Naturalised discharges take into account reservoirs in the Elan Valley.
catchmentDescription: Above Erwood (55007) are wet uplands draining impermeable Palaeozoic rocks; the lower third of the catchment is a narrow corridor draining ORS marls and subordinate glacial gravels, which supports arable farming. Upper catchment is predominantly grassland with some forest.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Lugg at Lugwardine
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with cableway. No records pre-1953. Peaks unreliable 1953-1965. Station for flood warning purposes only from Jul 1982 to Dec 1989. Reinstated as flow monitoring station from Jan 1990. Recent peaks truncated at 32.1 cumecs - rating limit (overbank flows spill onto wide floodplain). Excessive weed growth in summer months requires regular clearance and gravel movement is also an issue requiring maintenance - anecdotal evidence indicates this could be an issue at low flows. Following a rating review in 2013 flows for the period 01/03/1981 onwards have been reprocessed - note this results in a significant drop in flows during the 1995 drought.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Lower ORS (marl dominated) producing subdued relief - Herefordshire Plain. Fluvioglacial deposits near main channel worked for sand and gravel production.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Irfon at Abernant
stationDescription: Velocity area station with cableway. Valuable long record but downgraded, in 1982, to flood warning only.
catchmentDescription: Located on edge of upland area, steep valley sides rising to plateau level between 450m and 600m. Underlain by Ordovician strata. Landuse: extensive sheep grazing and forestry.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Wye at Rhayader
stationDescription: Velocity area station. Replaced by Ddol Farm (055026) in Oct 1969.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Elan at Caban Coch Reservoir
stationDescription: Compensation flows gauged by two circular orifices about 36m below the dam. Overspill measured by using the dam cill as a broad-crested weir 172.5m wide. Siphon gauge operates during first 4 months of the year if not overspilling. Direct abstraction to Birmingham. Long naturalised series available. Superseded by Caban Dam gauge (55032).
catchmentDescription: Very wet (>1800mm), high relief catchment draining predominantly Silurian shales and slates. Forestry and moorland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Wye at Erwood
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with a massive rock bar as a control. Bankfull width approx. 64m, cableway span 81m. All but the highest flows (which spread slightly onto narrow terrace on rb) contained. POR max.(taken from the HiFlows database) in Dec 1960 is under review - considerable rating extrapolation involved. In Aug 2012, flows back to 15/08/1999 reprocessed due to rating change, particularly affecting low flows which are difficult to gauge - and rate - due to uneven rocky bed. Extreme low flow periods in Jun and Aug 2004, Jul and Aug 2006, and May and Sep 2007 use old rating until improved low flow rating can be developed. Substantial flow modification from regulation and abstraction from the Elan, PWS and sewage effluent. Some naturalised sequences available.
catchmentDescription: Large wet upland catchment draining impermeable rocks - metamorphosed Palaeozoic sediments and an igneous complex. Summit levels exceed 600m. Predominantly (70%) grassland catchment with extensive grazing, moorland, and patches of forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Wye at Cefn Brwyn
stationDescription: 3-bay Crump profile weir (no divide piers), divide plates installed 1962; concrete piers built 1969, low crest 2.43m broad, high crests total 9.13m broad. Very steep channel, u/s accretion needs regular clearing. Treat early record with caution. Operated as an IH (now CEH) experimental basin since 1968 (15 minute flow data plus extensive hydrometeorological database). 1973 flood generated by 100mm storm. 11/9 - 8/10/97 dmfs est. from subcatchment whilst repairs to structure. Natural and very responsive flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Small, high relief, very wet (>2000mm) catchment, grassland on peat overlying weather resistant Silurian slates and shales.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Monnow at Kentchurch
stationDescription: Velocity area station. Replaced by Grosmont (055029) in Apr 1972.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Wye at Pant Mawr
stationDescription: Velocity area station. Informal Flat V weir control from 1973. Flood warning only from Jul 1982. Station in headwaters of Wye catchment.
catchmentDescription: The catchment has underlying Silurian strata which are resistant to weathering. Mostly forestry and grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Ithon at Llandewi
stationDescription: Velocity area station. Flood warning station from 1982.
catchmentDescription: Upper reaches of catchment underlain by Silurian Wenlock beds. Extensive sheep pasture.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Irfon at Cilmery
stationDescription: Velocity-area station, initially with a gravel shoal control, improved in 1979 by installing a 25m wide Crump profile Flat V weir. Weir rebuilt between 31/8/94 and 7/9/94, no data available. Cableway spans 44m. Above about 3m the rb floodplain is inundated. Natural regime. A major event in October 1998 altered the shape of the channel at low flows.  Flows since 23 October 1998 reprocessed in January 2013 due to new rating, resulting in minor decreases to daily means from this date (almost unnoticeable in 2000, 2001 and 2011), and to monthly maxima (relatively large decreases in 1998 and 1999).
catchmentDescription: Headwaters drain the very wet Tywi Forest (underlain by indurated Ordovician sediments). The middle and lower reaches are on relatively more permeable Silurian rocks, but generally a responsive catchment of very low permeability. Predominantly upland pasture (60% grassland) but with 25% forest, mainly in western headwaters.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Arrow at Titley Mill
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Low flow control is a stable riffle; otherwise a three-bay road bridge 50m d/s is the control. Cableway span is 21m. Gets out of bank but not bypassed. Intake pipes silted up during summer of 1994, treat data with caution. Anomalous step changes (e.g. Sept 1975, Sept 1991, March - May 1992) under review. Summer 1974 (July - Sept) low flows should be treated with caution; minima overestimated due to stilling well problem. Data estimated for July - Sept 1990 and June - July 1995. Natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Headwaters of moderate relief, draining durable Silurian slates and shales; otherwise, catchment underlain by ORS marls. Generally low permeability. Predominantly (70%) grassland, with sheep grazing on upland plateau and some patches of arable and forest in lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Lugg at Byton
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir, 1:20 cross-slopes, 12.5m wide. Cableway span 21m. Before 1970 a stable riffle was the control. Above 2m left bank overtopped. Right bank is steep cliff. Some shoaling u/s. Oct 1998 peak highest on record although subject to considerable uncertainty as extrapolated and out-of-bank. Estimated at 70 cumecs by WS Atkins using slope-area method. Flow moderately modified by abstractions for PWS.
catchmentDescription: Headwaters drain Radnor Forest - underlain by Silurian bedrock. Impermeable formations are covered by extensive alluvial gravel deposits in the valleys. This aquifer provides significant baseflow and moderates flood peaks. Predominantly grassland (70%) catchment supporting rough grazing, with 10% forestry.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Honddu at Tafolog
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Natural river section. Low flow control is a small rock bar; otherwise Tafalog road bridge 70m d/s is the control. Non-standard control means flows should be viewed with caution. First station with flat V weir opened 55m d/s of current site in 1954. Highest flow of 68.5 cumecs occurred in Oct 1960, before start of NRFA record (but listed on Hiflows-UK database). Weir deteriorated after this flood and station was moved u/s to current location in 1974. Station downgraded to level-only for flood warning in July 1982 and gauging ceased in July 1986. Station closed August 1982 to January 1992. Gauging restarted in 2005 and station status upgraded in 2010, with flows from 2005. Natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Located in high upland area of the Monnow catchment, underlain by the Breconian sequence of the Lower ORS strata. Mostly forestry and sheep grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ithon at Disserth
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir, 1:20 cross-slope, 18m wide, replaced velocity-area station with channel control in 1972. Cableway span 27.5m. Very high floods may inundate the rb. Data estimated for Oct 1972 and for April 1980. Weir rebuilt Aug 1995; no data available, but late Aug low flow deemed reliable. Missing data in 2001 due to access restrictions during Foot and Mouth outbreak. Suspect data arising from drift correction removed (13/07-5/08 2001). In Nov 2010, flows since 25 Aug 1995 reprocessed, reducing some high flows overestimated by previous rating. Natural flow regime. Station is a flood warning station.
catchmentDescription: Catchment drains rocks of very low permeability: Upper and western catchment drains Ordovician and Silurian shales; igneous complex in the SE. Predominantly (80% grassland, with high moorland and extensive forestry plantations on the higher ground. Noticeably reduced rainfall compared to the u/s Wye and Irfon catchments.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Chwefru at Carreg-y-wen
stationDescription: Natural river section with Flat V weir (date of installation vague - Nov 1972 or Feb 1974). Left bank is high and steep. Extensive floodplain on right above 2.2m.  Variable gravel shoals affect the low flow rating. Gabions were installed on each bank to maintain uniform flow, but deposition occurs on rb and main flow on left. Regime thought to be natural.  Station discontinued 1982.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment on low permeability geology. Predominantly rough grassland and moorland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Frome at Yarkhill
stationDescription: Velocity-area station using a road bridge with a flat, insensitive invert and an adjacent box culvert as low and medium range controls. Broad floodplains operate above 2m when the Lodon tributary may bypass station on rb. Floodplain on lb (above 2.5m) is very wide (0.8 km). No dmfs 15-26/9/98 due to recalibration problems at the station. Natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Subdued relief, relatively low rainfall. Catchment consists of bedrock of very low permeability. D/s of 55028 lithology changes from Old Red Sandstone to ORS marls. Rural catchment, mixed farming (50% grassland, 35% arable) with livestock on higher ground.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Pinsley Brook at Cholstrey Mill
stationDescription: Flat V weir width with 1:5 downstream, 1:2 upstream and 1:5 cross stream slopes. The structure is 10m long and 2m wide with 1m high side walls. Estimated bank full discharge is 3 cumecs. Gauged from adjacent footbridge. The station is located on the left bank of the Pinsley Brook, some 3km u/s from the confluence with the River Lugg.
catchmentDescription: The catchment is of subdued relief as a result of the underlying marl-dominated strata of the Lower Old Red Sandstone and subsequent deposition of superficial deposits.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Lugg at Butts Bridge
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with rough stone control (at low flows). Known bed stability issues. Width at bankfull is 21m. Station rebuilt in 1984. July - Sept 1996 flows under review. Station is a flood warning station.
catchmentDescription: Headwaters drain Radnor Forest (developed on Silurian formations). Subdued relief in the lower valley (mostly Old Red Sandstone). Impervious catchment but extensive valley gravels provide some baseflow. Land use: mainly grassland in upper catchment, rough grazing with some forestry. Mixed farming with higher proportion of arable in the lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Trothy at Mitchel Troy
stationDescription: Velocity-area station; informal Flat V weir installed 1975. Natural river section with low flow control. Monitored flow from area to S and W of Monmouth.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Wye at Redbrook
stationDescription: Channel control velocity-area station replacing Cadora (55001, 1937-71; 4040 sq.km. which was tidally affected; flows incorporated in the Redbrook series). Width at bankfull stage approx 69m. All but extreme floods contained. Severe summer weed growth problems causes gaugings to be increasingly undertaken by ADCP just u/s of Bigsweir Bridge, 7km d/s where channel is clearer. Gaugings at Redbrook are done by cableway. Flow regime moderately modified by exports and regulation. Some naturalised data available. In 2013, comprehensive rating review resulted in new ratings dating back to 1977, affecting both GDFs throughout the entire flow range (some increases in flow, some decreases) and HIFs (largely decreases).
catchmentDescription: Very large catchment of mixed Palaeozoic geology, impermeable rocks of Ordovician to Carboniferous age. Wet in upland areas to the west, drier in lower-lying areas in east and south. Catchment is 60% grassland, with moorland, forestry and grazing on higher ground; mixed farming with greater proportion of arable land in lower reaches.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Llynfi at Three Cocks
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with an informal broad-crested, asymmetrical Flat V weir enhancing the natural rock bar control. Cableway section formalised within the abutments of a former railway bridge. Maximum peak flow occurred in Dec 1979 - this was undoubtedly exceptional although uncertainty surrounds the magnitude (198.4 cumecs on Hiflows-UK database) as flow was extrapolated. Gap in record in 2001 the result of telemetry fault which could not be resolved due to Foot and Mouth restrictions. Data estimated for Aug - Nov 2003. Natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Impermeable geology: headwaters drain the ORS of the Black Mountains; lower reaches expose ORS marls which have lower relief. Contains Llangorse Lake. Predominantly (75%) grassland with patches of woodland and some arable in lower reaches.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Wye at Ddol Farm
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with rock bar as control. Informal Flat V installed 1972. Initially, gauged nearby at Rhayader (55005,1937-69). Bankfull width 30m. Cableway span 54m. All but exceptional floods contained. Issues with stilling well intake pipe from 2006, notably affecting low flows, lead to long-term reliance on back-up recorder and culminated in eventual station rebuild in 2012/13. Following rebuild, in July 2013, all ratings reviewed and flows since Aug 1978 reprocessed, particularly affecting gdf low flows since 1984 and hifs since 2003. Lowest gauging station on Wye unaffected by large water supply res. (flows from Elan valley complex enter just d/s).
catchmentDescription: Wet, upland catchment draining impermeable, metamorphosed Silurian sediments. High relief, headwaters reach over 600m, and feature steep sided valleys and high gradient streams. Dominant land use is grassland (70%) with, moorland and extensive areas of forest in headwaters.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Rudhall Brook at Sandford Bridge
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir. Station reinstated in October 1991 (very little data for 1978-90 period). Data processing suspended in 1998 due to severe problems with the approach channel.
catchmentDescription: Small catchment on northern edge of the Forest of Dean. Mixed agriculture underlain by Old Red Sandstone.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Frome at Bishops Frome
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir 5m wide, replaced velocity-area station in 1975. Cableway span 10m. Steep banks do not contain flood flows; some throttling by d/s road bridge whose soffit is below bankfull. Natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Impermeable bedrock geology, headwaters cutting into ORS of Bromyard plateau. In NE area of Wye catchment, which is drier than other areas to W. Superficial deposits confined to valleys. Mixed arable and pasture farming.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Monnow at Grosmont
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with an informal Flat V weir enhancing the natural rock step control. Approx. 30m wide at bankfull. Cableway spans 42m. Flows prior to Apr 1972 derived from Kentchurch (55009, 450m downstream). Missing flows in early 1963 likely to have been amongst the lowest winter flows on record. In August 2012, flows reprocessed from 01/01/1987 reflecting rating changes particularly affecting low flows. Lowest flows on record in 2003 corroborated by gaugings on 26th Aug and 13th Oct. Wide (>150m) floodplain inundated at high flows. Natural flow regime.  From 25 Mar 2009, flows from Grosmont Rhosllwyn gauging station (EA number 55047), a replacement station 200m d/s/ of old station.
catchmentDescription: Impermable catchment. Five parallel tributaries drain SE down the deeply dissected ORS plateau of the Black Mountains, the northern-most exposing the ORS marls. Land use: Moorland headwaters, mixed farming (with extensive arable) in lower reaches to E.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Claerwen at Dol-y-mynach
stationDescription: Twin-bay sharp edge weir constructed to monitor runoff from a the partially completed Dol-y-Mynach dam. Construction of the dam was abandoned, but it resulted in a storage of around 0.1 sq km. The discharge was regarded as natural runoff until the construction of Claerwen reservoir in 1948. Discharges above the weir capacity derived by straight-line extrapolation and station may drown, so high flows likely to be suspect (and over-estimated), No hif seris. Station discontinued 1950.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment on impermeable Lower Silurian slates, grits and conglomerates. Steep terrain with little soil cover and extensive areas of peat bogs.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Yazor Brook at Three Elms
stationDescription: Flat V weir, theoretically rated, replaced, in 1999, non-standard Flat V Crump (1:5 cross-slopes, 2.5m wide) - drowned out due to inappropriate initial specification; flow record unreliable. Zero flows experienced during summer months. Gravel accretion causes rating variability, checked by c/m. Floods contained. Affected by industrial abstractions from groundwater, which can dominate flow pattern at low flows.
catchmentDescription: Low relief catchment containing urban development of western Hereford. Some light industry but land use is mostly arable agriculture developed on Old Red Sandstone marls; extensively covered with glacial sands and gravel, which maintain baseflow in the Yazor Brk.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Elan at Caban Dam
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir 23m wide, 350m d/s of Caban dam; cableway spans 40m. Entirely regulated apart from overspill. 5 u/s reservoirs. Circa 4 cumecs exported to Midlands. Releases for compensation (currently 0.79 cumecs, previously 1.5 cumecs), regulation and freshets. Flows from 3 Jun 1981 reprocessed in Aug 2012 as a result of rating changes slightly affecting low flows. NRFA station name changed from Elan Village to Caban Dam in Aug 2012 to be consistent with gauging authority name and grid ref also updated. Monthly naturalised flows available for certain periods from older Caban Coch station (55006).
catchmentDescription: Very wet (>1800mm), high elevation catchment draining impermeable geology, predominantly Silurian shales and slates. Upland pasture and moorland, patches of forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Wye at Gwy flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed by Hydraulics Research Ltd, suitable for streams with steep gradients, heavy sediment loads and high flood/drought flow ratios. Shoal formation common, requiring prompt removal to avoid drowning. Check gauged by c/m, and volumetrically for lowest flows. Responsive natural regime. Long term IH research catchment (now CEH) nested within 55008. Primary 15 minute dataset available.
catchmentDescription: Very wet (>2500mm) catchment - drains highest area of Plynlimon massif: composed of Ordovician massive grits (unconfined aquifer maintaining baseflow), slates and Silurian mudstones. Land use: heath on peaty plateau; grassland on free draining slopes (supporting sheep grazing) and mires in valley bottoms.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Cyff at Cyff flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed by Hydraulics Research Ltd, suitable for streams with steep gradients, heavy sediment loads and high flood/drought flow ratios. Shoal formation common, requiring prompt removal to avoid drowning. Check gauged by c/m, and volumetrically at lowest flows. Responsive, natural flow regime. Long term IH research catchment (now CEH) nested within 55008. Primary 15 minute dataset available.
catchmentDescription: Very wet (>2500mm)catchment - drains Plynlimon massif: composed of Ordovician grits, shales and slates and Silurian mudstones, mostly covered by peaty soils. Land use: mainly natural or partly reseeded grassland, supporting sheep grazing.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Iago at Iago flume
stationDescription: Rectangular, side contracted critical depth flume designed by Hydraulics Research Ltd, suitable for streams with steep gradients, heavy sediment loads and high flood/drought flow ratios. Shoal formation common, requiring prompt removal to avoid drowning. Gauged by c/m, and volumetrically at lowest flows. Responsive, natural flow regime. IH research catchment (now CEH) nested within 55008. Primary 15 minute dataset available. Discontinued 06/99.
catchmentDescription: Very wet (>2500mm) catchment - drains Plynlimon massif: composed of Ordovician grits, shales and slates and Silurian mudstones. Vegetation: heath on peaty plateau; grassland on free draining slopes (supporting sheep grazing) and mires in valley bottoms.
operator: Centre for Ecology & Hydrology
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Garren at Marstow Mill
stationDescription: Velocity-area station using Ultrasonic flow measurement in natural section primarily for low flow conditions. US gaugings became unreliable in 2011 and flows calculated from rating. Located d/s of side overflow weir which operates during high flow conditions. Approx. 0.7km u/s of confluence with R. Wye.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Lower ORS overlain with brown earth soils of Erdiston Association. Mixed agriculture.
operator: Environment Agency - Shropshire Herefordshire Worcestershire and Gloucestershire

name: Usk at Chainbridge
stationDescription: Velocity-area station; permanent cableway. All flows contained by bridge but some overspilling occurs during extreme events d/s on left bank. Flows since Feb 1981 reprocessed in Sept 2014 due to rating change, with impact of slightly increasing low flow readings. Flows in Autumn 2003 suspect because stage below that measured at station (float resting on bottom of stilling well). Complementary station d/s (56010 - Trostrey, a 27.43m wide Crump weir) for flows <21 cumecs. Partial impact on flows resulting from three large existing PWS reservoirs in upper catchment. Intake to canal u/s of gauge. Some naturalised flows available.
catchmentDescription: Impermeable catchment, mainly Old Red Sandstone, some Boulder Clay and alluvium in valleys. Principal land use is grassland (65%), hill farming in upper areas, with dairy or livestock farming below; heathland in highest elevations, ~15% forest. Peaty soils in uplands, seasonally wet.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ebbw at Rhiwderin
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Originally natural section, low flow Flat V weir (width: 14.5m, cross-slope 1:20) installed in 1976. Weir refurbished, station completely rebuilt so no dmfs 19/07/96 - 05/01/97. Discharges up to MAF contained. Small water supply reservoirs in uplands. Some gw abstractions in valley. Drainage water from old coalmines can also influence flows. Flows since 1 February 1997 reprocessed in January 2013 due to rating change, resulting in minor decreases to daily mean and monthly maximum flows particularly for period 1997-2000. Location may also be referred to as Rhiwderyn.
catchmentDescription: Geology: moderate permeability, mainly Coal Measures. Mixed land use: 40% grassland, upland heath at highest elevations in N; 15% forest, mainly in lower valley to S. Significant urban development (>10%) in valleys.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Honddu at The Forge Brecon
stationDescription: Three-bay compound crump weir (centre crest width is 2.438m, flanking crests are 4.877m) replaced problematic river section. Current meter gaugings were taken to confirm theoretical rating. Steep high banks on each flank. Good fall in river bed d/s ensures modularity at all stages. Station discontinued 1984.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment, predominantly impermeable geology of Old Red Sandstone with an inlier of the Ludlow Series. Mainly rough grassland and moorland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Usk at Llandetty
stationDescription: Velocity-area station, downgraded to a flood warning level-only station in October 1985, then relocated 50m d/s and upgraded in Oct 1999. MA/NRFA record for original station ends Dec 1981 (now including 1980-81 data added in Aug 2014). The flow record for the reopened station available from Jan 2000. Peak flow data on Hiflows-UK database throughout "missing period". The station is located on the rb, immediately u/s of a sharp left bend in the channel. There is a raised cableway stanction on the lb. Banks in the measurement section are reinforced with boulders. Natural bankfull stage is 3.7 m, and ratings are likely to under-estimate flow for events that go out of bank. Cray and Usk Reservoirs in catchment affect runoff.
catchmentDescription: Geology predominantly impermeable Old Red Sandstone. Land use is mainly grazing and some forestry
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Lwyd at Ponthir
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir (three crests, each 6.096m wide). Calibration assumes modularity. Flows <176 cumecs contained. Central crest subject to occasional blockage by debris in high flows, middle walls of structure removed Sept 1994 because of this. Problems at site: new weir and work on approach channel needed; now level only with no flow data processed after 10/07/98. Small reservoirs for industrial and PWS in upper reaches. Some gw abstraction in valley where there is augmentation by drainage water from old mines.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Coal Measures. Generally livestock farming with urban development in lower areas. Forest 5%. Peaty soils in uplands, seasonally wet.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Usk at Trallong
stationDescription: Velocity-area station in a straight reach; gravel bed control associated with a rock exposure. All but very exceptional flows contained within banks. Computation of daily flows ceased in 1984 - now level only station, principally a flood warning facility. Responsive flow regime. Catchment includes the Cray and Usk Reservoirs.
catchmentDescription: Impervious catchment - mainly ORS - draining from the Brecon Beacons; land use is mainly grazing, some afforestation.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Senni at Pont-Hen-Hafod
stationDescription: Flat V weir replaced Crump weir (width: 7.01m) from December 1997. Concrete side walls form a contained approach to the weir and bridge abutments prevent bypassing. Flows contained up to wingwalls of about 2.27 m. Fish baffles on weir face may cause slight backing up at low flows. Crump weir was full range, modular, theoretically calibrated and confirmed by gaugings. Original fish pass removed in 1973. Gap in record in 2001 due to Foot and Mouth restrictions.
catchmentDescription: Natural catchment draining from high rainfall, upland area within Brecon Beacons national park. Impermeable bedrock geology (Old Red Sandstone). Dominant land use is grassland (80%), supporting livestock farming, with mainly peaty soils. Small patches of heathland and forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Sirhowy at Wattsville
stationDescription: Crump profile Flat V weir; crest width approx 12m and cross slope of 1:10. Rhb rises steeply up valley side after narrow berms. Severe accretion problems. Rating fits gaugings very well up to highest at 1.2m. Upper limit raised to slightly below wingwall height (2.0m). Structure is a standard weir; S-D relationship unlikely to change.
catchmentDescription: Narrow, linear catchment trending approx N-S. Predominantly upland, mixed geology of moderate permeability - Pennant and Upper Coal series overlying Lower Coal series. Some Boulder Clay and gravels on valley floor. Predominantly grazing, moorland in N, with some urban development in lower valley.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Grwyne at Millbrook
stationDescription: Crump weir of reinforced concrete and local stone with phosphor bronze crest 10.67m wide. Limited u/s shoaling and weed growth on weir may affect low flows. Fish counter on d/s side of weir not thought to affect performance. Flows contained up to 2.27m. No bypassing on either bank at high flows - channel contained by road wall on left bank.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable, predominantly ORS conglomerates, s'st and marl. Land use: 50% grassland, principally livestock farming, with significant (30%) heathland (on higher ground) and afforestation (15%).
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Yscir at Pont-Ar-Yscir
stationDescription: Crump weir with aluminium-bronze crest, (width 9m). between old railway abutments, which prevent bypassing. Calibration confirmed by gaugings. Full range, rarely non-modular. No cableway. Flows since 01/09/1999 reprocessed in 2011 due to new rating, leading to small changes in GDFs and HIFs.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Old Red Sandstone, low permeability. Natural catchment draining from upland areas of Cambrian Hills. Land use is 70% grassland, mostly hill farming, upland heath in upper catchment, with peaty soils. Isolated patches of forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Usk at Usk Reservoir
stationDescription: Outflows from Usk Reservoir. Highly artificial regime, and water balance substantially affected by PWS.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Olway Brook at Olway Inn
stationDescription: Crump weir 4.0m wide. Bypassing occurs above 1.8m into surrounding fields. Problems of backing up at high flows.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Lower Old Red Sandstone, 15% Boulder Clay cover (in NW) and alluvium in valley bottom. 75% grassland, with some arable agriculture  and patchy woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Ebbw at Aberbeeg
stationDescription: Velocity-Area station with non-standard bed control, immediately upstream of a vertical-drop weir. Some estimated flow between 7 Aug and 23 Sep 2012 caused by in-river works reprofiling the river channel gradient and weir. Rating established by current meter gaugings, taken by wading at low flows and from a bridge 1km upstream at medium to high flows. Although QMED is within bankfull, no gaugings to support ratings to bankfull so high flow performance is uncertain.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology of varying permeability, predominantly Pennant and Upper Coal Series overlying Lower Coal Series.  Upland catchment with moorland and rough grazing, but with significant urban development in the valley.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Taf Fechan at Taf Fechan Reservoir
stationDescription: Reservoir outflows. Artificially affected regime, and water balance substantially affected by exports for PWS.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Taf Fawr at Llwynon Reservoir
stationDescription: Reservoir outflows. Artificial regime, and water balance substantially affected by exports for PWS. Prior to 1987, station believed to be record of flows in compensation flume (now NRW gauge no 57817) and flood channel (now NRW gauge no 57816). Post 1987, station believed to be record only of flows in compensation flume, which surcharges when flows exceed about 1m3/s, that is flood channel flow no longer measured. Hence, station should be regarded as a low flow station.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Old Red Sandstone, mainly peaty soils. Land use: predominantly grassland and heath, used for rough grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Taff at Tongwynlais
stationDescription: Natural river section with bed control, rated by current meter. Channel width approx 40m.  Well-defined rating to bankfull, estimated thereafter but based on model for Treforest flood protection scheme. Direct water supply reservoirs in upper catchment affect runoff. Abstraction and effluent returns in valley for mining, industry and agriculture. Station closed 1972, main station at 057005 (Pontypridd).
catchmentDescription: Mainly Coal Measures in the south, with Millstone Grit. Carboniferous Limestone and Old Red Sandstone in the north. Some peat on hills, Boulder Clay and Alluvium in valleys. Taff valley, u/s of station, is narrow and steep.  Land use is pasture, some forestry and moorland in headwaters, some urban development in lower valley.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Cynon at Abercynon
stationDescription: Flat V weir (width: 14.24m; cross-slope 1:20) velocity-area station for high flows. Over-topped by extreme floods; no gaugings above bankfull. Data prior to 1962 of poorer quality than those since, although Sept / Oct 1959 minima realistic. Flow minima during period of missing data in 1960 likely to have been 0.30-0.35 cumecs. Small impounding reservoirs for PWS, effluent returns from industrial areas in valley.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures with Millstone Grit on northern boundary. 30% Boulder Clay cover and alluvium in valley. Livestock farming and heathland in upland area of peaty soils, seasonally wet. 20% forest, mainly in S half. Extensive urban and industrial development in valley, which is generally steep-sided. Open cast coal abstraction in upper areas.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Taff at Pontypridd
stationDescription: Non-standard flat V weir (width: 32m; cross-slope 1:20 u/s and d/s) velocity-area station for high flows. Weir refurbished Sept-Nov 1996; these data have been estimated. Full range and flows contained within the section. High flow gaugings taken using ADVP to around 3m. Small impounding reservoir in upper catchment. Some gw abstractions and effluent returns in valleys.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Coal Measures, some Boulder Clay cover (25%) and alluvial deposits in valleys. Mainly upland area with peaty soils on hills, seasonally wet. Catchment is 50% grassland with livestock farming on hills, heathland at highest elevations and 20% forest cover. Extensive urban and industrial development in valleys.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Rhondda at Trehafod
stationDescription: Velocity-area station; concrete trapezoidal channel formalised in 1980, bed width 18m, bankfull width approx 28m. Full range. Flows affected by mine-water discharge above station, and Trehafod Flood Alleviation Scheme. Small impounding reservoir for PWS in upper catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures with alluvial deposits and Boulder Clay in valleys, peat in upper catchment. Upland area with livestock farming and heathland on hills, 25% forest cover (mainly on high ground to NW). Extensive urban and industrial development in valleys.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Taff at Fiddlers Elbow
stationDescription: Flat V weir (width: 23m; cross-slope 1:20), velocity-area station for high flows. Full range. Flows affected by mine-water discharges u/s, also impounding reservoirs and industrial abstractions in valley.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures with Millstone Grit and Carboniferous L'st in northern area. Boulder Clay and Alluvial deposits in valleys. Mainly upland area with peaty soils, seasonally wet. Land use is 55% grassland, heathland at highest elevations and >10% forest. Considerable urban development in valley bottom.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Rhymney at Llanedeyrn
stationDescription: Flat V weir (width: 15m, cross-slope 1:20); velocity-area station for high flows. Full range. Flows since Nov 1991 reprocessed in 2013 following rating change, causing minor decreases in gdfs and hifs in period 2002-2011. May-Sept 1990 low flows under review. Extensive floodplains on both banks; bypass (200m d/s) keeps flow on floodplain. Impounding reservoirs, for PWS, in upper catchment. Some groundwater abstraction and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Coal Measures (moderate permeability). Peaty soils on hills, seasonally wet. Land Use: 50% grassland with upland heath at highest elevations in N, 15% forest (in lower valley to S), and significant urban and industrial development in the valleys.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ely at St Fagans
stationDescription: Flat V weir (width: 10.6m; cross-slope 1:20); velocity-area station for high flows. Full range with flow gauged up to 96 m3/s. A proportion of flow (thought to be around 10%) bypasses the gauging station at higher levels, hence, above 55 m3/s, the rating is based on a hydraulic model in addition to gaugings, to account for the bypass flow. Some early - poorer quality - data available (station 57805; 1957-60). Flows from 7/3/2010 to 10/5/2010 suspect due to temporary damming of river at site. Flows affected by sewage works discharges u/s. Some industrial abstractions.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Coal Measures with some Millstone Grit in northern area; mixture of Trias, Lias, l'st and ORS to the S. Extensive (75%) superficial deposits, Boulder Clay in N and sands and gravels in S. Land use: mainly pasture, with patches of forest and some built-up areas.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ely at Lanelay
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with non-standard Flat V bed control (width: 7.94m; cross-slope 1:20). Flows from 16/10/2010 to 3/11/2010 suspect due to blocking of the inlet pipe. Now primarily a flood warning station.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures. Lowland catchment. Dairy and livestock farming with urban and industrial development in the valley. Forest 8%. Soils have permeable substrate.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Rhymney at Bargoed
stationDescription: Bargoed gauging station (previously called Gilfach Bargoed) opened in 1967, measuring stage in a natural river section; a weir was constructed in 1975 and a cableway added in 1977. The rating was unstable due to silting and, from 1986, the station operated as a flood warning station only. A new gauging station with a flat V weir was constructed in 2001, 30m u/s of the original site. Bed at gauging site is rocky and underlain by concrete weir approach channel. Rating based on recent gaugings has reasonable confidence only at low-medium flows (flows less than median annual flood). Impounding reservoirs for PWS in upper catchment; some effluent returns. Flood warning station.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Coal Measures. Peaty soils on hills with livestock farming and heathland. Significant urban and industrial development, with some forestry, in valley bottoms.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Taff at Merthyr Tydfil
stationDescription: Flat V weir; velocity-area station for high flows. From 1998, structure is 14 m wide, 1:20 cross slope. Full range. Flow records from 1978 updated in August 2014 due to rating change. POR max. of 228 cumecs in Oct 1998 should be treated with caution as extrapolated, may have been overestimated. Dec 1979 flood (which destroyed the weir) may have been higher but considerable uncertainty surrounds magnitude. Flows for the period 03/06/13 to 15/08/13 are backfilled from Troedyrhiw FWS (57032) due to weir refurbishment. Flows affected by large direct PWS reservoirs.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment in Brecon Beacons National Park. Geology: Millstone Grit and Carboniferous L'st. Old Red S'st in upper areas; some Boulder Clay in valleys. Mainly peaty soils, seasonally wet. Land use: 60% grassland supporting livestock farming, significant upland heath and some forest. Minor urban development just u/s of station.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Rhondda Fawr at Tynewydd
stationDescription: Flat V weir in good condition (in 2010) with fish baffles on d/s slope. Channel is 7.35m wide with 1.667m high concrete wing walls either side of weir. Gauging section is concrete channel with good bed stability, though prone to shoaling which has an adverse effect on the modular limit of the weir. Full range; no bypassing. Low to medium flows are wade-gauged and high flows are gauged by cableway. Flows for levels above 1.1m are based on a hydraulic model. Natural flow regime. Flood warning station.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures with alluvial deposits and Boulder Clay in valleys, peat in upper catchment. Livestock farming and heathland on hills; forest cover on high ground to NW.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Clun at Cross Inn
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ogmore at Bridgend
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with Flat V weir (1:20 cross-slope; installed in Jul 1975). Channel width: 20m. Flows up to 170 cumecs contained. Flows estimated for March-April 1986. Flows since 1 Jan 2005 reprocessed in 2013 due to new rating.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Coal Measures, some Boulder Clay and alluvium in valleys. Peaty soils on hills, seasonally wet. Forest 16%. Land use: 55% grassland supporting livestock farming in uplands to N, dairy farming in S, with 20% forest cover. Heavy urban and industrial development in valleys, including significant development just u/s of station.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Neath at Resolven
stationDescription: Flat V weir (installed in 1978); velocity-area station for high flows; channel width: 28m. Some u/s right-bank spillage during floods; water stored on floodplain and does not bypass. Some records from 1961 available, but channel considered unstable. Hiflows-UK lists the POR max. as Oct 1967, but the NRFA does not hold these early peaks; they may not be homogeneous. PWS reservoir in upper catchment. Industrial abstractions and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: A mainly upland catchment with mixed geology, from S to N: Coal Measures; Millstone Grit; Carboniferous L'st and ORS. Extensive Boulder Clay cover in mid-catchment. Land use: livestock farming predominates; some areas of upland heath; >20% forest cover; minor urban development in valley.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ewenny at Ewenny Priory
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Water level records from Jul 1960. Discharge records (Oct 1962-1965) take account of mine water. Bed erosion and weed growth rendered S/D relationship inaccurate, especially at low flows, so no data published after 1965. Station abandoned. Superseded by Flat V weir at Keeper's Lodge (58009) d/s in 1971.
catchmentDescription: Geology: north - Coal Measures; S - mixture of Millstone Grit, Carboniferous L'st, Trias, Lias and alluvial deposits. Lowland area with urban/industrial development and dairy/livestock farming. Soils have permeable substrate.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Afan at Cwmafan
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with an informal control (sewage pipe crossing). Open channel rating. Fully functional station, with stilling well. Low flows are beyond rating lower limit. Station reopened in 1989 at the site of the original auxiliary gauge, around 1.5km u/s of the main gauge location in Cwmafan between 1961 and 1977. Data between 1989 and 1997 of low quality and not currently held on the NRFA. Runoff reduced by PWS abstraction. Minewater discharges in upper catchment affect flows. In 2013, NRFA station name changed from Cwmavon to Cwmafan for consistency with Natural Resources Wales spelling.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures, 40% superficial deposits with peat in uplands and Boulder Clay in valleys. 50% forested, remainder rough grazing. Some urban development, including just u/s of station. Past mining activity evident.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Ogmore at Brynmenyn
stationDescription: Flat V weir; velocity-area station for high flows. Channel width is 13.7m. All flows contained.Weir refurbishment phase I Sep 2012 to Apr 2013, with data recorded at a temporary sensor over this whole period and nearby 58001 Ogmore at Bridgend used to infill missing data and correct erroneous data. Effluent discharge to river u/s.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures. Land use: 60% grassland with livestock farming, some heathland in upland area, urban development in the valleys. Forest 20%. Peaty soils in upper areas, seasonally wet.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Mellte at Pontneddfechan
stationDescription: Flat V weir and velocity-area station; channel width 15m. Weir refurbishment undertaken June / July 2009; hydraulics should not be affected. During this period, upstream sensor used in place of stage measurement instrument within stilling well; data may display minimal amount of variation to period of record data. Steep section with heavy bed load. Station in steep valley and flows well contained. Flashy river so high flow gaugings are problematic. Data estimated for Nov - Dec 1980, Sept - Oct 1991, and May - July 2005. PWS reservoir in catchment has partial effect on flows.
catchmentDescription: Rural upland catchment. Geology: from S to N - Millstone Grit; Carboniferous L'st and ORS, with 50% Boulder Clay cover in S half of catchment. Predominantly grassland (65%) with significant areas of upland heath and some forestry.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Llynfi at Coytrahen
stationDescription: Flat V weir and velocity-area station. Bankfull channel width 9.1m. Full range. Station in steep valley where flows fairly well contained. Flashy response so obtaining high flow gaugings problematic. Industrial abstractions and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures, some Boulder Clay and alluvium in valley. Upland area with livestock farming (>50% grassland), significant (25%) forest cover and urban development in valleys. Mainly peaty soils, seasonally wet.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dulais at Cilfrew
stationDescription: Compound Crump weir and Flat V weir from Aug 1991. Formerly Flat V weir (1:10 cross slope) flanked by horizontal side section - no divide piers; velocity-area calibration for high flows. D/s of single arch railway bridge of limited discharge capacity. Modular throughout range as sited on top of natural rock fall. Steep valley, flows fairly well contained. Sewage discharges affect runoff.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures, with 35% Boulder Clay Cover. Upland area with livestock farming, some heathland and 25% forest cover. Some urban development in valley, and open cast coal mining.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Ewenny at Keepers Lodge
stationDescription: Flat V weir (1:15 cross-slope terminating in a 1:2 sloping revetment); velocity-area calibration for high flows. All flows contained. Channel width 12.25m. Rating changed due to drowning caused by not being able to regularly clear d/s channel since early 1990s. Data 21/06/95 - end 2002 reprocessed. Poorer quality data (1962-1965) available for d/s station Ewenny Priory (58003). Regime influenced by abstractions and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Geology: north - Coal Measures. South - mixture of Millstone Grit, Carboniferous Limestone, Triassic and Lias sediments. 35% superficial deposits with Boulder Clay and alluvium. Mainly lowland area with dairy and livestock farming, some forest and urban development (including just u/s of station).
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Hepste at Esgair Carnau
stationDescription: Velocity area station with Flat V weir (1:10 cross slope; 1:2 u/s; 1:5 d/s). Channel width 7m. Modular up to wingwall height, QMED at approx. bankfull, but lack of high flow gaugings. Low flow data believed to be of very good quality. Record maximum flow in Oct 2000 was extrapolated and may be an overestimate. Data between 9 Jan and 16 May 2012 estimated due to logger problem. Flows from 1 Dec 1993 to 2012 reprocessed in 2012 following rating update, with minimal change to data. No artificial influences.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment within the Brecon Beacons National Park. Mostly impermeable ORS with carboniferous outcrops at southern limits, some Boulder Clay cover in SW. Land use is upland pasture.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Thaw at Gigman Bridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir for low and medium flows, US for high flows, commissioned April 1999. Replaced non-standard bed control weir; velocity-area calibration based on gaugings from bridge u/s. Flows from April 1999 reprocessed in August 2014 due to rating change, with effect of lowering high flows. Experienced problems with leakage under bed control in early record. Out-of-bank flow can occur on rb. Station was taken off-line in September 1998 for complete rebuild with 7-month gap in data. Reason for further 3-month gap in 2004 not known. Flows affected by effluent discharges and gw abstractions.
catchmentDescription: Lowland catchment in the Vale of Glamorgan. Mixed geology: Lias; Trias; Carboniferous Limestone and ORS. Significant areas of low permeability substrate. 40% Drift cover, mainly glacial sands and gravels. Mainly grass pasture, some patches of arable and forest; minor urban development.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Afan at Marcroft Weir
stationDescription: Non-standard compound Crump profile weir. No divide walls between centre and side weirs. Weir refurbishment 3 Sep 2012 - 1 Jul 2013, with temporary loss of data during this period. Nearby 58004 Afan at Cwmafan and modelling used to infill missing data and correct erroneous data until new rating at 58012 established. Stilling pipes not submerged at low flows and shoaling around pipes. Channel width: 17.2m. Modular limit not known but unlikely to exceed bankfull flow. High velocities due to steep gradients. Runoff reduced by PWS abstraction. Minewater discharges in upper catchment affect flows and have severely affected water quality.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures, 40% superficial deposits with peat in uplands and Boulder Clay in valleys. 50% forested, remainder rough grazing. Some urban development, including just u/s of station. Past mining activity evident.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Tawe at Ynystanglws
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Gravel bed provides unstable control. Pressure transducer installed in 2005 with logger attached. All but extreme floods contained since construction of floodbanks in 1959; floodbanks raised following Dec 1979 flood. Flood storage area d/s built in early 1980s, controlled by d/s side weir on rb (water returns through valve d/s with no effect on flow at station), weir now removed. During low flow events (level below 0.448m), well intake pipe often out of water due to significant bed erosion.  It is unclear when problem started, but results in limited low flow sensitivity; levels adjusted as necessary to be realistic though flow repetitions may occur (e.g. Aug 2006). Since Aug 2010 secondary sensor installed u/s of existing intake pipe to facilitate measurement at low water levels.  Limestone outcrop at north of catchment has partial effect on baseflow; GW and industrial abstractions also.
catchmentDescription: Geology: principally Coal Measures. Mostly mixed permeability, with impermeable strata in headwaters. 30% Boulder Clay cover. Mainly upland area with livestock farming (>60% grassland). Forest and some Urban and industrial development at lower levels. 30% in Brecon Beacons National Park.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Loughor at Tir-y-dail
stationDescription: Velocity-area station with bed control (crude oblique Crump weir) built over sewer crossing. Right bank overtopped on rare occasions. PWS abstraction from main spring source. Gw and industrial abstractions and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Southern half is mainly Coal Measures; Millstone Grit, Carboniferous L'st and ORS in northern half. Extensive Boulder Clay cover at lower elevations. Mainly dairy farming (>75% grassland) and some patches of forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Tywi at Ty Castell
stationDescription: Velocity-area high flow station where levels are monitored using a telemetered shaft encoder (hiflows data only on NRFA). Natural open-channel section within a straight reach of river. Channel width: 42m. lb contains high flows, rb allows extreme high flows to spill and bypass station. Ty Castell effective above 50 cumecs. Low flows measured d/s at 60010 (Capel Dewi). Llyn Brianne in headwaters regulates flow down to major DCWW PWS abstraction d/s of station (but u/s of 60010) and may be detected in hydrograph.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly impermeable, Ordovician and Silurian with ORS on southern boundary. Peaty soils in headwaters. Alluvium in valleys. Catchment is 70% grassland with hill farming in upper catchment, some livestock and dairying at lower levels. 20% forest cover, significant concentration along NE margin.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Cothi at Felin Mynachdy
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Straight reach and natural rock control. Channel width: 20m. Stable section. All but very extreme flows contained. POR max. is believed to be Oct 1987, when bypassing occurred on right bank. Magnitude subject to uncertainty owing to rating extrapolation, estimated to be c.430 cumecs. Flows from 1 Jun 2007 to 31 Dec 2012 reprocessed in 2014 due to rating change, increasing gdfs below approx 5 cumecs and, as a consequence, Summer hifs in this period. Effectively a natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable strata, mainly Silurian with Ordovician along SE boundary. Land use: 75% grassland, upland pastures, livestock and dairy farming below. Significant forest cover (20%).
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Taf at Clog-y-Fran
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Lb steep with berm for recorder. Right flood bank at approx 3.2 - 3.4m; considerable overspill during flood events. Channel width 13.9m. Flow record from 60002 Cothi at Felin Mynachdy used to infill various short periods of missing data (principally in 1986, but also 1991, 2001, 2004). Flows since 1 Jan 2010 reprocessed in 2013 due to new rating, with main affect of lowering low flow gdfs. Natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Mainly impermeable strata, Ordovician with some narrow bands of igneous rock, ORS in S. Alluvium in valleys. Land use: >80% grassland, mainly dairy farming. Very small patches of arable and woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
