farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Dewi Fawr at Glasfryn Ford
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Concrete ford d/s acts as a stable bed control. Station constructed in opening of railway embankments where railway bridge crosses river. Bridge contains all flows. Channel width 7m. At approx 2m water spreads out along road either side of ford resulting in some backing up. Discontinued at end of 1983, reinstated in Apr 1990. Flows since 13 Jan 2006 reprocessed in 2013 due to new rating, with main affect of reducing high flow hifs.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Ordovician strata. Rural catchment, 80% grassland (mainly dairy farming), minor patches of woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: E: Runoff increased by effluent returns.

name: Bran at Llandovery
stationDescription: Ultrasonic installed, record from Dec 1995, Flat V weir for low flows. Replaced velocity-area station with records from 1968, bed control installed 1972. Channel width: 7.5m. Flood Alleviation Scheme u/s of Llandovery stops any bypass flow. Ultrasonic is prone to dropping out. Low flows in Aug - Sep 1995 are suspect due to work on weir prior to installation of ultrasonics.  Low flows in Jul - Aug 2006 are suspect due to period of turbulent levels. High flows may be affected by confluence 100m d/s. Peak flood flow of 63.5 cumecs in Oct 1985 was extrapolated, subject to some uncertainty. Flows since 16/7/2006 reprocessed in 2011 due to new rating, affecting both low and high flows. Reprocessed HIFs Nov 1995 - Mar 2003 added in 2012, slightly reducing magnitude of flows in this period. Agricultural abstractions have a minor impact on flow records.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Ordovician substrate, with alluvium on valley floor. Peaty soils, seasonally wet, in hill area. Soils have permeable substrate in lower areas. Land use: 50% grassland, with hill farming in uplands, dairy in valleys. 35% forest cover.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: I: Runoff reduced by industrial and/or agricultural abstraction.

name: Gwili at Glangwili
stationDescription: Velocity-area station; stable section except period 1973-85 where section affected by land drainage scheme. Channel width: 15.5m. Station is 750 metres u/s of tidal limit - no backwater effects suspected. All but very extreme flows contained within banks. High flow rating well defined and performs well. Flows from 11 Oct 2005 to 2012 reprocessed in 2012 following rating update, with effect of slightly lowering extreme high and low flows. PWS and agricultural abstractions and effluent returns have minimal impact on flow records.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Ordovician and Silurian strata. Rural catchment, mostly grassland supporting dairy farming, but with extensive (20%) forest Cover.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Tywi at Dolau Hirion
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Stable section with natural control and fairly steep and high banks. Channel width: 38m. River regulated - Llyn Brianne Reservoir in upper catchment. Flows since 1969 reprocessed in 2010 due to rating change, leading to increase in low flows and decrease in high flows, most significant since 1988. Flows since 31 Jan 2008 reprocessed in 2013 due to rating change, with main affect lowering of low flow gdfs and hifs.
catchmentDescription: Upland area of Cambrian Hills. Geology: principally impermeable Ordovician strata. Mainly peaty soils, seasonally wet. Land use: 60% grassland (mostly hill farming with some livestock at lower levels); extensive forest cover (35%), very dense in NE.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Tywi at Ystradffin
stationDescription: Crump weir, single crest. Site owned by Welsh Water. Artificial flow regime - station used principally to monitor compensation and regulated flows from Llyn Brianne Res.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Ordivician and Silurian formations. Land use is 50% forest, 40% rough grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Sawdde at Felin-y-cwm
stationDescription: Flat V. Channel width: 13.7m. Station re-rated 1991-93. Substantial weir refurbishment between 27 Jun 2008 and 3 Oct 2009. Flows from 14 Sep 2009 to 31 Dec 2012 reprocessed in 2014 due to rating change, decreasing gdfs below approx 2 cumecs. Suspect data between 19 Oct 2009 and 2 Feb 2010 due to a blocked pipe. Llyn Y Fan Fach reservoir in headwaters.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly impermeable; ORS at source, Silurian in middle section, Ordovician in lower reaches. Predominantly (85%) grassland, some forest at lower elevations. Mostly within Brecon Beacons National Park.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Tywi at Capel Dewi
stationDescription: Flat V weir (1:20) set in Crump profile flanking section. Shoaling d/s influences modular range; calibration based on gaugings. Channel width: 43m. lb contains high flows, rb allows flows to spill and bypass station. Capel Dewi effective below 221 cumecs. High flows measured u/s at 60001 (Ty Castell), from which all pre-74 flows derive. POR max. occurred in Oct 1987, a well documented event which caused substantial damage in the catchment. A contemporary report estimated the peak at Ty Castell to be 1200 cumecs. Llyn Brianne in headwaters regulates flow down to major DCWW PWS abstraction u/s of station (but d/s of 60001) and may be detected in hydrograph. In 2013, NRFA station name changed from Nantgaredig to Capel Dewi for consistency with MA nomenclature, though station may still be referred to as Nantgaredig on occasion.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly impermeable, Ordovician and Silurian with ORS on southern boundary. Peaty soils in headwaters. Alluvium in valleys. Catchment is 70% grassland with hill farming in upper catchment, some livestock and dairying at lower levels. 20% forest cover, significant concentration along NE margin.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Twrch at Ddol Las
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Channel width 4.65m. Low flows in July - Aug 2006 are suspect as intake pipe was not completely submerged, under investigation (Dec 2007). Natural regime.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment in S Cambrian mountains. Geology: impermeable formations; lower Silurian shales, grits and mudstones. Catchment is 85% grassland, with some patches of woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Cothi at Pont Ynys Brechfa
stationDescription: River section with gravel bed (shoaling occurs d/s near bridge). Steep sides above bankfull, station on RHB. Station was a subsidiary to 60002 (Felin Mynachdy). Station closed after damage from Dec 1979 flood when station floor level was exceeded. NRFA only holds 1971 - 1976 data but peak flow data up to 1981 available on Hiflows-UK database.
catchmentDescription: Geology is mainly impermeable Silurian with Ordovician along south-eastern boundary.  Predominantly grassland, hill farming in upper areas, dairy farming in lower areas with extensive patches of woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Western Cleddau at Prendergast Mill
stationDescription: Velocity-area station, channel width 10.5m. Tidally affected but this is edited out. Occasional overtopping on rb. Flow data was merged with 61004 (Redhill) to produce a continuous record, held as 61001 (Jan 74 - May 90 data is from 61004). Some concerns about quality of flow data since January 2004, particularly low flow data. 2008 and 2012 data highly suspect.Generally natural, some effects of abstractions and effluent returns.
catchmentDescription: Mostly lowland catchment. Geology: impermeable, Ordovician formations with igneous intrusions. Land use: predominantly (75%) grassland with mainly dairy farming, patchy arable in lower areas.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Eastern Cleddau at Canaston Bridge
stationDescription: Velocity-area station; artificial control installed in 1974. Channel width: 17.4m. 1986 POR max. triggered by 80-100mm storm but considerable uncertainty attends the magnitude of the peak flow (significant rating extrapolation was needed). Atypical recession throughout May - July 1984 remains under review. Impounding reservoir for PWS in upper catchment regulates the river down to the gauging station.
catchmentDescription: Mainly lowland with hills to N. Geology: impermeable formations, mainly Ordovician with bands of igneous rock in N. Land use: 75% grassland with some forest and patches of arable in low-lying areas to W.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Gwaun at Cilrhedyn Bridge
stationDescription: Velocity-area station in straight reach (width: 7.0m). Natural steep-sided catchment - very responsive. Flows since 1982 reprocessed in 2010 leading to decrease in both low and high flows.  Flows since 11 October 1995 reprocessed in 2011 leading to to significant increase in high flows (which had been truncated under previous rating).  Natural regime.
catchmentDescription: Small catchment within Pembrokeshire Coast National Park. Geology: impermeable Ordovician formations with intrusions of igneous rock. Land use: 80% grassland, dairy farming in lower areas and livestock on hills. Small patches of woodland and arable.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Teifi at Glanteifi
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Straight reach (width: 35m), natural control. Flood flows (> c4m) spill over right bank. Well gauged - recent gaugings include floodplain flows. PWS impounding reservoirs in upland and minor agricultural abstractions; Tregaron bog (10 sq.km.) has partial effect on flows. Nonetheless, a sensibly natural regime. Staff gauge re-levelled in 2007.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly impermeable Ordovician and Silurian deposits. Land use: 80% grassland, Dairy farming predominates in south; hill farming in upper catchment. 10% forest, mainly on E margin. Peaty soils on hills, seasonally wet. Apart from Tregaron bog, most of the lower areas have soils with permeable substrate.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Teifi at Llanfair
stationDescription: Natural river section with cableway in straight reach of river. Channel width is approx 22.6m. Bed material is alluvium deposits. Steep left bank, right bank more gentle. Lack of high flow gaugings to calibrate upper end of rating, but low flow performance is believed to be very good. PWS impounding reservoirs in upland and minor agricultural abstractions; Tregaron bog (10 sq.km.) has partial effect on flows. Nonetheless, a sensibly natural flow regime. Flows since April 2002 reprocessed with new rating in 2010.
catchmentDescription: Mainly impermeable Ordovician and Silurian geology. Predominantly (80%) grassland. Hill farming in uplands, dairy farming in southern area.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ystwyth at Pont Llolwyn
stationDescription: Velocity-area station (channel width: 16m). Records from 1963, with bed control installed in 1973. Floods spill over right bank. Discharges from lead mines. Post-1985 flows below 3 cumecs are unreliable due to blockage of lower inlet pipe. Channel re-graded and weir refurbished 21 - 27/09/98 (dmfs estimated by NRFA), new rating produced. POR max.in Dec 1964 is listed as 153 cumecs on Hiflows-UK database; this may be an underestimate due to bypassing.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable silurian deposits. Peaty soils in eastern hills, seasonally wet. Most of western catchment has soils with permeable substrata. Land use: 75% grassland, hill farming in uplands, livestock at lower levels. 20% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Rheidol at Llanbadarn Fawr
stationDescription: Velocity-area station. Shoaling affects gauged section (channel width: 20m). Lower limit of current rating is 0.452m, and flows for levels below this considered suspect due to shifting shoals. Public water supply abstractions from river gravels. Impounding reservoir for hydro-electric station at Cwm Rheidol have major effects on flows. Drainage water from old mineral mines in upper catchment. Station closed in 1984; reopened on 31/10/95.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly Silurian with some Ordovician on the northern catchment boundary. Mostly hill farming in upland areas. Forest: 20%. Soils mainly have permeable substrate.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Wyre at Llanrhystud
stationDescription: Velocity area station. Ford (10m d/s) acts as bed control (but may also hold back flow, particularly during periods of snow and ice, inflating readings). Coarse gravel bed. Bypassing at high flows down a mill stream and along a road. Lack of high flow gaugings to calibrate upper end of rating (flagged as estimated above rating upper limit of 0.45m), but low flow performance is thought to be good. POR max.in Aug 1973 triggered by a >100mm storm; peak flow (77.8 cumecs on Hiflows-UK database) is suspect - station was bypassed. Gap in NRFA record from 1979 to 1999; earlier record has some anomalous sequences. Flows 24 Dec 2011 - 23 Jan 2012 estimated from 63001 data due to anomalous behavior in the form of sharp trough caused by silt partially blocking well intake pipe; similar behaviour exhibited in Jan 2005 and 2011 unable to be improved by MA. No known artificial influences on regime.
catchmentDescription: Small Cardigan bay catchment, mostly lowland. Impermeable Silurian geology. Rural land use, predominantly grassland supporting dairy farming, with small patches of woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Ystwyth at Cwm Ystwyth
stationDescription: Flat V weir with vertical side walls, channel width 13m. Land access to station agreed with landowner (as of 2009); should permit future high flow gauging and work to increase confidence in station performance. High flows contained within structure; non-modularity only occurs during extreme events. Flows over 1987-90 period derived from three-hourly level data. Flows 5 Aug-20 Oct 1987 erroneous and deleted in July 2014.
catchmentDescription: Geology: mainly impermeable Silurian shales and grits. Catchment is 60% grassland (mainly used for sheep farming) and 20% forestry. There are numerous disused lead and zinc mines within the catchment.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Dyfi at Dyfi Bridge
stationDescription: A 40m wide river section controlled by the invert and arches of the historical Dyfi road bridge d/s. A good stable section although records in early years are marred by substantial engineering works carried out on the bridge. Natural regime.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Silurian formations, minor Boulder Clay and alluvium deposits. Catchment is 60% grassland and 30% forested, with patches of upland heath.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Dysynni at Pont-y-Garth
stationDescription: Before April 1997, station located in a 40m wide section (between floodbanks) controlled by sheet-piling d/s in straight channel. In 1997, station moved 50m d/s, sheet-piling removed and weir constructed. New station re-rated from Oct 1997. In 2011, updated with reprocessed flows for 2000 and 2001. Station is vulnerable to tidal influence, which is allowed for in the stage-discharge conversion. Insensitive at low flows. Difficult to gauge at high flows due to flashy response. Suffers from weed growth in the channel and on the pitching strip control, with regular clearance sometimes difficult due to high water levels, and also gravel accumulation. Natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Impermeable Ordovician sediments with volcanic rocks outcropping. Tal-y-Llyn (southernmost ribbon lake in Britain) lies within catchment. Land use: 65% grassland, with areas of upland heath; 20% forest (in S of catchment).
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Twymyn at Cemmaes Road
stationDescription: Velocity-Area site with an extremely mobile bed. Attempts were made to stabilise the section with engineering works and the inclusion of a gravel trap. The gravel trap failed to perform (requiring far more emptying than envisaged) and the situation of the trap also spoiled hydraulic conditions in the cableway section. Site was therefore considered uneconomical to maintain and was closed in 2001.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Leri at Dolybont
stationDescription: A 10m wide single crest Crump profile weir in a straight floodbanked reach. Wing walls contain flows to high levels. However, the river burst its banks u/s of the station during the record high flow event of 8 June 2012, causing some of the flow to bypass the station. The rating has not been checked beyond medium flows. Data from 2011 onwards should be used with caution as weir is damaged and deteriorating, with water seeping under the crest. Also suffers from gravel accumulation. However, low flow gaugings do not suggest that leakage is significantly affecting flows. Abstraction into Craig-y-Pistyll reservoir and five discharges upstream of gauging station; substantial net loss of water resulting from these transfers, since sum of discharges does not nearly equal amount of water withdrawn for reservoir.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly impervious Silurian rocks. 80% grassland, 10% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Mawddach at Tyddyn Gwladys
stationDescription: VA station with pitching strip 100m downstream of cableway that is used to stabilise rating curve at low flow. Problems with scouring of bed - boulders and shale move in flood. New rating created after gravel clearance in July 2004, which followed significant accretion since 2001. Bed has since thought to have remained relatively stable. High responsiveness and geographical location make site difficult to gauge and high flow gauging is impaired by poor visibility.
catchmentDescription: Steep mountainous catchment with much bare rock. Predominantly forested.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Glaslyn at Beddgelert
stationDescription: A 20m wide river section rated by c/m and, in the past, by dilution gauging. Rating tends to be insensitive at low flows due to subtle movements in the natural bed control but a non-standard stone section control improves stability. Gravel removal may have produced abrupt level changes in the past but careful management and regular clearance now allows a more stable rating to be maintained. Flows since June 2006 reprocessed in 2011 due to new rating which better defines low flows. 2012 flows reprocessed in 2014, affecting low flows in particular. High flow gauging restricted to peaks and troughs because of rapid water level changes. Station bypassed at high flows. Lakes (Dinas and Gwynant) and HEP discharge from the higher Llyn Llydaw marginally affect records.
catchmentDescription: Very wet, upland catchment draining southern flanks of Snowdonia with much bare rock exposure (impermeable Silurian volcanics). Otherwise, land use is 60% grassland, mainly rough moorland grazing, 20% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Gwyrfai at Bontnewydd
stationDescription: A 10m wide single crest Crump profile weir containing flows to high levels. Weir thought to remain modular, although no cableway to gauge high flows (highest gauging is 5.27 cumecs in Jan 2009) or no downstream level recorder; consequently, modular limit unknown. Check gauging suggests some (constant) loss due to inadequate cutoffs, hence low flows affected. Significant abstraction from Llyn Cwellyn reservoir u/s. No data since 2005 due to a structural failure of the weir; currently being rebuilt. 2004-05 data suspect, particularly at low flows, due to uncertainty surrounding date of weir failure.
catchmentDescription: A steep and typically Snowdonian upland catchment. Geology: impermeable Lower Palaeozoic and volcanics. 70% grassland (rough moorland), >10% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Erch at Pencaenewydd
stationDescription: A 6m wide Crump profile weir with high wing walls containing the full flow range. Check gauged up to medium flows. The outstanding flood in August 2000 resulted from a localised but very intense thunderstorm. It was contained within the structure (and confirmed by wrack marks) but the peak flow is an estimate. Gravel accumulation issues and frequent clearance. Ongoing investigation into unexplained changes in water balance over time.
catchmentDescription: Mostly lowland catchment on the Lleyn peninsula, with hills in N. Impermeable bedrock geology, covered with 85% Boulder Clay. Land use: 75% grassland (rough grazing), 15% woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Seiont at Peblig Mill
stationDescription: A rated river section in a straight reach which has not yet been bypassed (possible on the left bank floodplain). Control provided by a roughly Crump profile shaped structure originally built as part of investigations prior to construction of the Dinorwic pumped storage scheme, which very marginally affects the record. Treat data with caution.
catchmentDescription: A steep, wet catchment on impermeable formations, with much bare rock surface - Snowdon and Glyder Fawr are both in the catchment. Contains two large ribbon lakes, Padarn and Peris, the latter acting as the lower reservoir of the Dinorwic scheme. Land use is 65% grassland (rough upland grazing), 10% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dwyfor at Garndolbenmaen
stationDescription: A compound Crump profile weir with divide piers separating the 6.5m wide lower crest from two flanking crests each 5m wide. Suffers from weed growth and gravel issues: weed frequently removed, gravel occasionally cleared. Station built as the control point for the Cwmystradllyn Reservoir/Afon Dwyfawr regulation scheme. Consequently, not intended for high flow gauging; no cableway present to gauge high flows. Bypassing not observed.
catchmentDescription: The catchment is mainly steep, developed on impermeable Lower Palaeozoic formations; 45% Boulder Clay cover. Land use: 80% grassland (rough grazing), much bare rock.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Peris at Nant Peris
stationDescription: Velocity-Area station (previously known at Nant Peris at Tan-yr-Alt) in almost straight reach. 5m wide river channel, substantial problems with maintaining an accurate rating due to non-standard concrete control d/s and extensive gravel accumulation problems u/s and d/s. Informal concrete bed control constructed in Sept. 1978. Bankfull about 2 metres and all flows thought to be contained to date. High flow rating possible due to cableway on site. Data for 2000-2005 should be treated with caution due to problems with gravel accretion. Annual gravel clearance reduces problem. Very responsive natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: The Nant Peris follows the Llanberis Pass and drains a rugged, steep and exceptionally wet (annual av. c3500 mm) catchment to the north-east of Snowdon. Impermeable geology, land use is rough grazing, with much bare rock exposure.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Colwyn at Hafod Wydr
stationDescription: A 5m wide Crump weir (u/s approach depth 1m). Located in a steep section of the River Colwyn. Modular through to high flows. High wingwalls prevent bypassing and structure only drowns at very high flows due to steep channel section d/s. Some problems with gravel accumulation, which is removed annually, and weed growth. Gauging bridge present at this site.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable catchment draining southern flanks of Snowdonia, predominantly Silurian volcanics often exposed at surface.  Steep, wet catchment covered with 70% grass, 10% forest and 20% bare rock, used for rough grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Llyfni at Pont Y Cim
stationDescription: A 9m wide Crump weir (u/s approach depth 0.5m). Data quality thought to be of acceptable quality at low-medium flows, but poor approach flow conditions cause significant problems when attempting to calibrate weir in high flow conditions. Data from 10 Jan 2011 suspect due to discrepancy between in-well levels and in-river levels (but MA believe problem to have been present for longer), and subject to ongoing (as of September 2013) investigation by MA. Gravel accumulation a serious issue at this site.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable catchment draining western flanks of Snowdonia, Lower Palaeozoic formations and volcanics partially covered with glacial till in lower reaches. Land use: 70% grassland, 10% forest, 20% bare rock, used for rough grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Clwyd at Pont-y-Cambwll
stationDescription: VA station. Station refitted June-Aug 1997, with gabions added to stabilise the section: no dmfs during this work. Rating is relatively stable although known to be affected by weed growth and sediment movement - inlet pipe prone to silting up. A bend 100 m downstream causes variation in velocities across the section, which results in greater uncertainty in the rating, particularly at low flows. Out of bank flows occur on both banks around 50 cumecs and some out-of-bank storage u/s at high flows. New rating created following Feb 2004 flood. Low flows augmented using gw (approx. 12% of Q95 flow). Flood discharges affected by floodplain storage in Vale of Clwyd u/s.
catchmentDescription: Headwaters rise in Silurian shales and grits of Denbigh Moors and Clwydian Hills, then flow across generally confined Triassic Sandstone aquifer (with artesian heads over large areas). 60% superficial deposits; mostly Boulder Clay. Rural catchment with mixed land use: grouse moors to lowland dairy farming, 20% forested (mainly in SW).
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Elwy at Pant yr Onen
stationDescription: Rated section formed just u/s of a small tributary; thought to be far enough to avoid backwater affects. During a flood the peak on the tributary has probably passed before the main peak occurs. In extreme floods (>2.9m) the stations begins to be bypassed u/s on the left bank over a distance of 75m. No gaugings available above QMED. High flows may have been substantially underestimated.
catchmentDescription: The Elwy catchment lies on impermeable Silurian Shales and Mudstones, with a small amount of Carboniferous Limestone and an extensive Boulder Clay cover. Land use is predominantly grassland, with some forestry in lower valley.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Wheeler at Bodfari
stationDescription: Crump weir 3.034m wide, between vertical side walls. Immediately d/s of rectangular channel beneath disused railway bridge. Station bypassed at high flows due to bend u/s of bridge (for example, during the 2000 flood event). Bridge u/s causes some surcharging and is obstructed at high flows; flow data above 3.95 m3/s are highly suspect and should not be used - low flow station only. Limited seasonal weed growth. Station refitted in June 1997; hut re-built and bridge installed, but weir untouched (no rating change). Natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: moderate permeability carboniferous limeston to N of river; impermeable Silurian to S. Extensive cover of patchy sand and gravel deposits and Boulder Clay. 70% grassland, with 20% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Clwyd at Ruthin Weir
stationDescription: Non-standard 14m wide concrete weir with central low flow notch (short trapezoidal flume) leading to fish passes. Levels recorded 14m u/s in float well against left bank. Large modular range (extending well beyond the 2000 flood). Leakage around the weir, not quantified. Data was reprocessed from 2001 -2006, using three separate ratings for the period. High flows increased, and the 2005 and 2006 low flows were reduced. No data since 28/7/2009 due to weir rebuild. Hydraulic continuity between g/w and streamflow along part of Afons Clywd and Hesbin.
catchmentDescription: Upper Clwyd rises in area of impermeable Palaeozoic rocks, with more permeable strata in lower catchment. Patchy Boulder Clay cover (60%). Most grassland, with 20% forest cover.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Elwy at Pont-y-Gwyddel
stationDescription: Twin arch bridge provides control at medium flow. 1m wide Crump weir blocks set in castellated manner within 10m wide archway to achieve low flow sensitivity. New rating from 01/08/97, data reprocessed. Some bypassing at levels > 2.0m. Rebuild in 2006 when instrument hut moved from lh to rh bank with bank works designed to contain more extreme events. Missing data July-September 2006 during rebuild. New cableway installed summer 2007 to allow high flow gauging. New rating developed June 2009 and applied back to June 2006. Low flows affected (>10%) by residual flow of 0.2 cumecs in Afon Aled (from reservoirs which drain 6% of catchment).
catchmentDescription: Impermeable Silurian strata with patchy Boulder Clay cover (50%). Land use: 80% grassland, mainly sheep pastures; patchy woodland and some upland heath.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Conwy at Cwmlanerch
stationDescription: A 50m wide river section requiring frequent recalibration due to shifting bed control. Record is very important in Conwy valley flood forecasting so much effort is spent to ensure rating is kept accurate. Bankfull around 4m and some bypassing and u/s overbank storage (significant on right bank) at very high flows - channel contains 400 - 450 cumecs before bypassing occurs. Low flows are gauged about 500m d/s. POR max. in December 2006 is under review, treat with caution. Gap in Feb 2013 data infilled from 66012 Lledr at Pont Gethin. Largely natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Very wet upland catchment - mainly mountainous, developed mostly impermeable Palaeozoic formations and volcanics. 65% grassland, most upland hill farming, with exposures of bare rock and heath. 15% forested, mainly in lower catchment.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: P: Runoff reduced by public water supply abstraction.

name: Lledr at Pont Gethin
stationDescription: A 17.2m pitching strip acts as a low flow control at this site (previously known as Gethin's Bridge). Some problems with low flow calibration due to subtle bed movements, but higher flow calibration observed to be stable at the present time.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable catchment draining eastern flanks of Snowdonia, Lower Palaeozoic formations and volcanics with some alluvial, boulder clay or glacial till cover in different parts of lower catchment. Steep, wet, upland catchment covered with 50% grassland and heath, 30% forest, 20% bare rock, used for hill farming.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Clwyd at Pont Dafydd
stationDescription: Combined EM (low flows) and rated section (medium to high flows), data sets merged to give GDF. Substantial bypassing above mean annual flood level. 1996-2000 flows reprocessed in 2012 due to new rating. 2007-2010 flows reprocessed in 2010 due to new rating. EM gauge faulty from late 2011; flows from 2012 based entirely on rated section, low flow end of which is not well-defined due to paucity of gaugings. Furthermore, well is prone to siltation and blockage of the inlet pipe, particularly affecting low flows. Nearby 66001 remains the primary gauging station.
catchmentDescription: Headwaters rise in Silurian shales and grits of Denbigh Moors and Clwydian Hills, then flow across generally confined Triassic Sandstone aquifer (with artesian heads over large areas). 60% superficial deposits; mostly Boulder Clay. Rural catchment with mixed land use: grouse moors to lowland dairy farming, 20% forested (mainly in SW).
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dee at Bala
stationDescription: From June 2004, hybrid site consisting of crump weir and cross-path US for flows >40 cumecs. Some missing data due to rebuild (summer 2004 and 2005). Previously a triangular profile weir, 1:1 u/s and 1:3.5 d/s, calibrated by wading and cableway gaugings (with some hydraulic model tests also). Thought to drown at about bankfull flows. This structure replaced an original broad-crested weir in 1968. Low flows controlled by Bala sluices about 750m u/s. Llyn Celyn also in catchment.
catchmentDescription: Thin soil cover over mostly impermeable Lower Ordovician rocks. The rapid response to rainfall is modified by the natural storage of Llyn Tegid. Mainly open moorland and sheep pastures with 15% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dee at Erbistock Rectory
stationDescription: Velocity-area station on left bank. Prior to the level recorder installation in 1937, the levels at Erbistock Weir (67812) were used. Slight overspilling at very high flows. Unstable stage/discharge relationship, due to both weed growth and natural river channel changes following floods, which led to replacement by Manley Hall (67015).
catchmentDescription: Geology: Majority of underlying bedrock is low permeability (88%) - Ordovician sandstone with felstite, tuff, agglomerate and dolerite and Silurian shales and grits. Small outcrops of Carboniferous limestone, grit, Coal Measures and Trias sandstone. Boulder clay in the extreme east and some valleys. Land use: Rural catchment of predominantly grassland (63%).
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Brenig at Llyn Brenig outflow
stationDescription: Sharp-edged weir built 1923, unchanged except extension of wing walls in 1975. Fully checked calibration to 30 cumecs. Natural flow until Aug 1975, when impounding started: monthly naturalised flows since. Llyn Brenig holds nearly four times annual average runoff. Before Aug 1975 flows above 15 cumecs estimated by rating curve extrapolation and hydrograph estimation because vertical drum level recorder truncated peaks. Owned and operated by Welsh Water Plc; dmfs sent to Measuring Authority, may be patchy and not up to date.
catchmentDescription: Open moorland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ceiriog at Brynkinalt Weir
stationDescription: Compound broad-crested weir modified from original velocity-area site in 1969. High flows rated using cableway. Regular clearance of gravel trap (approx 20m u/s) maintains stable rating. Flows >20 cumecs regularly verified by gaugings. Data prior to 1969 suspect. Station closure explains missing data from Nov 1983 to Nov 1989. Station refitted June - Sept 1997; no flows available, new rating thereafter. Near natural catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: 90% of catchment is impermeable, with more permeable rocks in lower catchment to E. River valleys are deeply incised. Soils are thin and peaty, supporting pasture for sheep grazing and 15% woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Alwen at Druid
stationDescription: Natural river section about 20m wide. Stable since last major flood in 1964. Some minor revisions of rating from time to time. Data post high flows of Feb 2011 reprocessed in 2013 following rating change, affecting low flows in Feb-Apr 2011 particularly. Flows greater than 60 cumecs are estimated. Bypassed during floods on lb. Reservoirs (Llyn Brenig and Alwen) control 15% of catchment.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Palaeozoic rocks, with some patchy Boulder Clay in valleys. Land use: 65% grassland, mostly upland pasture. Significant (20%) forest cover, mainly in N of catchment. Catchment area changed in 1976 to exclude Llyn Bran (0.8 sq.km).
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Alyn at Pont-y-Capel
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound crump weir (two crests). Divide wall was lowered in 1986 as debris regularly blocked the lower part, which may still occur. Current meter calibration took place before and after. Weir drowned above 1m. Structure not thought to have been bypassed. High flow gaugings have been taken (>40 cumecs). Flow data from 1 Jun 1965 reprocessed in 2013 following rating change, improving definition of low flows. Some weed growth on low flow crest. Small reservoirs in catchment, also affected by abstractions/returns.
catchmentDescription: Ill-defined catchment boundary to NE and SE. Mixed geology, generally of moderate permeability; 30% is Carboniferous Limestone. Extensive glacial deposits. Major loss of water from upper 70 sq.km. in limestone and mine drainage tunnels. Land use: 60% grassland pasture, 15% woodland with some urban development in low-lying areas to W.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Alyn at Rhydymwyn
stationDescription: Trapezoidal flume in concrete trapezoidal channel. Discharge frequently zero due to flow entering swallow holes in limestone u/s of site. Hence, rating only computes flows when stage exceeds 0.05m. Site is subject to rapid gravel accumulation. Reprocessed flows for 2000-2008 loaded in 2012. Pre-1968 data is very suspect, not held on the NRFA.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Silurian rocks in W half, Carboniferous Limestone escarpment to E. Swallow holes frequent between Maeshafn and Rhydymwyn, substantial losses of river flow through percolation to mine discharge tunnels. Land use: 65% grass pasture, 20% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Gelyn at Cynefail
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir. Combination of no cableway and poor access makes high flow gaugings difficult. Seasonal weed growth is managed but it is difficult to clean heavy moss growth on flanking weirs. Station closed 1981-83 and 1984-87; flows exist for intervening period, thought to be due to informal data collection. Despite station closure and re-commissioning, homogeneous hydrometric performance; compound crump weir identical to that originally installed in 1960s. Fully operational since 1988. Very responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Ordovician formations and volcanics. Boulder Clay in valley. Upland pasture, rural.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Hirnant at Plas Rhiwedog
stationDescription: Rated section on a small steep stream that enters the Dee d/s of Bala Sluices.  The control consists of a number of very large boulders. Gravel movements during large floods affect the rating. Bypassing of station at high flood flows.
catchmentDescription: Upland catchment, developed on impermeable Silurian geology.  The catchment is very heavily wooded (c.45%), with rough pasture.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Dee at Corwen
stationDescription: Level-only station with bridge control. No bypassing until approx 2m. Influenced by Bala Lake sluice gates (built 1953-1956) and Llyn Celyn reservoir (1965).
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dee at Manley Hall
stationDescription: Asymmetrical compound Crump profile weir, checked by current metering. Structure comprises two crump weirs (low/high flow weirs 15/20m wide respectively). Drowns at flows above 200 cumecs but not bypassed (due to rock outcrop on left bank and revetments on right bank); site is used for flood warnings. Data prior to Feb 1970 is poorer quality - based on d/s Erbistock Rectory (67002, area: 1028 sq.km.) record, which this site replaced in order to provide more accurate measurement of low-medium flows on the river Dee. Low flows maintained by releases from major river regulating reservoirs: Celyn and Brenig. Flood attenuation on the lower Dee flood plain is notable. Refurbished in 1997.
catchmentDescription: Geology: predominantly impermeable Palaeozoic rocks and volcanics. Land use: 60% grassland (upland pasture to lowland livestock farming). 15% forestry, significant upland heath.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Tryweryn at Llyn Celyn outflow
stationDescription: Compound broad-crested weir measuring controlled outflow and overspill from Llyn Celyn regulating reservoir.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable catchment, predominantly Ordovician with Upper Cambrian shales, slates and mudstones in the SW. Rural, upland catchment supporting sheep grazing.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dee at New Inn
stationDescription: Original control based on a rough stone paved ford with stepping stones u/s to provide sensitivity at low flows. Formalised (between 04/98 and 04/99 - data infilled using 67/10) into a shallow V concrete ford with square blocks cast on the u/s base to mimic the original configuration. Located d/s of confluence with Afon Lliw, and on a bend. Gravel collects at the confluence and is removed annually. Calibration by current meter. Out-of-bank flows occur almost annually and station is significantly by-passed above around 2.2m. Flow data from 28 Apr 2009 reprocessed in 2013 following rating change, with minimal impact. Data are used operationally for flood alleviation, flood warning and a daily naturalization indicator for upper Dee flows. Natural flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Rapidly responding upland catchment. Geology: mainly impermeable Ordovician rocks, with minor superficial deposits. Land use is mostly rough upland pasture and 25% forest.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Dee at Chester Weir
stationDescription: Velocity-area station u/s of ancient weir liable to tide over-spilling. 30% of time unsuitable hydraulically for continuous flow record. Superseded by 67033 (Chester Suspension Bridge) and level only from March 1998. Used to derive the high flows of the synthetic record at Eccleston Ferry (67026). Hifs not archived.
catchmentDescription: Geology: upper catchment impermeable Lower Palaeozoic rocks with intermittent Drift cover. Below Llangollen, R. Dee crosses Carboniferous L'st escarpment and outcrops of Cefn-y-Fewd S'st, Coal Measures and Sherwood S'st, providing many artesian areas in lower catchment.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Clywedog at Bowling Bank
stationDescription: Simple Crump profile weir 6m wide which drowns at 8 cumecs. Bypassed by flood flows of >10 year return period. A road bridge 150 m d/s of gauging station can act as a constriction, particularly during high flows. The channel frequently blocks with debris from fallen trees/branches (the river banks are covered with thick vegetation and tree growth). Until 7/10/92, 50% of low flow was treated effluent from Wrexham; after this date the discharge was piped directly to the R. Dee. Mine drainage and abstraction in upper catchment. Jun-Aug 2013 levels/flows affected by commissioning of new water treatment works in catchment.
catchmentDescription: Mainly Carboniferous strata, mostly of moderate permeability, with > 80% superficial deposits with Boulder Clay and sands and gravels. Land use: mixed farming (pasture predominates), substantially urbanised (>15% built-up). Much mining in the catchment.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dee at Ironbridge
stationDescription: Originally a 45 metre rated section. Converted to cross-path US during 1994. Good throughout the range up to approximately 6.8m (200 cumecs). Above 6.8m goes out of bank on right bank and recirculates; in extreme events site can be bypassed on both banks (on left bank via serpentine). Managed weed growth. Data missing during Oct 2000 event, which was the highest on record at Chester (67033). Site is tidally influenced and abstractions mean that flows are about 6 cumecs higher than d/s 67033.
catchmentDescription: Geology: upper catchment impermeable Lower Palaeozoic rocks with intermittent Drift cover. Below Llangollen, R. Dee crosses Carboniferous L'st escarpment and outcrops of Cefn-y-Fewd S'st, Coal Measures and Sherwood S'st, providing many artesian areas in lower catchment. Land use: principally grassland (upland pasture to lowland livestock farming). 15% forestry, significant upland heath.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Ceidiog at Llandrillo
stationDescription: Velocity-area station utilising a series of gravel traps as a control for low and medium flows only (flows exceeding 1 cumec based on rating extrapolation and should be treated with extreme caution). Station converted to a level-only site in 2001.
catchmentDescription: Geology: impermeable Ordovician mudstones and shales with volcanics which provide rapid runoff rates and low baseflows. Mostly rough grazing, with 15% woodland.
operator: Natural Resources Wales
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Trystion at Pen-y-felin Fawr
stationDescription: Site measures flows from small Cynwyd reservoir just upstream.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Dee at Chester Suspension Bridge
stationDescription: Ultrasonic gauge, 6 path (paths 5 and 6 used for high flows) installed in 1994. River channel approx. 40m wide. High flow performance under review. Superseded 67020 (non-standard weir, tidally affected) approx. 300m d/s; some data (1974-86) also available for nearby station 67026. Abstractions mean that flows are about 6 cumecs lower than u/s 67027.
catchmentDescription: Geology: upper catchment impermeable Lower Palaeozoic rocks with intermittent superficial deposits. Below Llangollen, river crosses Carboniferous Limestone escarpment and outcrops of S'sts; many artesian areas in the lower catchment. Upper catchment is rough grazing, with some patches of forest; Lower catchment is mixed farming.
operator: Natural Resources Wales

name: Weaver at Ashbrook
stationDescription: Initially a river section (from 1937). Early gaugings lost; rating accuracy unknown. Mobile control. Data before 1972, particularly low flows, unreliable. Relocated 400m d/s with an informal timber Flat V control and cableway in 08/1978. Located just u/s of a bend in the river. Weir drowns at relatively low flows. Bypassing on right bank above 3.5m; inundates adjacent fields, but not regularly. Prone to weed and algal growth, and sedimentation has required several rating changes. Some bank re-profiling in the 1990s (slippage occurred in 1993) also affected the rating. Revised data from 15/08/1979 to 12/2008 loaded in 03/2010: new values are lower, especially at high flows - some reduced by over 25%; annual runoff typically reduced by 15%; max GDF unchanged, as it was in 1946. High flows can be protracted, with recession delayed.
catchmentDescription: A generally flat catchment below the headwaters. Developed on mixed geology, comprising Keuper Marl mostly overlain with post-glacial deposits. Lower Lias shales in the south with Coal Measures, Permian Sandstones and Bunter Pebble Beds in the east; mostly low permeability with extensive Drift cover. Land use is largely agricultural but catchment includes Nantwich and parts of Crewe.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Gowy at Picton
stationDescription: Natural section V/A station calibrated by gaugings. Severe weed growth problems, variable in intensity from year to year. Station closed in 1979.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Dane at Rudheath
stationDescription: Originally a VA section; charts from 05/1949. Low flows poor before 06/1957 (changing bed levels and cross section due to large amounts of silt). Gauged by wading or from u/s bridge. Mobile control gave unreliable results - flow data should be treated with caution prior to 02/1979, when an informal flat V weir (width 14m) was completed a few metres downstream of original site. (No data during construction period 11/1978 to 02/1979.) Cableway installed 01/1982; since removed. Drowns at approx 1 m. High flows regularly inundate fields over left bank (3.6m), and variable backwater from Weaver may affect the flood rating. Summer 1976 flows might be overestimated. 10/2010: re-rated data for 09/2000 to 12/2009 loaded onto NRFA; highest DMFs reduced by around 10%; all HIFs replaced. Responsive regime. Headwater transfers particularly to Rudyard and Bosley reservoirs for canal usage. Many artificial influences within the catchment.
catchmentDescription: Headwaters in the southern Pennines but, for most part, river meanders over Cheshire plain with varying depths of post glacial drift overlying Triassic s'st and marl (mostly low permeability). Mixed land use with appreciable urban development in the lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Wistaston Brook at Marshfield Bridge
stationDescription: Initially a 6m wide section on a bend with chart records from 1955, but early data in particular are poor. Unstable control obliged a move u/s in Sept 1972; low flow control installed 1978 and modified to an informal Flat V piled weir in May 1980. Silt accumulates behind weir in times of low flow. Flows above 0.7m less reliably measured. Bankfull at 2.3m. Responsive regime with artificial influences evident during periods of low flow.
catchmentDescription: Below headwaters geology is generally of low permeability (Mercia mudstone with extensive Drift cover). Land is primarily agricultural but central and southern parts of Crewe dominate lowest portion of the catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Weaver at Audlem
stationDescription: Some level measurement at site since 1936 but near-continuous records from 1951 when rectangular thin-plate weir was installed (but accuracy limited due to d/s level measurement). C/m calibration for medium flows. New recorder house in 1969 (u/s level measurement) and modern rating assumed to apply from then. All flows are contained but rating insensitive at very high flows. The weir collects debris and drowning is possible. Only minor gw abstractions and returns. Flows from 1973 onwards reprocessed in 2002, with substantial reduction in high flows. Revised data from 1/1977 to 12/2008 loaded in 03/2010: new values are lower, especially at high flows; max GDF reduced from 26.1 m3s-1 (25/6/07) to 23.3 m3s-1 (6/11/00); annual runoff typically 7% lower. Pre-1973 data are considered to be overestimates.
catchmentDescription: A mostly flat catchment in the Cheshire Plain developed on post glacial deposits over marl, clay and sand; largely impermeable. Mainly mixed farmland with only a few villages.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Dane at Hulme Walfield
stationDescription: Original natural river section augmented Jun 1978 with an informal flat V control. Station closed 1984.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Wincham Brook at Lostock Gralam
stationDescription: Open channel section from Oct 1960 in straight reach. Informal steel pile control commissioned in early 1982. Siltation problems have led to control being submerged, necessitating multiple ratings. Silt became a more serious problem after 11/2006 as the channel naturally realigned following stabilisation works on the left bank; new rating applied in 2010, which caused changes in flow values from April 1986 onwards: most flows reduced, except for very low flows, which were increased. Improved quality from 15/06/2010 when external pressure transducer was installed. Period of missing data in 2013 due to station improvement works. Gauging by wading or cableway. No bypassing reported. Vandal-prone. Bankfull 2.0m.
catchmentDescription: Linear, low relief catchment with mudstones and saliferous beds of the Mercia Mudstone blanketed by Boulder Clay and glacial sands and gravel. Rural, except for SW Knutsford.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Fender at Ford Lane
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Arley Brook at Gore Farm
stationDescription: Critical depth flume installed as a component of an experimental catchment scheme. Design capacity 7 cumec in modular range, rated by gaugings above this. Station closed 1982.
catchmentDescription: Low relief catchment between Northwich and Warrington on Boulder Clay covered Keuper Marl. N boundary may be partially formed by the M56. Entirely rural but with M6 and M56 runoff.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire
farText: N: Natural to within 10% at the 95 percentile flow.

name: Gowy at Huxley
stationDescription: Shallow V sheet pile control installed in May 1979; some levels available from EA prior to this but not processed to flow. Some low flow features caused by ponding and release u/s. However, flows above 1.5 cumecs should be treated with caution until high flow rating established. Late 2010 and 2011 flows removed from NRFA due to serious overestimation caused by vegetation d/s of the weir causing it to drown out. Responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: River shares most of its valley with the Shropshire Union Canal. Catchment mostly in Cheshire plain; post glacial Drift overlying Triassic sandstone and marl. Rural in character.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Dane at Congleton Park
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Gowy at Bridge Trafford
stationDescription: Flat V Crump profile weir (1:5) with flanking broad-crested weirs and cableway, installed 08/1979. Replaced Picton (68002), 1km d/s. Significant u/s and d/s seasonal weed growth problems (caused backwater effects in e.g. 2008); siltation can also cause backwater problems (channel is intermittently dredged); flows rejected by NRFA 11/07/2009 to 10/10/2009. Midsummer flows estimated by gauging from u/s road bridge. Revised data from start of record (08/1979) to 10/1981 loaded on 11/08/2009 due to new rating that has reduced the flows: previous max GDF on 06/08/1981 reduced from 36.6 to 19.4 m3s-1; 1980 runoff reduced by 17%. Some circulation effects d/s of structure near right bank. Station performs well up to bankfull, but inundates adjacent fields at high flows. Complex range of issues since 01/2012: reduced dredging and conveyance of sediment affecting rating curve and stage discharge techniques, whilst broken tidal flaps have introduced tidal fluctuations in stage record. As a result, flow derivation suspended in 2012, to explore alternative flow measurement methods, but since re-instated. Responsive regime. Relative primacy of the 1981 and 2000 floods under review. Sluice gates control outflow to Manchester Ship Canal.
catchmentDescription: A largely rural catchment in the Cheshire Plain; low relief, glacial drift over Triassic sandstones and marl.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Arrowe Brook at Acton Lane
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Dane at Hug Bridge
stationDescription: Flat V weir (1:20 cross-slopes) constructed in November 1991, continuous records from August 1992. Gravel accretion above the weir and limited-capacity d/s bridge can affect low/high flows. Above bankfull at 0.95m, flow expands out onto a wide flood plain, bypassing the cableway on both banks. Not rated above bankfull. Very responsive regime.
catchmentDescription: A predominantly rural catchment with steep relief, rising to a maximum altitude of 547m. Drains the western flank of the Peak District. Mixed geology including Millstone Grit, Yoredale series, coal measures and sandstone. The area is partly covered with glacial clays, sands and gravels.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Mersey at Irlam Weir
stationDescription: Original 1934 calibration of this rather insensitive broad-crested weir was by formula but, in 1938, a model-based rating led to significant increases in estimates of high flows. C/m gaugings 1975-85 suggest that flows above 10 cumecs should be higher still. Longdendale reservoirs control 10% of the catchment. (1921 data from published source.)
catchmentDescription: Steep tributary streams rise mainly on western slopes of Pennines (Millstone Grit), extensive Drift cover below headwaters. Land use transition from open moorland to the heavily urbanised lower catchment (includes much of south Manchester).
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Irwell at Adelphi Weir
stationDescription: 40m wide broad-crested weir subject to siltation, weed growth, and drowning at high flow. All except extreme events are contained. Some records from 1935; routine data capture began in 1949. Rating established by model test and gauging u/s at the nearby Manchester Racecourse gauge (69025, level-only since 2/86). Station re-rated from 1/2/75 but maintenance problems persist. Major technical problems in 2000: gaps in Mar, Apr, Jun, Sep & Oct estimated by CEH using 69023. No gaugings since 5/2005 due to access problems. 2009: flows leaking through right bank retaining wall. Very responsive regime. Many abstractions and storage reservoirs. Note that a single combined annual maxima series for 69002 and 69025 is held on HiFlows-UK under 69025.
catchmentDescription: Most of the catchment comprises post glacial drift over heavily faulted Carboniferous grit, shale and sandstones. Deeply incised headwaters valleys drain the Pennine moors; lower catchment includes the urban/industrial areas of Bolton, Bury and Rochdale.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Irk at Scotland Weir
stationDescription: An old, non-standard, broad-crested weir, diagonal to flow on a bend in a heavily polluted river. No bypassing and very likely to remain modular. Ratings by model (1936) and c/m gauging at Redbank 1km u/s. Siltation, debris and weed growth are recurrent problems and throw particular doubt on low-flow records before 1976, although none are good. Weir damaged by a flood in Dec 1983, subsequent flows under review. Station closed May 2001. Replaced by Collyhurst (69/43) in 1999. Responsive flow regime. Wide range of artificial influences including many industrial abstractions and effluent discharges.
catchmentDescription: Low to moderate relief catchment; moorland headwaters but extensively urbanised (includes north Manchester). Solid geology: Coal Measures and Permo-Triassic sandstones, fully overlain by post-glacial sands and gravel and subordinate boulder clay.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Etherow at Bottoms Reservoir
stationDescription: Rectangular thin-plate weir. Monitors outflows from Bottoms Res.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Millstone Grit catchment, peat covered moorland in headwaters, steeper slopes drift free.
operator: North West Water
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Glaze Brook at Little Woolden Hall
stationDescription: Ultrasonic gauge installed in 1995 which replaced a Velocity-area station. VA record unreliable as very badly affected by weed growth throughout the year and suffered backwater effects from the Ship Canal level. Used for flood warning. Bank full is at 4.43m.
catchmentDescription: Low relief catchment with headwaters in South Wigan with 3 major "flashes" (instream lakes). Solid geology: Coal Measures in the headwaters, otherwise Sherwood Sst with some Marl. Extensively draped with Boulder Clay, some peat and glacial gravels south of Leigh. Many urban centres, much effluent, motorway drainage and potential mine drainage pollution.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Bollin at Dunham Massey
stationDescription: V/A station with cableway a few km u/s of confluence with the Manchester Ship Canal. Level records from 1937. Flows from 1954 but of poor quality. Rating only approximate owing to very unstable bed and weed growth. In 8/71, the Bridgewater Canal (crosses just u/s) burst its banks and disturbed the river bed, affecting the record for at least 18 months. No bypassing reported. Closed as a flow station on 10/01/2002. Replaced by station 750 m upstream at Bollington Mill (69045), for which NRFA hold data from 19/11/2009 - the earlier record from Bollington Mill is not held as it does not include flow through the mill race and fish pass, which can exceed 25% of the total flow. Very responsive regime. Reservoirs and many industrial abstractions and discharges affect low flows particularly.
catchmentDescription: Mixed geology but with extensive Drift cover (some Millstone Grit exposed in headwaters). Land use is very mixed - a predominantly rural catchment but with Macclesfield below the headwaters and other urban centres.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Mersey at Ashton Weir
stationDescription: A compound broad-crested weir, 24 m wide overall, with central lower section 12.2 m wide and 0.49 m lower than flanking crests (no divide piers), cableway - not operational at present, and (since 1/2009) a cross path ultrasonic flow meter for high flow measurement. Replaced 69001 (in 1958) but, despite theoretical superiority at low flows, still doubts about rating curve (varying downstream conveyance and differential drowning of the weir crests may be factors). 2009: previous high flows (over 90 m3s-1) considered to be greatly overestimated; new ultrasonic data to be used to revise the previous record. No bypassing. Responsive regime despite substantial reservoir development in the headwaters (Longdendale reservoirs control 10% of the catchment).
catchmentDescription: Steep tributary streams rise mainly on western slopes of Pennines (Millstone Grit), extensive Drift cover below headwaters. Land use transition from open moorland to the heavily urbanised lower catchment (includes much of south Manchester).
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Dean at Stanneylands
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir, crest widths 3.05m and 6.1m (total) at 0.46m. Crest tapping not used. Low to medium flow range calibrated by c/m. High flows also gauged since a footbridge 200m u/s of the structure was built in 1999. Channel accretes at high flow requiring regular clearance. Significant flow disturbance from reservoir, abstractions and returns.
catchmentDescription: Steep moorland headwaters drain Millstone Grit from W Pennines and contain Lamaload Res. Lower catchment developed on Boulder Clay covered Triassic sandstones and contains Bollington.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Micker Brook at Cheadle
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir, low crest 1.52m, two flanking crests of 4.57m at 0.46m. Low divide piers, wing walls 2.2m. Sited just u/s of the Mersey confluence but does not drown out owing to d/s mill weir. May bypass on lb above 2m. Upper 12% of the catchment reservoired; significant modification to flows by reservoir operations, PWS and WRW.
catchmentDescription: Moderate relief catchment draining western flanks of Pennines; headwaters Millstone Grit, lower catchment has Boulder Clay, terrace gravels over Triassic s'st. Upper two thirds predeominantly rural, the rest suburban.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Bollin at Wilmslow
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir, crest lengths 4.1m and 4.3m (total); divide piers 1.0m (probable drowning stage), wing walls 2.0 m. Crest tappings no longer used. D/s recorder installed 2005: observations indicate drowning above 0.93 m stage. Calibration now by current metering; d/s for low flows, u/s off road bridge for high. Siltation can be a problem. No bypassing reported. 2009: rating to be investigated. Responsive regime. Substantial flow modifications because of headwater reservoirs and major STW u/s of station.
catchmentDescription: Moderate relief catchment with steep, reservoired headwaters. Upper catchment drains Millstone Grit, generally Drift free. Otherwise Boulder Clay and glacial sands and gravel over Permo-Triassic sandstone. Contains Macclesfield.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Sinderland Brook at Partington
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir, crest lengths 2.13m and 5.48m (total). Contained to wing wall height (2.0m). Weir much wider than u/s channel; big siltation problem, crest tapping usually blocked. Responsive regime. Storm waters from Wythenshaw, Sale and the M56 are directed to the Mersey. Revised data from start of record (1/1976) to 12/2008 loaded in 03/2010: new data are lower at high flows and slightly higher at low flows; previous max GDF on 6/8/81 reduced from 12.2 to 8.7 m3s-1.
catchmentDescription: Very flat catchment, heavily urbanised although the bottom end is rural. Solid geology is Keuper Marl (70%) and sandstone (30%); south western portion Boulder Clay covered. Soils fine red loams or clays.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Etherow at Compstall
stationDescription: Crump profile weir 18m wide, wing walls 2.8m high, located 0.5 km u/s of Goyt confluence. Further contained by flood banks. Crest tapping readings were used to establish a non-modular rating; tapping no longer used. High flow gaugings not possible. Half the catchment drains through Longdendale reservoirs, with significant effect upon flows.
catchmentDescription: Predominantly Millstone Grit catchment, peat covered moorland in headwaters, steeper slopes drift free; Boulder Clay in lower catchment. Mixed urban and farmland lower down.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Goyt at Marple Bridge
stationDescription: Compound Crump profile weir, crest lengths 7m and 11m (total). Wing walls 2.9m, divide piers 2.1m. Crest tapping unreliable, subject to siltation, data not used. 2009: rating is good in low flow crest (below 0.5 m) but is consistently 20% less than gaugings above this; downstream recorder, installed 8/2006, will enable a non-modular rating to be produced. Data for 1969 & 1970 are of lower quality. The weir is fitted with bypass sluices. Several reservoirs in catchment - Kinder, Combs, Todd Brook, Errwood and Fernilee - control approximately 32% of catchment in total. Moderate disturbance to flow regime.
catchmentDescription: Catchment mostly Millstone Grit and Coal Measures. Highest moorland peat covered, steeper slopes drift free. Boulder Clay cover lower down. Mixed farmland, small towns and industry in main valley.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Worsley Brook at Eccles
stationDescription: Critical depth trapezoidal flume in artificial channel; throat width 0.6m, side slopes 1:2. Structure full at 2.43m, flood banks at 3.13m. Flows fully contained. Twin box culvert 110m d/s with grids may block in flood, drowning flume. Data quality poor. Station not operational between December 1985 and June 1993 and closed in April 2001.
catchmentDescription: Generally low relief catchment to the NW of Manchester. Solid geology is 80% Coal Measures with Permo-Triassic S'sts to the South, extensively covered by Boulder Clay. Urban areas cover approximately 60%, otherwise poor pasture and heathland and small area of woodland. Dense motorway network includes the M61, M62, M63, M602.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Medlock at London Road
stationDescription: A non-standard weir 8.99m wide with broad crest, in a rectangular, concrete channel. The weir was designed as an entrance sill to the culvert d/s. Theoretical formula in use to Nov 1976 when superseded by c/m based rating. Two periods of construction of the Mancunian Way (motorway) have significantly altered the river channel downstream of the gauging station. During the second phase, the channel was narrowed and wooden crest blocks were attached to the weir to create a low flow notch.  They proved unsuccessful and were removed on October 1992. Access is difficult and gauging hazardous. Greatly affected by effluent discharges with consequent heavy pollution; also problems with debris on weir. Very poor site.
catchmentDescription: The catchment is heavily urbanised. Any natural runoff is generated on soils derived from Boulder Clay deposits overlying, predominantly, Coal Measures.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Irwell at Irwell Vale
stationDescription: VA station with non-standard flat V installed to replace gauge at Stubbins (69040). Reliable gaugings by wading at low flows only; bridge gaugings u/s are unreliable due to the nature of the bridge and inflows from River Ogden. Full record rerated in 9/09, reducing the higher flows, typically by 10% at 20 m3s-1; max DMF, on 30/10/2000, reduced from 58.5 m3s-1 to 48.2 m3s-1. Reservoirs in headwaters have significant effect on low flows.
catchmentDescription: Moderate relief catchment draining the Forest of Rossendale. Solid geology Millstone Grit and Coal Measures; peat on highest moors, intermittent glacial drift otherwise. Urbanised main valley.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Roch at Blackford Bridge
stationDescription: Broad-crested mill-type curved weir with damaged crest; affected by debris. Siltation affected inlet pipe pre-1984. Original theoretical rating unsafe; a c/m rating was applied back to 1949 notwithstanding doubts about state of weir in earlier years. Cableway installed 2001 to improve high range. A dozen small water-supply reservoirs in headwaters control a total of 16% of the catchment area.
catchmentDescription: Geology mostly Coal Measures within Millstone Grit to the Northeast and Northwest. Peat overlays geology in moorland hilltops; mixed glacial drift in the lower catchment. Catchment is highly urbanised and contains the town of Rochdale.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Croal at Farnworth Weir
stationDescription: A non-standard broad-crested mill weir 45m wide in narrower river; insensitive, especially at low flows. Tight meander immediately u/s; flows above 70 cumecs will bypass station on the inside of the loop. Some records from 1948, but low and medium flows before 1976 particularly are of doubtful quality, although none are good. Confluence with River Irwell, 600 m downstream, may cause backwater and drowning, though no observations to confirm. A cableway was installed in June 2000. Several reservoirs in headwaters, particularly on the Bradshaw Brook tributary, controlling 27% of the catchment. Many effluent discharges lower down.
catchmentDescription: Geology of Millstone Grit overlain with peat moorland in headwaters, with Boulder Clay and coal measures in the urban lower parts. A highly urbanised and industrialised catchment which includes Bolton.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Irwell at Manchester Racecourse
stationDescription: Open channel. Flow measuring station terminated 11/2/86 due to faulty well. Continued as level only site for flood warning purposes, forming part of the Regional Communication System (RCS). The Environment Agency hold data from 1976. For NRFA data for the Irwell in this vicinity, refer to the nearby d/s station 69002 (Adelphi Weir). Note that a single combined annual maxima series for 69025 and 69002 is held on HiFlows-UK under 69025.
catchmentDescription: Large Catchment with lower half of the catchment dominated by the urban areas of Rochdale and Bolton. Underlying Geology mainly Coal Measures with Millstone Grit in the Northeast. Limited coverage of sandstones and mudstones upstream of the gauging station. Peat overlies the upland, moorland, areas with extensive drift deposits of Boulder Clay and sands and gravels within the low lying areas.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Tame at Portwood
stationDescription: Over 100 year old curved mill type weir, 21m wide, 5m high, just d/s of 90deg. bend and 2 km upstream of Goyt confluence. Meandering reach but bypassing on rb floodplain prevented by stop banks. An old mill cut was closed in 1967 so that the entire flow passes over the weir. Records from 1943; a model-based calibration was used before being superseded by one based on current metering in 1970. Gaugings carried out from footbridge downstream of weir. Gravel shoaling occurs at the base of the weir, extending 20m downstream. Station is d/s of Broomstair Bridge (69041). Low flows dominated by effluent returns. Many impoundments in the upper reaches control 25% of the catchment in total. Revised data from start of record (1/1970) to 10/73 loaded in 2009 due to new rating that has greatly reduced the high flows: previous max GDF on 21/2/70 reduced from 137.9 to 34.9 m3s-1. Full rerated record loaded in Oct 2011: main change is reduction in low flows of up to about 20% over 2001-09.
catchmentDescription: For the most part a narrow, steep sided valley network on Millstone Grit with peat moorland in upper reaches (includes Saddleworth Moor); underlying geology of Coal Measures and Permian sandstones and mudstones overlain by Boulder Clay lower down. Sheep grazing in the headwaters; lower catchment heavily urbanised (east Manchester)
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Sankey Brook at Causey Bridges
stationDescription: VA gauge, records from 1953. Backwater problems from sluice operation ceased with closure of adjacent canal in 1976. Frequent calibration changes followed continual d/s channel works in 1976/7 (also caused by floods in 1981). New trench-piled bed control and stone pitched lb built 07/1983; acts as a weir up to 0.35m. Bankfull 2.66m. Full re-rating for the structure 2001. At higher flows, gaugings are taken from a bridge 30m upstream of the station. Susceptible to siltation and weed growth. Industrial abstraction and effluent. Revised data from start of record (1/1977) to 9/2006 loaded in 10/2009: flows from 17/6/77 to 30/8/77 increased by approx 2.5 times; previous max GDF on 28/12/78 reduced from 38.5 to 28.9 m3s-1; other differences generally minor.
catchmentDescription: Geology: Coal Measures within the North overlain by sand and peat; Sherwood Sandstone in the South, overlain by Boulder Clay. Mixed farmland predominates but contains urban areas (~35%) including St Helens in the centre. Possible that there was a small increase in catchment area in late 1980s or very early 1990s with diversion of a ditch crossing Sankey Valley Park to join Sankey Brook upstream of the gauging station.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Alt at Kirkby
stationDescription: Originally a velocity-area station but silt deposition prevented sensible calibration until a Flat V bed control was built in 1977. The weir easily drowns and all flood flows are measured under drowned conditions. Gaugings taken from upstream footbridge. Siltation causes inlet pipe blockage and lag between stilling well and river level. Vandal prone. Industrial abstractions and discharges. Large sewage treatment works upstream at Fazakerley, which can cause fluctuations in daily mean flows.
catchmentDescription: Very flat catchment; boundary on SW side difficult to define. Geology mostly of blown sand deposits with Boulder Clay over Sherwood Sandstone. Highly urbanised (approx 50%), containing parts of north Liverpool and Kirkby.
operator: Environment Agency - Cumbria and Lancashire

name: Alt at Sefton
stationDescription: V/A station 11.5km u/s of tidal gates at Hightown and unaffected by their operation. Very vandal prone. Low to medium flows dominated by Liverpool effluent (augmentation).
catchmentDescription: Low relief catchment with SW boundary difficult to define. Blown sands and Boulder Clay over Sherwood Sandstone. Heavily urbanised (~60%).
operator: Environment Agency - Cumbria and Lancashire

name: Irwell at Bury Bridge
stationDescription: VA station with an old broad-crested weir, oblique to the river, as its (insensitive) control. A rating, based on d/s gaugings, applicable to about 100 cumecs was developed in 1979. However, subsequent gaugings were not consistent; calibration under review. 1995 peak under review. Runoff influenced by storage reservoirs and abstractions.  Replaced by Bury Ground (69044) in 1996.
catchmentDescription: Catchment mostly u/s of the urban and industrial areas which dominate at d/s station at Adelphi Weir (69002). Geology mainly Boulder Clay over Lower Coal Measures and Millstone Grit.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Mersey at Westy
stationDescription: Ultrasonic, multipath, cross path station replacing an earlier unsatisfactory site at Howley (1.5km d/s). Flow comprises Ship Canal overflow only; flows need to be combined with Latchford (69038) for the total Mersey Flow. Limited reliability, significant gaps in flow record. Out of use 27/02/2007 to 25/06/2008 whilst being upgraded. Equipment failure 19/11/2008 to 18/03/2009, and 20/04/2010 to 26/08/2012. Anomalous and missing data in 2013 due to repair works on Manchester Ship Canal. Experiences abstraction, augmentation and regulation. Runoff probably substantially modified, at least temporally.
catchmentDescription: Large catchment containing much of Manchester. The main tributaries arise on peat moorlands to W and N of Manchester. High ground mostly Millstone Grit or Coal Measures. The centre and south of the catchment is underlain by Sherwood Sandstones and Mercia mudstones blanketed by Boulder Clay, terrace gravels and lowland peat. Diverse catchment, extensively urbanised.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Medlock at New Viaduct Street
stationDescription: Broad-crested weir. Sporadic flow record (of limited quality). Station closed due to continuing vandalism. Superseded by 69020 (d/s).
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire

name: Tame at Broomstairs
stationDescription: Ultrasonic multi-path gauge replaced a non-standard short-crested mill weir in February 1995, but performance of the US remains under review in relation to the highest flows. The weir, a control for medium to high flows, was capped with timber that had bowed allowing water to flow beneath it (dev. from rating up to 70%) thus early record poor. Gaugings made from bridge immediately u/s. Bridge arch shape likely to affect high flows as it extends to river level. The following erroneous data deleted on 13/08/2009: July & August 2006. This station is upstream of Portwood (69027). Reservoired headwater affects low flows.
catchmentDescription: Geology of Millstone Grit and Coal Measures with peat cover on high moors. Steeper valley sides drift free, otherwise mixed glacial drift cover. Heavily urbanised lower catchment.
operator: Environment Agency - Greater Manchester Merseyside and Cheshire
farText: S: Reservoir(s) in catchment affect runoff.

name: Ding Brook at Naden Reservoir
